source.content:
Talia Benson: trying to line up PR review coverage + landing sequence for the KV compaction series this week. want to keep the chain tight so we’re not rebasing for days.

Proposed order:
1) metrics/counters PR (PR-19342)
2) allocator + compaction primitives (PR-19357)
3) scheduler budgeting hook (PR-19371)
4) eviction policy (PR-19388)

Can folks reply w/ review owners + when you can look? Also call out any “must land together” constraints.

Omar El-Sayed: +1 on that order. metrics first so Allison/Sean can get dashboards/alerts staged before we flip anything.

Samir Qureshi: allocator PR depends on a couple metric structs (names + tags) but shouldn’t hard-depend on dashboards. can gate allocator changes behind `kv_compaction_enable=false` default.

Allison Grant: I can take PR-19342 review today. please ping me once it’s rebased, I’ll focus on naming + label cardinality. also want to ensure we export fragmentation ratio in a way that matches perf standards.

Sean Gallagher: I’ll review PR-19342 too (SRE lens). mainly checking it won’t blow up our ingest + that counters are safe under high QPS. :eyes:

Sofia Ivanova: I can review PR-19371 (scheduler integration) but I’d like Noah on it as well for “no decode stalls” correctness.

Noah Patel: yep, I’ll review PR-19357 + PR-19371. for PR-19357 I’m mostly looking at invariants around relocation + cancellation boundaries.

Sahana Iyer: FYI CI perf jobs are already tight. if PR-19342 adds more tracing in hot paths we’ll see noise. keep counters cheap + avoid per-token logs.

Omar El-Sayed: on the sequencing: PR-19357 introduces relocation primitives + a no-op compaction loop (behind flag). PR-19371 is what enforces budget at safe points (prefill/decode boundary). we should avoid landing PR-19357 and then waiting multiple days for PR-19371.

Talia Benson: 👍 makes sense. target: land 19342 today, 19357 + 19371 back-to-back tomorrow (or same day) if reviews clear.

GitHub Bot: PR opened: https://github.com/redwood/redwood/pull/19342 "add kv fragmentation counters"

Allison Grant: looking now. immediate nit: please use `kv_cache_fragmentation_ratio` not `kv_frag_ratio`. also include `model`, `tier`, `pool` tags but NOT `request_id`/`session_id`.

Sean Gallagher: +1 on tags. also for eviction counters later: we need “reason” as an enum, not raw strings.

Samir Qureshi: ack. I’ll update metric names. For fragmentation ratio, I’m exporting both `free_bytes` and `largest_contiguous_free_bytes` so ratio is derivable + we can sanity-check.

Omar El-Sayed: for PR-19388 (eviction) we should keep it separate + last. it’s where product will have opinions (tier-aware defaults). also bigger blast radius.

Benji Okafor: on feature flags/config plumbing: make sure each PR compiles with defaults off. rollout system expects `kv.compaction.enabled` + `kv.compaction.budget_ms` to exist even if set to 0. ping me when you’re ready to wire flags.

Sofia Ivanova: scheduler PR note: I want compaction steps only at scheduler “tick” boundaries, not inside the inner decode loop. If you need to move pages during decode, we need a hard budget ceiling + preemption.

Samir Qureshi: agreed. current plan: compaction worker runs between batches; it gets `budget_ms` from scheduler once per tick; worker bails if batch is ready.

Noah Patel: please add a comment re: cancellation: if request is cancelled mid-relocation we must ensure the source pages aren’t freed until destination mapping is committed (or vice versa). also include a stress test case.

Grace Kim: quick note: if you need a fast path memcpy/pack kernel, loop me in early. but ideally first version uses cuda memcpy and we optimize only if p99 moves.

Hiro Tanaka: same. don’t block on kernels unless perf data forces it.

Talia Benson: ok review assignments (tentative):
- PR-19342: Allison + Sean (today)
- PR-19357: Noah + Omar (tomorrow AM), Samir to incorporate feedback quickly
- PR-19371: Sofia + Noah (tomorrow PM)
- PR-19388: Omar + Noah + (later) Lauren/PM for defaults, Sean for SRE impact

Anything missing?

Sahana Iyer: please add perf acceptance note in PR descriptions (esp 19357/19371): “no >1% throughput regression in perf-canary mixed-length scenario”. helps us decide when to rerun jobs.

Omar El-Sayed: also request: when 19357 lands, do NOT flip the flag in code. we’ll do enablement via config after perf-canary passes.

Allison Grant: PR-19342: left comments. once updated I’m good to approve.

Sean Gallagher: same, one ask: add a `kv_cache_compaction_bytes_moved_total` counter stub now (even if always 0) so dashboards can be built without another deploy.

Samir Qureshi: can do. will add `bytes_moved_total` + `compaction_time_ms_total` placeholders behind flag, increment only when compaction actually runs.

Talia Benson: perfect. I’ll keep the release train thread updated once 19342 merges. please keep this thread as the source of truth for merge order + who’s blocking.

Omar El-Sayed: :thumbsup: I’ll chase remaining reviews if anything stalls >4h.

GitHub Bot: PR-19342 updated with new commits.

Allison Grant: approved ✅

Sean Gallagher: approved ✅

Talia Benson: awesome. Samir once 19342 merges, please rebase 19357 and drop a note here so Noah/Omar can start review.

perf-ci-bot: :rotating_light: CI perf regression detected on `main` (runtime/perf-suite)
- culprit range: merge base..HEAD includes `pr-19405-add-compaction-tracing-spans`
- scenario: `decode_mixed_lengths_burst` (A100), `hosted_profile`
- delta: p50 +0.6%, p95 +2.1%, p99 +4.3%
- throughput: -1.4%
- fail threshold: p99 > +3.0%
- report: https://perf-ci.redwood.ai/runs/1767740942 (internal)

Sahana Iyer: seeing this too. it’s a hard fail on the gating job, not flaky (3/3 repeats). anyone around that can sanity check if the spans are on the hot path?

Marcus Reed: yup, looks tied to the tracing PR. the regression aligns exactly with when we added per-step spans for compaction/eviction. decode loop got a bit fatter.

Julian Moore: fwiw the PR adds:
- `trace::span!("kv.compaction.step", ...)` inside `maybe_compact()`
- extra counters in the allocator for bytes moved + eviction reasons
If those are executing even when compaction is *not* moving bytes, we’re paying overhead every decode tick.

Samir Qureshi: `maybe_compact()` is called at decode boundary for every active seq (budget check). even when budget=0 it still touches a couple atomics + now a span create/enter.

Omar El-Sayed: can we see how many spans we’re generating? If it’s per sequence per step that’s going to be brutal.

Marcus Reed: quick profile from the perf runner shows `tracing::dispatcher::get_default` and `span::Id::from_u64` showing up in top 20. not huge individually but multiplied.

Allison Grant: are these spans needed at full resolution? i thought we wanted exemplars, not “every tick”. we can sample or emit only when bytes_moved > 0 / eviction happens.

Julian Moore: +1. we can gate span creation behind `if moved_bytes != 0 || evicted != 0`.
Right now it’s:
```rust
let _s = tracing::info_span!("kv.compaction.step", budget, frag_ratio, ...).entered();
// then checks and early returns
```
so span is always created even on early return.

Sofia Ivanova: also scheduler hook calls it at prefill/decode boundaries; for decode it’s per microbatch. so worst case we’re creating spans at microbatch frequency.

Caleb Johnson: tracing overhead depends on subscriber too. In CI do we have tracing enabled? (I assume yes since the runtime has the default subscriber installed.) But we can lower level to `debug` and keep production at `info`?

Sahana Iyer: perf CI runs with `RUNTIME_TRACING=1` because we want the metrics; i can toggle for perf suite but then we lose signal for compaction correlation.

Logan Wright: metrics are separate from tracing. if we’re relying on tracing spans for exemplars, we can sample spans while keeping counters always-on. or use `tracing::enabled!(Level::INFO)` checks before span creation.

Samir Qureshi: the counters are atomics too. in the PR we increment `compaction_attempts` even when no move happens. should probably only increment on actual move to reduce churn (and make metric meaningful).

Marcus Reed: question is what we do *now* to unblock `main`.
Option A: quick revert PR-19405 and re-land with sampling
Option B: hotfix PR on top that changes spans to be conditional + reduces counter updates
Connor, what’s the release train pressure here?

Connor O'Brien: runtime-1.22 cut is this week; we don’t want to destabilize perf gates. If it’s failing CI we can’t ship it as-is. Prefer minimal fix over revert if it’s quick (< half day) and low risk.

Omar El-Sayed: i’d rather not revert entirely because SRE asked specifically for trace correlation during canary. but we can absolutely make spans “event-driven”.

Allison Grant: please keep the span names/fields stable. dashboards will key off `kv.compaction.*` and `kv.eviction.*`. but changing frequency is fine.

Julian Moore: proposal:
1) Replace `info_span!(...).entered()` with:
- `if moved_bytes > 0 { tracing::info!(...); }` for now (event instead of span)
- or `if tracing::enabled!(Level::INFO) && moved_bytes > 0 { let _s=... }`
2) Move allocator counters off the decode hot path:
- maintain local counters per worker tick and flush at end-of-batch (non-atomic) OR
- only atomic-add when `moved_bytes > 0 || evicted > 0`

Samir Qureshi: easiest: gate both span + counter updates behind `if moved_bytes != 0 || evicted != 0`. that should eliminate most overhead in steady state.

Caleb Johnson: if you keep spans, also avoid formatting strings/fields with heap alloc. e.g. don’t log large vectors. keep to ints.

Marcus Reed: can someone own the patch? i can run perf locally but i’m on benchmark work today.

Julian Moore: i can do it. Samir, can you review quickly since it’s in allocator path?

Samir Qureshi: yes. ping me on the PR.

Sahana Iyer: please also add a micro perf test around `maybe_compact()` call overhead? even a small criterion bench would help prevent reintroducing.

Omar El-Sayed: also make sure we still emit something when compaction is *triggered* but blocked by budget=0. that’s useful debugging. maybe `debug` event for attempts, `info` span/event for actual moves.

Allison Grant: +1. from observability standpoint:
- always-on counters: `kv_compaction_bytes_moved_total`, `kv_compaction_time_us_total`, `kv_evictions_total`
- optional sampled tracing: only when bytes_moved>0 OR eviction
Attempts counter isn’t that helpful unless it’s extremely cheap.

Logan Wright: if you want “attempts” without atomics, could do per-thread `u64` and export periodically. but that’s bigger change.

Julian Moore: ok doing minimal patch:
- remove span creation on no-op paths
- downgrade attempt event to `debug!` and only emit when `tracing::enabled!(DEBUG)`
- counters only updated on move/evict
Will post PR in ~1h.

perf-ci-bot: (automated) rerun requested by @Sahana Iyer for job `perf_gate_decode_mixed_lengths_burst`

Connor O'Brien: please tag it as `release-blocker` if it’s the fix. we need green by EOD.

Sahana Iyer: thanks. once PR is up i’ll kick the perf CI rerun.

[~70 min later]

Julian Moore: PR up: https://github.com/redwood/redwood/pull/19412
Summary:
- gated `kv.compaction.step` span to only create when `bytes_moved>0 || evicted>0`
- removed unconditional `compaction_attempts` atomic inc
- added `debug!` event when compaction considered but skipped due to budget=0 (guarded by `tracing::enabled!`)
Samir review appreciated.

Samir Qureshi: looking now. diff is small. one nit: the `debug!` includes `frag_ratio` float formatting; should be ok but can we keep it as fixed-point int? not blocking.

Julian Moore: good call, i’ll change to `frag_ratio_bp` (basis points) to avoid float formatting.

Marcus Reed: i pulled the branch and reran the local perf harness; p99 delta back to +0.7% (noise). looks promising.

Sahana Iyer: perf CI rerun started.

Allison Grant: ty for keeping the field names consistent-ish. `frag_ratio_bp` is fine as long as it’s documented.

perf-ci-bot: :white_check_mark: Perf gate passed for `decode_mixed_lengths_burst` on PR 19412
- p99 delta: +0.8%
- throughput delta: -0.2%

Connor O'Brien: ship it. once merged we can unpause the release train.

Omar El-Sayed: follow-up item: we should decide if we want spans vs events long-term. spans are nice for time attribution but only when we’re actually moving bytes.

Sahana Iyer: +1. I’ll open a small ticket to add a perf regression test for tracing overhead / ensure `RUNTIME_TRACING=1` stays within budget.

Julian Moore: merged after Samir’s approval. thanks all.

source.text:
Marcus Reed: Dropping early bench results for compaction branch (allocator+relocate behind flag). Ran mixed-length burst scenario + long-lived sessions. TL;DR: p99 looks better once fragmentation starts, but budget too high can show up as decode jitter. Sheet: https://drive.google.com/…/kv-frag-results-jan

Rina Kobayashi: :eyes: which scenario version? is this with the new churn generator or the older mixed-length script?

Marcus Reed: New churn generator from benchmark-lab PR-881 (1.5k concurrent, cancel rate ~12%, prompt lens bimodal 128/2k, decode 256). 30 min steady + 5 min burst every 10.

Samir Qureshi: Nice. Can you paste the headline deltas? Particularly fragmentation ratio and bytes moved.

Marcus Reed: Sure (H100, llama3-70b-ish profile, decode-heavy):
- Baseline (no compaction): frag_ratio climbs 0.18 -> 0.47 over 25 min, then oscillates 0.40-0.52
- With compaction (trigger 0.28, budget 96MB/step): frag_ratio stabilizes 0.12-0.22
- p99 decode token latency during burst: 185ms -> 128ms
- p95: 62ms -> 55ms
- Throughput: -1.8% avg tokens/s
- Bytes moved: ~22GB over 30 min (peaks during burst)

Omar El-Sayed: That p99 drop is the first “real” number I’ve seen that matches the hypothesis. :thumbsup:

Sofia Ivanova: What’s “budget 96MB/step” mapped to in scheduler terms here? per-iteration? per decode boundary? (I’m trying to reason about worst-case stalls.)

Marcus Reed: Current wiring is “per scheduler loop iteration, after decode batch finishes” (so it can run multiple times/sec depending on load). In these runs, compaction ran ~4-8 times/sec under churn.

Sofia Ivanova: ok that’s the concern. 96MB x 8/sec is a lot of copy if it lines up badly. Do you have trace exemplars showing per-iteration compaction time?

Marcus Reed: Coarse timing from logs (not full tracing spans yet): p50 compaction step 0.4ms, p95 1.7ms, p99 3.6ms. But there are spikes when it has to relocate a “bad” chain; saw a couple 7-9ms steps.

Julian Moore: Were those 7-9ms spikes on the same iterations where decode batch got smaller? (i.e., compaction stealing time from scheduling?)

Marcus Reed: Hard to say without the spans. My intuition is yes. You can see minor sawtooth in per-token latency when budget >128MB.

Samir Qureshi: I can add a hard time cap as well as bytes cap. Right now it’s bytes-first. If we do min(bytes_cap, time_cap) we can bound worst-case.

Allison Grant: +1 time cap. From SRE side we’ll want an explicit “compaction_time_ms” distribution anyway, but “budget_ms_per_step” is a cleaner knob for guardrails.

Omar El-Sayed: Agree. Also: budgets should differ for Hosted vs Dedicated. Hosted cares about jitter; Dedicated customers tend to accept stable throughput tradeoffs if it prevents OOM/evictions.

Nikhil Sharma: For Hosted: if this introduces occasional 8-10ms stalls, that will show up in p99 immediately for chat workloads (and we’ll get paged). I’d rather ship conservative defaults + ability to ramp.

Lauren Bishop: Dedicated: our biggest pain is burst-driven tail + forced shedding when fragmentation causes allocator “thrash.” If we can keep arena healthy, I’m ok paying a few % throughput. Dedicated buyers will prefer “predictable” over “max peak.”

Sean Gallagher: If we do tiered defaults, we need to be super explicit what metrics trigger rollback in canary. Suggest we keep initial Hosted budget low enough that even worst-case compaction can’t exceed, say, 2ms/loop.

Sofia Ivanova: That implies budget should be expressed in time not bytes (because bytes->time depends on GPU + copy path + contention). Bytes cap can be secondary safety.

Samir Qureshi: Implementation idea:
- budget_ms_per_step (default 1.0ms Hosted, 3.0ms Dedicated)
- budget_bytes_per_step (cap, default 32MB Hosted, 96MB Dedicated)
- trigger_frag_ratio (0.30 Hosted, 0.25 Dedicated)
- cool_down_ms between steps to avoid “hammering”

Marcus Reed: I can rerun a sweep to see where the knee is. My current sweeps were 32/64/96/128MB per step.

Rina Kobayashi: Please include A100 too. Some Hosted pools still A100 and their H2D/D2D copy behavior differs.

Marcus Reed: yup. Will queue A100 (80GB) tonight.

Omar El-Sayed: Another nuance: Dedicated has more long-lived sessions (agents). Compaction might be higher ROI there because fragmentation accumulates over hours. Hosted churn is high but sessions shorter.

Nikhil Sharma: Hosted also has more “noisy neighbor” patterns. If compaction eats budget during bursts, I’d rather we integrate with backpressure (i.e., pause compaction when queue depth is high).

Sofia Ivanova: We can gate compaction on scheduler health signals (queue depth, decode backlog, “time_since_last_tick”). I can add “skip compaction if scheduler_lag > X” or “if backlog > threshold.”

Sean Gallagher: For guardrails: if p99 token latency increases >5% over baseline for 10m AND compaction_time_p99 > threshold, auto-disable flag for cohort.

Allison Grant: Metric naming reminder: we’ll need at least:
- kv_compaction_steps_total
- kv_compaction_bytes_moved_total
- kv_compaction_time_ms (hist)
- kv_fragmentation_ratio (gauge)
- kv_evictions_total (once added)
…and they must be labeled by model + tier.

Samir Qureshi: Re budgets, I’m leaning:
Hosted default: 0.75ms step + 32MB cap, trigger 0.30
Dedicated default: 2.5ms step + 96MB cap, trigger 0.25
Both with “max_steps_per_sec” = 5?

Marcus Reed: In the H100 run, 32MB cap wasn’t enough to keep frag under control once it crossed ~0.40. It slowed growth but didn’t reverse fast. Might be ok if trigger is earlier.

Omar El-Sayed: That’s key: early trigger + small steps might be safer than late trigger + big steps.

Sofia Ivanova: Exactly. Let’s avoid “panic compaction.” We’d rather do continuous small maintenance.

Lauren Bishop: Dedicated bursts though: if they have a spike after idle, we may already be fragmented. Are we okay letting it heal slowly?

Samir Qureshi: Dedicated could allow a short “catch-up” mode when below load (prefill quiet). Like: if backlog low, temporarily raise time budget up to 5ms.

Nikhil Sharma: Please do NOT do catch-up mode in Hosted without a hard on/off flag. That’s the kind of thing that surprises us.

Sean Gallagher: +1. Keep Hosted behavior monotonic/simple for first release. No dynamic modes unless we can explain and monitor.

Julian Moore: If we keep it simple: tie compaction to a fixed phase boundary (end of decode batch) AND cap by time. That gives us deterministic-ish behavior.

Marcus Reed: Action items I can take:
1) Sweep trigger (0.22/0.26/0.30/0.34) with small time cap (0.5/1.0/2.0ms) on H100
2) Repeat on A100
3) Add a chart “p99 vs frag_ratio” to the sheet so we can pick trigger at the inflection point

Omar El-Sayed: :thumbsup: please also include “throughput delta” and “compaction bytes moved/sec” so we can sanity check copy load.

Allison Grant: And label runs with “Hosted-like” vs “Dedicated-like” workload profiles (session length distribution differs).

Marcus Reed: will do. I’ll add two workload presets in the harness and mark in the sheet.

Sofia Ivanova: Once you have the sweep, we can pick initial defaults and bake into runtime-1.22 config. Let’s keep Hosted conservative and plan to ramp via canary if results hold.

Samir Qureshi: I’ll open a follow-up PR for time-based budget + “max_steps_per_sec”. Should be small diff.

Sean Gallagher: Please ping me when it’s up; I want to review the metrics + failure modes (what happens if compaction never runs / always runs).

Marcus Reed: :ok_hand: Will post updated numbers tomorrow after the A100 run finishes.

1) Sweep trigger (0.22/0.26/0.30/0.34) with small time cap (0.5/1.0/2.0ms) on H100
2) Repeat on A100
3) Add a chart “p99 vs frag_ratio” to the sheet so we can pick trigger at the inflection point

Omar El-Sayed: :thumbsup: please also include “throughput delta” and “compaction bytes moved/sec” so we can sanity check copy load.

Allison Grant: And label runs with “Hosted-like” vs “Dedicated-like” workload profiles (session length distribution differs).

Marcus Reed: will do. I’ll add two workload presets in the harness and mark in the sheet.

Sofia Ivanova: Once you have the sweep, we can pick initial defaults and bake into runtime-1.22 config. Let’s keep Hosted conservative and plan to ramp via canary if results hold.

Samir Qureshi: I’ll open a follow-up PR for time-based budget + “max_steps_per_sec”. Should be small diff.

Sean Gallagher: Please ping me when it’s up; I want to review the metrics + failure modes (what happens if compaction never runs / always runs).

Marcus Reed: :ok_hand: Will post updated numbers tomorrow after the A100 run finishes.

source.text:
Omar El-Sayed: Need eyes on the proposed eviction policy before I turn it into the ADR. Goal: when allocator hits pressure (or frag-trigger), we evict cached KV to free contiguous pages rather than thrash/oom. Proposal is tier-aware LRU (sequence-level) + prefix-cache interaction rules. Questions: pinned sessions, and whether prefix KV should be in the eviction pool at all.

Samir Qureshi: can you paste the sketch of the policy? esp what you mean by “tier-aware” (Hosted vs Dedicated) and what object is LRU’d (seq vs block)

Omar El-Sayed: yup. current draft:
- Maintain LRU list per “eviction class” keyed by (tier, pool)
- Eviction classes ordered by priority: Free/Trial Hosted < Paid Hosted < Dedicated burst < Dedicated pinned < Private (no eviction unless explicitly enabled)
- Evict whole sequences’ KV allocations (not individual blocks) to avoid partial state
- Don’t evict ACTIVE sequences (currently decoding/prefill) — only idle ones
- For prefix cache: prefixes are treated as “preemptible cached seqs” with their own class at bottom (i.e., evict prefixes before paid hosted idle chats)

Noah Patel: “evict whole sequence” is good for correctness, but need to be precise: what does eviction mean for an idle sequence? do we fail subsequent decode? do we restart from prompt? I assume this is for shared compute / non-pinned sessions where we can return an error upstream?

Omar El-Sayed: for idle sequences (i.e., session that might continue later): eviction means we drop KV for that session. If it gets a new token later, the scheduler treats it as cache-miss and recomputes from prompt (or refuses if prompt not available). For Hosted chat we *usually* have the full transcript/prompt, so recompute is possible; for some internal streaming modes we may not. Those would be “pinned” / non-evictable.

Sofia Ivanova: +1 we need crisp invariants for scheduler. “pinned” in scheduler today basically means “don’t migrate / don’t preempt”. If eviction tries to touch those, I’d rather we backpressure earlier instead of surprise recompute.

Lena Fischer: from overload/backpressure angle: if we can’t allocate due to pinned / high priority, we should shed low-tier requests *before* we evict something user-visible for paid/dedicated. so ordering matters.

Allison Grant: also for observability: eviction counters need labels by tier + reason (pressure vs frag-compaction vs emergency). otherwise we’re blind when p99 goes weird.

Samir Qureshi: I’m a bit nervous about “prefix cache at bottom”. prefix cache is supposed to be a win for latency; if we always evict it first under pressure, we’re basically turning it off during bursts. maybe that’s ok but we should be explicit.

Marcus Reed: in the mixed-length burst scenario, prefix cache hit rate drops anyway because churn. but if eviction nukes it, our “after” graphs might look worse than baseline. we should decide what we want to optimize for: tail vs average.

Mateo Alvarez: optimize for tail + stability first. prefix cache is nice-to-have if it’s fighting with serving stability. but we should still avoid pathological thrash.

Noah Patel: correctness point: prefix cache isn’t just “nice-to-have” if it’s referenced by active sequences. you need refcounting. if seq A is using prefix P as its first N blocks, you can’t evict P until A is done or has its own copy.

Omar El-Sayed: yep. prefix cache entries are currently “shared prefixes” with refcount > 0 while attached. eviction only considers prefixes with refcount==0 (fully detached). for attached prefixes, they’re effectively part of the sequence’s KV allocation.

Samir Qureshi: ok, but implementationally: do we actually have a single object that represents “sequence KV” vs “prefix KV”? because allocator sees pages. if you say “evict whole sequence”, you need a stable mapping from seq->pages.

Omar El-Sayed: we do: each seq has a handle with a list of KV blocks. prefix cache entry is another handle. attached prefix means seq handle contains pointers to prefix-owned blocks but marked “shared”. eviction path will only take handles where all blocks are owned (or shared w/ refcount==0).

Sofia Ivanova: wait, “shared w/ refcount==0” is weird. if refcount==0 it shouldn’t be shared.

Omar El-Sayed: sorry sloppy wording. for eviction: seq eviction only allowed if it doesn’t reference any shared blocks. prefix eviction only allowed if its refcount==0. we never evict a seq that references shared blocks (because that implies it’s active or at least still logically attached).

Noah Patel: that implies if a seq is idle but still attached to a shared prefix, we *won’t* evict it. is that intended? because that might block eviction and we’ll keep more memory pinned.

Omar El-Sayed: good catch. alternative is: on eviction, we can detach the seq from the shared prefix by “materializing” its prefix blocks (copy-on-write) OR by treating the shared prefix as part of the seq for eviction purposes (i.e., evicting the prefix even if refcount>0 by force-detach). Both are risky.

Samir Qureshi: force-detach is scary. materialize is expensive and defeats the purpose.

Mateo Alvarez: I’d rather be conservative. If idle seq is attached to shared prefix, classify it as non-evictable (for now) and rely on backpressure / evict other things.

Lena Fischer: +1. we can add a metric for “eviction skipped due to shared-prefix attachment” and see if it’s common. if it’s rare, fine.

Marcus Reed: in practice, the prefix cache is most used by short-lived sessions. those sequences end quickly and refcounts go back to 0. I don’t expect tons of idle-but-attached.

Allison Grant: if we’re going conservative, please make sure the “skipped” metric includes a breakdown so we don’t misinterpret low eviction counts as “healthy”.

Benji Okafor: config/flags question: do we want separate knobs for (1) allow eviction at all, (2) allow eviction of prefix cache, (3) allow eviction of paid tiers? I’m thinking runtime flags like:
- kv_eviction_enabled
- kv_eviction_allow_prefix_cache
- kv_eviction_min_tier (free|paid|dedicated|none)

Omar El-Sayed: yes. I was thinking a single “min_tier” gate plus a boolean for prefix cache since it’s not exactly a customer tier. Something like:
- min_evictable_class = hosted_free by default
- prefix_cache_evictable = true by default for Hosted, false for Dedicated?

Sofia Ivanova: for Dedicated, “prefix cache” is still used but it’s mostly within a single customer pool, right? If so, evicting it hurts only that customer, not neighbors. Might be ok, but Dedicated folks care about p99 too.

Noah Patel: pinned sessions: can we define them sharply? I want: pinned == cannot be evicted and cannot be compacted in a way that changes address unless relocation is atomic. otherwise this becomes a correctness bug farm.

Samir Qureshi: pinned in allocator already means “don’t move these pages during compaction” (we added a pin bit on blocks in the draft). eviction should respect the same pin bit.

Omar El-Sayed: proposal update:
- Use the same pin bit: pinned blocks are excluded from both compaction relocation candidates and eviction handles
- Pinned sessions should be *admitted* only if headroom exists; otherwise reject at admission (scheduler) rather than later eviction.

Lena Fischer: admission control path exists. we can add “kv_pin_reservation_bytes” per pool and fail fast if below threshold.

Mateo Alvarez: ok. last issue: what’s the exact ordering? I don’t want “Dedicated burst” evicted before “Paid Hosted” if Dedicated is paying for reserved.

Omar El-Sayed: ordering I’ll put in ADR:
1) Hosted Free/Trial idle seq
2) Hosted background (evals/internal) idle seq
3) Hosted Paid idle seq
4) Prefix cache entries w/ refcount==0 (configurable: can be moved earlier/later)
5) Dedicated *preemptible* idle seq (explicit flag)
Never: Dedicated pinned, Private by default

Sofia Ivanova: makes sense. one nuance: prefix cache maybe should be #1 actually (evictable + doesn’t create user-visible recompute failures, just cache miss). But it can increase load and cause worse tail in the next minute. tradeoff.

Marcus Reed: we can measure. if we evict prefix cache first, we might see throughput drop because more prefill. but tail might still improve due to avoiding OOM/fragmentation. I can add a benchmark toggle for “prefix eviction first vs last”.

Allison Grant: if you do that, please emit a config tag in metrics so we can compare cohorts.

Noah Patel: can we commit to: prefix eviction only when pressure is “hard” (allocation failure imminent), not on “soft” frag-trigger? i.e., compaction handles soft, eviction handles hard.

Samir Qureshi: +1. eviction is a bigger hammer.

Omar El-Sayed: I like that. So:
- Soft trigger (frag_ratio > X): compaction only
- Hard trigger (alloc fail / watermark): eviction, starting with hosted_free + background; prefix cache eviction only if still can’t satisfy request OR if “hard_prefix_ok” flag set

Mateo Alvarez: good. write it up like that.

Omar El-Sayed: will update ADR draft and post for review. TODOs I’m taking:
- define eviction reason codes (HARD_ALLOC_FAIL, HARD_WATERMARK, MANUAL)
- clarify pinned semantics (shared w/ compaction pin bit)
- add “skip reasons” metrics for refcount/pin

Allison Grant: and please add metric names you plan to use. I’ll sanity check against our perf standards.

Sofia Ivanova: tag me in the doc. also, make sure scheduler gets a signal when eviction happened so it can annotate traces + avoid repeating eviction every tick.

Omar El-Sayed: yep, I’ll add “eviction cooldown” in scheduler hook discussion. thanks all.

Benji Okafor: once you land on knobs, tell me and I’ll make sure config propagation supports them (and kill switch can flip fast).

Omar El-Sayed: :thumbsup: will do

Sofia Ivanova: tag me in the doc. also, make sure scheduler gets a signal when eviction happened so it can annotate traces + avoid repeating eviction every tick.

Omar El-Sayed: yep, I’ll add “eviction cooldown” in scheduler hook discussion. thanks all.

Benji Okafor: once you land on knobs, tell me and I’ll make sure config propagation supports them (and kill switch can flip fast).

Omar El-Sayed: :thumbsup: will do

source.thread:
Sofia Ivanova: Trying to close on compaction approach. Tradeoff feels like incremental-in-scheduler vs background worker. Main worry: decode stalls if we do too much on the critical path. Thoughts?

Omar El-Sayed: Yeah, agree. Background compaction sounds nice in theory, but the KV arena is shared + we’d be moving pages that active sequences might touch. If we do it async, we need real pinning + handoff semantics anyway.

Samir Qureshi: If we do “always-on background”, we also risk chewing GPU memcpy bandwidth during decode and messing with p99 in a different way.

Sofia Ivanova: Right. Scheduler boundaries: where can we hide it? Between decode steps? End of a batch? Only after prefill?

Samir Qureshi: My current proposal:
- incremental compaction steps, *budgeted*, invoked at a safe scheduler boundary
- boundary = after we’ve built the next decode batch but before launching kernels OR right after a decode step completes, before we admit new work
- hard budget in either bytes moved or wall time (like 50–150us per tick)

Noah Patel: Incremental is easier to reason about for correctness IMO. Background thread will require more locking + invariants (“page can’t be moved while referenced by kernel in-flight”) and that gets hairy.

Julian Moore: For boundaries: we already have a per-iteration phase split (`prefill -> decode -> bookkeeping`). If compaction runs in bookkeeping w/ strict budget, the worst case is it eats into next iteration start, but it’s bounded.

Sofia Ivanova: But bookkeeping is on CPU side mostly. Actual copy/pack is GPU work, right? Won’t that still steal time from the decode stream?

Samir Qureshi: We can schedule the copy kernels on a low-priority stream, but yeah, it still shares memory bandwidth. The question is “predictable small tax” vs “rare big stall from fragmentation/eviction at bad times”.

Omar El-Sayed: +1. Today the stall is unbounded when allocator hits a bad free list state. I’d rather pay controlled tax.

Mateo Alvarez: We should be explicit: what’s the failure mode we’re avoiding? If decode loop blocks waiting for KV alloc / defrag, we spike p99. If compaction is unbounded, we also spike p99. So the only acceptable thing is compaction w/ hard cap + kill switch.

Noah Patel: Also, cancellation edge cases. If we relocate KV for seq X while request is being cancelled, you can get orphan pages or double-free unless the state machine is tight.

Samir Qureshi: Yep. That’s why I don’t love background worker: you’re constantly racing cancel/finish. With incremental invoked by scheduler, we can gate relocation on sequence state == “stable” (not in-flight kernel, not cancelling).

Julian Moore: How do we detect “in-flight kernel” safely? We can treat “seq included in current decode batch” as pinned until decode completes + we’ve advanced the pointer.

Sofia Ivanova: So scheduler provides pin/unpin around decode step, and compaction only touches unpinned?

Omar El-Sayed: Exactly. Think of it like:
- mark batch seqs pinned
- run decode
- unpin
- compaction tick can move from “movable set” only

Samir Qureshi: Here’s a sketch (super rough):
```
loop {
  build_decode_batch();
  pin(batch);
  launch_decode(batch);
  wait_decode_complete();
  unpin(batch);

  // safe point
  if (frag_ratio > threshold) {
     compaction_tick(bytes_budget=B, time_budget_us=T);
  }
}
```

Sofia Ivanova: What about prefill? Prefill tends to allocate large contiguous blocks, could be a better time to compact?

Omar El-Sayed: Prefill is also latency sensitive (TTFT). But we can be stricter there: maybe only run compaction after prefill for *non-streaming* or only when backlog is low.

Mateo Alvarez: I’d rather not add variability to TTFT in v1. Keep compaction tied to decode loop + budget.

Noah Patel: Another concern: compaction could create head-of-line blocking if it holds allocator lock too long. Make sure the lock scope is tiny (plan moves, then execute moves).

Samir Qureshi: Plan/execute split is in the design: CPU computes relocation plan (free list snapshot) under short lock, releases, then does GPU copies + metadata swap with per-seq locks.

Julian Moore: Background worker option: can we do “opportunistic background only when GPU idle”? Like when scheduler sees no decode work but fragmentation high, we can compact more aggressively.

Sofia Ivanova: That seems like a hybrid that reduces risk. Incremental always available; background only when we’re not serving.

Omar El-Sayed: Hybrid is good as long as it’s *never* competing with active decode. Also if it’s “idle”, we may still have long-lived streaming sessions waiting for next token. Hard to call truly idle.

Mateo Alvarez: Let’s frame it as:
1) primary mechanism = scheduler-driven incremental compaction with strict budgets
2) optional add-on later = “drain mode” compaction for dedicated pools during low traffic windows
Keep scope sane.

Sofia Ivanova: OK. What budgets are we thinking? If we do 100us per tick, at 200 ticks/sec that’s 20ms/sec overhead, might be fine.

Samir Qureshi: Budget proposal from early tests: start with `max_bytes_moved_per_tick=64MB` and `max_time_per_tick=150us`, whichever hits first. We’ll tune per tier.

Noah Patel: 64MB per tick seems big if it actually happens; time budget should dominate. Also ensure we can stop mid-plan without leaving arena inconsistent.

Samir Qureshi: Agreed. The implementation will be resumable: move N pages, checkpoint progress, bail.

Julian Moore: Scheduler boundary question: should we do it before admitting new requests (backpressure-friendly)? If compaction is going to run, do it before we increase concurrency.

Sofia Ivanova: Yeah, compaction tick after unpin + before `admit()` makes sense. Also we can skip compaction entirely if queue depth > X.

Omar El-Sayed: That’s the key: integrate with backpressure. If we’re overloaded, we should *not* compact (or compact only minimal) because any extra work hurts. But if we never compact under load, fragmentation gets worse and we die later.

Mateo Alvarez: We need a policy: under overload, cap compaction to tiny (like 25us) + allow eviction if allocator is at risk. Otherwise we’re just deferring a cliff.

Sofia Ivanova: Ok, action items:
- I’ll update scheduler proposal: safe point after decode/unpin, before admit; skip or reduce budget when queue depth high
- Samir/Omar align on hard budget defaults + what “pinned” means
- Noah sanity check invariants + cancellation interactions

Noah Patel: :thumbsup: Please include an explicit “decode stall budget” in the doc. If compaction tick exceeds budget, it must abort and emit metric.

Samir Qureshi: Will do. Also adding a metric `kv_compaction_tick_over_budget_total`.

Omar El-Sayed: I’ll add notes on why background-only is risky (race + bandwidth contention) so we have it captured for review.

Mateo Alvarez: Good. Keep it shippable behind a flag. If we see p99 wobble in canary, we need to disable within minutes.

Sofia Ivanova: +1. I’ll also make sure feature flag is checked in scheduler path (cheap) and defaults off for hosted until canary.

Julian Moore: Cool. I’ll help with instrumentation so we can correlate `compaction_tick_time_us` with decode latency. Need tracing spans around the safe point.

Sofia Ivanova: Thanks all.

source.text:
Omar El-Sayed: Kicking off kv fragmentation investigation thread. We need a baseline before we touch allocator/compaction. Can folks drop (1) reproduction workload, (2) allocator snapshot output, (3) initial KPI proposal (what we track + target)?

Marcus Reed: I can run the mixed-length churn workload we’ve used for decode tail spikes (the “bursty chat” profile). Question: do we already have a snapshot tool or are we printing internal state?

Samir Qureshi: We have a debug dump behind `RUNTIME_KV_ARENA_DUMP=1` (staging only). It prints page map + free list buckets + a couple summary stats. I’ll paste a sample from last night.

Samir Qureshi: snapshot (A100, llama-2-70b-ish config, 80 req/s, mixed 64-2k prompt, decode up to 512 tokens). After ~25 min steady load + 3 burst cycles:
```
kv_arena:
  page_size_bytes=2097152
  total_pages=1536
  used_pages=1392
  free_pages=144
  free_list_buckets=[0:12, 1:7, 2:5, 4:4, 8:3, 16:2, 32:1, 64:0, 128:0]
  largest_free_run_pages=9
  alloc_fail_soft=27
  alloc_fail_hard=0
  evict_count=0
  relocate_count=0

frag_summary:
  free_bytes=301989888
  free_runs=102
  avg_free_run_bytes=2960685
  largest_free_run_bytes=18874368
  fragmentation_ratio=0.9375

notes:
  cancellations=412
  seq_end=18493
```
Fragmentation ratio here is `1 - (largest_free_run / total_free)` (so higher = worse). Looks nasty: largest run is ~18MB out of ~288MB free.

Marcus Reed: Whoa. That aligns with the “can’t allocate contiguous” stalls we saw? `alloc_fail_soft=27` is the re-try path right?

Samir Qureshi: yup. “soft” == we had enough total free pages but couldn’t satisfy the run-length needed for the next KV extend without walking/merging for a while. That’s where we see occasional 10-40ms spikes in the allocator critical section.

Sofia Ivanova: Can we correlate this snapshot with p99 decode? Like grab a snapshot right before/after the spikes?

Julian Moore: I can add a quick log hook to emit a snapshot when `alloc_fail_soft > 0` in a 10s window + include current scheduler phase (prefill vs decode). But we should keep it staging-only or rate-limited.

Allison Grant: Before we go too far: we need to agree on metric definitions (names + semantics). I’m seeing “fragmentation_ratio” in Samir’s dump, but we should standardize what it means. Also we probably need at least: free_bytes, largest_free_run, bytes_moved (once compaction exists), compaction_time_ms, eviction_count, and a “compaction_active” flag.

Omar El-Sayed: +1. For now I’m mostly looking for baseline numbers and a KPI target we can socialize (runtime + SRE). Samir, can you share a second snapshot from a “healthy” state (fresh boot / low churn) so we have a delta?

Samir Qureshi: same config, 2 min after start (before burst):
```
kv_arena:
  total_pages=1536
  used_pages=812
  free_pages=724
  largest_free_run_pages=662
frag_summary:
  free_bytes=1518338048
  largest_free_run_bytes=1387266048
  fragmentation_ratio=0.0864
alloc_fail_soft=0
```
So we go from ~0.09 to ~0.94 after churn.

Sean Gallagher: jumping in from SRE side. What tail metric are we anchoring this to? For Hosted, folks keep quoting p99 end-to-end. If this is allocator-induced, we should track a runtime-internal latency too (e.g., “decode step time” or “scheduler loop duration”) so we can distinguish network/queueing.

Marcus Reed: For perf suite I can capture:
- p50/p95/p99 end-to-end request latency
- p95/p99 time-to-first-token
- decode token latency distribution
Also can isolate “allocator critical section time” if we have a span/counter.

Omar El-Sayed: Proposed KPIs (v0):
1) Fragmentation: keep `fragmentation_ratio` <= 0.25 in steady state under the mixed-length churn workload (after warmup + 3 bursts).
2) Tail latency: under same workload, p99 decode token time regression <= +3% vs baseline (and ideally improve by >=10% on the burst scenario).
3) Safety: hard alloc fails / OOMs = 0; correctness test suite green.
4) Overhead: compaction CPU/GPU copy time <= 1% wall clock (or budgeted so it can’t spike decode).
Feedback?

Allison Grant: On #1: ratio threshold is fine but we also need absolute numbers. Ratio can look “good” when free_bytes is tiny (near-full arena). Suggest we emit both:
- `kv_free_bytes`
- `kv_largest_free_run_bytes`
- `kv_fragmentation_ratio = 1 - largest_free_run_bytes / max(free_bytes, 1)`
And alerting uses ratio only when free_bytes > some minimum.

Sean Gallagher: +1. Also for rollout gating I want a single “allocator health” panel: ratio + soft_fail_rate + time_in_alloc_ms. If soft_fail_rate goes up, we roll back.

Sofia Ivanova: For the overhead KPI: we should enforce compaction only at safe boundaries (prefill/decode boundary) or with a strict per-iteration budget. If we try to compact mid-decode we’ll create the exact spikes we’re trying to eliminate.

Samir Qureshi: Agree. Also note: the run-length requirement grows with sequence length. When you get a couple long-lived chats, their KV extends tend to request larger contiguous chunks (depending on how we pack blocks). That’s why `largest_free_run` matters.

Marcus Reed: Can we define the “mixed-length churn workload” concretely so we’re not each running something slightly different? Here’s what I’ve been using:
- 70% sessions: prompt 64-256 tokens, decode 128-256
- 25%: prompt 256-1024, decode 256-512
- 5%: prompt 1024-2048, decode 256-512
- churn: 20% cancellation rate mid-stream
- bursts: 2x QPS for 90s every 8 min
I can check this into benchmark-lab if we agree.

Omar El-Sayed: Yes, let’s lock that in as “frag-burst-v1”. Marcus please write it up + link here.

Mateo Alvarez: (catching up) This is great. Two asks:
1) Make sure KPI is customer-aligned: “reduce p99 spikes during bursts” should be the headline.
2) Don’t overfit to a single model/GPU. At least run A100 + H100 + one smaller model config.

Marcus Reed: Ack. I’ll run A100/H100 for llama-70b profile + a smaller 13b-ish profile (whatever our standard perf harness uses).

Allison Grant: Naming proposal for metrics (so dashboards aren’t a mess):
- `runtime.kv.free_bytes`
- `runtime.kv.largest_free_run_bytes`
- `runtime.kv.fragmentation_ratio`
- `runtime.kv.alloc_fail_soft_total`
- `runtime.kv.alloc_fail_hard_total`
Later:
- `runtime.kv.compaction.bytes_moved_total`
- `runtime.kv.compaction.time_ms_total`
- `runtime.kv.eviction.total`
- `runtime.kv.eviction.reason{tier,reason}`
Please don’t bake “A100/llama70b” into metric names; use labels.

Samir Qureshi: works for me. Also we should emit `runtime.kv.free_runs` (count of free extents). It’s a good proxy for fragmentation even when the ratio is noisy.

Sean Gallagher: +1 on `free_runs`. It tends to correlate with allocator walk time.

Omar El-Sayed: Cool, let’s finalize v0 KPIs:
- Fragmentation health: `fragmentation_ratio` and `free_runs` at end of warm steady state + after each burst. Target: ratio <= 0.25 AND free_runs <= 20 (tunable) when free_bytes >= 256MB.
- Tail latency: p99 decode token time and p99 end-to-end. Target: no regression vs baseline; stretch goal: reduce burst p99 by >=10%.
- Allocator pain signals: soft_fail_rate (per minute) should drop materially (goal: <1/min in the workload).
- Safety: no hard fails/OOM; cancel path correctness.

Marcus Reed: I’ll capture baseline runs today and post:
- timeseries of ratio/free_runs
- p95/p99
- a couple snapshots at spike times

Julian Moore: I can add the “snapshot on soft_fail spike” hook to staging this afternoon. Will keep it behind `RUNTIME_KV_ARENA_DUMP_ON_FAIL=1` and rate-limit to 1/min. Will PR once ready.

Allison Grant: Please also include a short note on how to compute ratio exactly (float, 0..1). We keep getting inverted ratios in dashboards :)

Samir Qureshi: lol yes. I’ll update the dump output to print both `largest_free_run_bytes` and the computed ratio so it’s unambiguous.

Omar El-Sayed: Great. Next step: Marcus baseline link + Samir add metric/dump clarifications. Then we can draft the design doc with real numbers.

Marcus Reed: will post results by EOD. :thumbsup:

Sean Gallagher: Thanks all. Once you have baseline, ping me and Allison—SRE wants to bake alerts from day 1 of canary.

Omar El-Sayed: will do.

Allison Grant: Before we go too far: we need to agree on metric definitions (names + semantics). I’m seeing “fragmentation_ratio” in Samir’s dump, but we should standardize what it means. Also we probably need at least: free_bytes, largest_free_run, bytes_moved (once compaction exists), compaction_time_ms, eviction_count, and a “compaction_active” flag.

Omar El-Sayed: +1. For now I’m mostly looking for baseline numbers and a KPI target we can socialize (runtime + SRE). Samir, can you share a second snapshot from a “healthy” state (fresh boot / low churn) so we have a delta?

Samir Qureshi: same config, 2 min after start (before burst):
```
kv_arena:
  total_pages=1536
  used_pages=812
  free_pages=724
  largest_free_run_pages=662
frag_summary:
  free_bytes=1518338048
  largest_free_run_bytes=1387266048
  fragmentation_ratio=0.0864
alloc_fail_soft=0
```
So we go from ~0.09 to ~0.94 after churn.

Sean Gallagher: jumping in from SRE side. What tail metric are we anchoring this to? For Hosted, folks keep quoting p99 end-to-end. If this is allocator-induced, we should track a runtime-internal latency too (e.g., “decode step time” or “scheduler loop duration”) so we can distinguish network/queueing.

Marcus Reed: For perf suite I can capture:
- p50/p95/p99 end-to-end request latency
- p95/p99 time-to-first-token
- decode token latency distribution
Also can isolate “allocator critical section time” if we have a span/counter.

Omar El-Sayed: Proposed KPIs (v0):
1) Fragmentation: keep `fragmentation_ratio` <= 0.25 in steady state under the mixed-length churn workload (after warmup + 3 bursts).
2) Tail latency: under same workload, p99 decode token time regression <= +3% vs baseline (and ideally improve by >=10% on the burst scenario).
3) Safety: hard alloc fails / OOMs = 0; correctness test suite green.
4) Overhead: compaction CPU/GPU copy time <= 1% wall clock (or budgeted so it can’t spike decode).
Feedback?

Allison Grant: On #1: ratio threshold is fine but we also need absolute numbers. Ratio can look “good” when free_bytes is tiny (near-full arena). Suggest we emit both:
- `kv_free_bytes`
- `kv_largest_free_run_bytes`
- `kv_fragmentation_ratio = 1 - largest_free_run_bytes / max(free_bytes, 1)`
And alerting uses ratio only when free_bytes > some minimum.

Sean Gallagher: +1. Also for rollout gating I want a single “allocator health” panel: ratio + soft_fail_rate + time_in_alloc_ms. If soft_fail_rate goes up, we roll back.

Sofia Ivanova: For the overhead KPI: we should enforce compaction only at safe boundaries (prefill/decode boundary) or with a strict per-iteration budget. If we try to compact mid-decode we’ll create the exact spikes we’re trying to eliminate.

Samir Qureshi: Agree. Also note: the run-length requirement grows with sequence length. When you get a couple long-lived chats, their KV extends tend to request larger contiguous chunks (depending on how we pack blocks). That’s why `largest_free_run` matters.

Marcus Reed: Can we define the “mixed-length churn workload” concretely so we’re not each running something slightly different? Here’s what I’ve been using:
- 70% sessions: prompt 64-256 tokens, decode 128-256
- 25%: prompt 256-1024, decode 256-512
- 5%: prompt 1024-2048, decode 256-512
- churn: 20% cancellation rate mid-stream
- bursts: 2x QPS for 90s every 8 min
I can check this into benchmark-lab if we agree.

Omar El-Sayed: Yes, let’s lock that in as “frag-burst-v1”. Marcus please write it up + link here.

Mateo Alvarez: (catching up) This is great. Two asks:
1) Make sure KPI is customer-aligned: “reduce p99 spikes during bursts” should be the headline.
2) Don’t overfit to a single model/GPU. At least run A100 + H100 + one smaller model config.

Marcus Reed: Ack. I’ll run A100/H100 for llama-70b profile + a smaller 13b-ish profile (whatever our standard perf harness uses).

Allison Grant: Naming proposal for metrics (so dashboards aren’t a mess):
- `runtime.kv.free_bytes`
- `runtime.kv.largest_free_run_bytes`
- `runtime.kv.fragmentation_ratio`
- `runtime.kv.alloc_fail_soft_total`
- `runtime.kv.alloc_fail_hard_total`
Later:
- `runtime.kv.compaction.bytes_moved_total`
- `runtime.kv.compaction.time_ms_total`
- `runtime.kv.eviction.total`
- `runtime.kv.eviction.reason{tier,reason}`
Please don’t bake “A100/llama70b” into metric names; use labels.

Samir Qureshi: works for me. Also we should emit `runtime.kv.free_runs` (count of free extents). It’s a good proxy for fragmentation even when the ratio is noisy.

Sean Gallagher: +1 on `free_runs`. It tends to correlate with allocator walk time.

Omar El-Sayed: Cool, let’s finalize v0 KPIs:
- Fragmentation health: `fragmentation_ratio` and `free_runs` at end of warm steady state + after each burst. Target: ratio <= 0.25 AND free_runs <= 20 (tunable) when free_bytes >= 256MB.
- Tail latency: p99 decode token time and p99 end-to-end. Target: no regression vs baseline; stretch goal: reduce burst p99 by >=10%.
- Allocator pain signals: soft_fail_rate (per minute) should drop materially (goal: <1/min in the workload).
- Safety: no hard fails/OOM; cancel path correctness.

Marcus Reed: I’ll capture baseline runs today and post:
- timeseries of ratio/free_runs
- p95/p99
- a couple snapshots at spike times

Julian Moore: I can add the “snapshot on soft_fail spike” hook to staging this afternoon. Will keep it behind `RUNTIME_KV_ARENA_DUMP_ON_FAIL=1` and rate-limit to 1/min. Will PR once ready.

Allison Grant: Please also include a short note on how to compute ratio exactly (float, 0..1). We keep getting inverted ratios in dashboards :)

Samir Qureshi: lol yes. I’ll update the dump output to print both `largest_free_run_bytes` and the computed ratio so it’s unambiguous.

Omar El-Sayed: Great. Next step: Marcus baseline link + Samir add metric/dump clarifications. Then we can draft the design doc with real numbers.

Marcus Reed: will post results by EOD. :thumbsup:

Sean Gallagher: Thanks all. Once you have baseline, ping me and Allison—SRE wants to bake alerts from day 1 of canary.

Omar El-Sayed: will do.

source.messages:
Alex Chen: Morning async — quick status:
- Done: landed kernel changes for "bucketed" batching (grouping by prefix hash instead of strict arrival). Observed ~12% throughput uplift on short-sequence mix.
- Blocker: 95th p99 tail on >2k tokens increased by ~30ms for a small subset of canary traffic. Possibly KV eviction interplay.
- Today: triaging eviction + warmup timing, will run a longer perf sweep at 10:30 PT and push a canary with conservative eviction thresholds.

Priya Natarajan: Nice work on bucketed batching. Quick thought — are we miscounting batchable windows when slot steal occurs?
If so, batches might be smaller in the tail causing more context switches.

Alex Chen: good call. I saw some slot-steal logs that align. Will add extra metrics for 'batchable_window_hits' and 'slot_steals_during_batch'.

Mateo Ruiz: I can run the longer perf sweep on the dedicated pool if you want. I have a runbook snippet:
```bash
./scripts/run_sweep.sh --profile short_seq_heavy --duration 30m --collect=latency,kv_hits,batch_sizes
```
I'll kick it off once you push the canary.

Alex Chen: please do. I'll tag you when canary is live (~10:35).

Jin Park: quick ping — left a tiny review on the batching patch (inline comment about an atomic on the slot counter). Not blocking but could cause ABA if we don't CAS correctly.

Alex Chen: thanks, Jin. I'll address the CAS and re-run unit tests.

Sara (SRE): Heads up: if the canary triggers the tail increase we'll auto-rollout-fallback to the older kernel per the policy, but we want to avoid that. If you want more headroom, enable the 0.5s soft-sla in the canary group.

Alex Chen: good point. I'll set the canary soft-sla to 0.5s and increase evict grace from 60s -> 120s for the canary pool.

bench-bot: perf-sweep started @ 2026-03-17T17:35:12Z for profile short_seq_heavy (duration 30m). Collecting metrics: latency, kv_hits, batch_sizes.

Mateo Ruiz: started run on rtx-dedicated-7; will watch dashboards and post a short summary when done.

Priya Natarajan: small debug tip — when you instrument 'kv_eviction_reason', add values: LRU, TTL, MANUAL, BATCH_PRESSURE. That made analysis easier last time.

Alex Chen: will add those enums. Also adding a quick repro command for the tail locally:
```python
# repro snippet
from infra.tests import synthetic_client
cli = synthetic_client(profile='long_seq_spike')
cli.run(200, warmup=50, seq_len=2500)
```

Jin Park: if you need help reproducing I can pair after lunch. I have a short script that toggles warmup timing and shows kv warm hit rate per request.

Alex Chen: appreciate it. If the sweep shows stable p95/p99 I'll open a follow-up to relax the eviction tweak; if not, will revert and iterate.

Mateo Ruiz: bench summary (short): after 30m run — steady throughput +11%, p95 +18ms, p99 +42ms on the canary. KV warm-hit rate dropped ~3% during peak bursts. Posting full graphs to #eng-runtime-dash in 5.

Alex Chen: thanks Mateo. That aligns with slot-steal hypothesis. Next steps: add slot metrics, tweak eviction grace for canary, address Jin's CAS, re-run.

Priya Natarajan: if you want an eyeball on the metrics once posted ping me and I'll help interpret.

Sara (SRE): I'll watch auto-rollout during the next hour and standby to pause if we cross the rollback threshold.

Alex Chen: summary: triage slot-steal metrics + add eviction_reason enums + fix CAS + tweak canary evict grace -> re-sweep. Will post updates here. Thanks all. :thumbsup:

source.text:
Yuki Sato: heads up from the baseline sweep today: on **L40S** we’re seeing attention kernel auto-select fall back to the slow path for a bunch of runs (qwen2.5-7b + long-prefill). TTFT p99 blows up vs H100/A100. Anyone else seeing this?

Marcus Reed: yep. it’s only on L40S in our bench fleet so far. the same manifest on A100 picks the fused kernel and looks fine.

Hiro Tanaka: do we have the kernel debug dump for one of the bad runs? (from the new `--dump-kernel-selection` flag)

Rina Kobayashi: pulled from the artifact bundle (run id `rw-baseline-2025-12-02_eu-west_l40s_qwen2.5-7b_longprefill_cold`). here’s the relevant snippet:
```text
[attn] device=L40S sm=89
[attn] requested=auto
[attn] candidate=flash_attn2: supported=true
[attn] candidate=fused_sdpa: supported=true
[attn] candidate=triton_sdpa: supported=true
[attn] select=triton_sdpa
[attn] reason=flash_attn2 rejected: "workspace bytes > limit" (limit=134217728)
[attn] reason=fused_sdpa rejected: "prefill_seq_len 8192 outside tuned regime"
```

Grace Kim: workspace > limit smells like we’re computing workspace based on max batch instead of realized? for these long-prefill runs we ramp concurrency but batch assembly is small-ish.

Omar El-Sayed: +1. also note long-prefill workload is heavy prefill, modest decode. if we’re pessimistically allocating for worst-case it’ll trip the cap.

Hiro Tanaka: for L40S we do have a tighter workspace cap vs A100/H100. but rejecting flash_attn2 entirely seems wrong. should degrade to a smaller tile config, not bail to triton.

Mateo Alvarez: what’s the impact on baseline deliverables? we can’t have “random kernel fallback” in the golden runs.

Marcus Reed: impact is material. quick compare from the tracker:
- H100: p99 TTFT ~ 1.8s
- L40S: p99 TTFT ~ 3.4s (when it picks triton)
variance is also worse.

Yuki Sato: and it’s not 100% deterministic. with same workload seed, different hosts sometimes pick flash_attn2 (passes cap) sometimes not. so it’s likely depending on batch shape / max_seq_len observed in the warmup window.

Hiro Tanaka: ok. for Q1 baseline we should pin the kernel so the measurement is attributable and repeatable.

Rina Kobayashi: we can do that in the harness via manifest override. today we have:
```json
kernel_selection_mode: auto
```
we can add something like `kernel_override: flash_attn2` (or whatever exact enum the runtime expects).

Grace Kim: what’s the safest “known good” on L40S for these models? flash_attn2?

Hiro Tanaka: yeah, flash_attn2 is what we want for this regime. fused_sdpa path is ok but we’ve seen cliffs around 8k depending on head_dim.

Mateo Alvarez: decision: **pin flash_attn2 on L40S for baseline runs** until auto-selection is fixed. but make sure we still *report* what would’ve been selected in auto (so we can track the bug).

Omar El-Sayed: can we do both? like run with override but also log the auto decision as “would_select”? or at least record the rejection reasons.

Hiro Tanaka: we can do “dry-run selection” logging. not in place yet but we can hack it for benchmark mode.

Yuki Sato: short-term proposal:
1) for baseline sweeps: set `RW_KERNEL_OVERRIDE=flash_attn2` in the bench job env **only for L40S**
2) keep `--dump-kernel-selection` enabled so we capture the rejection reasons when override is not set (we can schedule a nightly auto run separately)
3) open bug + fix in auto-selection: workspace limit calc + add a smaller workspace config for flash_attn2 on L40S

Rina Kobayashi: works for me. i’ll patch the workload runner to accept a per-SKU override in the run matrix, and write it into the manifest as:
- `kernel_selection_mode=override`
- `kernel_override=flash_attn2`
Also adding a big note in the run tracker so nobody compares L40S auto vs pinned apples-to-oranges.

Marcus Reed: +1 and for dashboards we should tag these runs `kernel=pinned` so we don’t mix with future auto.

Mateo Alvarez: :thumbsup: please do. and add an explicit “baseline exception” entry in the plan doc: L40S pinned kernel due to known auto-selection instability.

Grace Kim: can someone file the issue and assign? i can help test once there’s a patch.

Yuki Sato: filing now. will tag you + Hiro. ETA: initial fix PR by end of week if we can reproduce reliably on the L40S bench nodes.

Hiro Tanaka: cool. also let’s capture a minimal repro command for the issue.

Rina Kobayashi: repro from the harness (current head) that hits the fallback ~50%:
```bash
benchlab run \
  --model qwen2.5-7b-instruct \
  --workload long_prefill_v1 \
  --prefill-tokens 8192 \
  --decode-tokens 256 \
  --concurrency 24 \
  --region eu-west \
  --gpu-sku l40s \
  --kernel-selection auto \
  --dump-kernel-selection
```

Omar El-Sayed: thanks. also pls collect the scheduler batch size histograms for the “good” vs “bad” runs. if the auto-selection is using a max batch estimate derived from early batches, that would explain the flip.

Marcus Reed: i can pull those from the run artifacts and attach to the issue.

Mateo Alvarez: locking decision so we don’t churn: **pin kernel on L40S for baseline**, track bug separately, and run an auto-selection nightly for visibility (non-gating) until fixed.

Hiro Tanaka: agreed. :thumbsup:

Yuki Sato: :thumbsup: will update once issue is in and we have a branch.

Rina Kobayashi: i’ll land the harness override today + rerun the affected L40S cells overnight.

Marcus Reed: i can pull those from the run artifacts and attach to the issue.

Mateo Alvarez: locking decision so we don’t churn: **pin kernel on L40S for baseline**, track bug separately, and run an auto-selection nightly for visibility (non-gating) until fixed.

Hiro Tanaka: agreed. :thumbsup:

Yuki Sato: :thumbsup: will update once issue is in and we have a branch.

Rina Kobayashi: i’ll land the harness override today + rerun the affected L40S cells overnight.
text:
Yuki Sato: heads up from the baseline sweep today: on **L40S** we’re seeing attention kernel auto-select fall back to the slow path for a bunch of runs (qwen2.5-7b + long-prefill). TTFT p99 blows up vs H100/A100. Anyone else seeing this?

Marcus Reed: yep. it’s only on L40S in our bench fleet so far. the same manifest on A100 picks the fused kernel and looks fine.

Hiro Tanaka: do we have the kernel debug dump for one of the bad runs? (from the new `--dump-kernel-selection` flag)

Rina Kobayashi: pulled from the artifact bundle (run id `rw-baseline-2025-12-02_eu-west_l40s_qwen2.5-7b_longprefill_cold`). here’s the relevant snippet:
```text
[attn] device=L40S sm=89
[attn] requested=auto
[attn] candidate=flash_attn2: supported=true
[attn] candidate=fused_sdpa: supported=true
[attn] candidate=triton_sdpa: supported=true
[attn] select=triton_sdpa
[attn] reason=flash_attn2 rejected: "workspace bytes > limit" (limit=134217728)
[attn] reason=fused_sdpa rejected: "prefill_seq_len 8192 outside tuned regime"
```

Grace Kim: workspace > limit smells like we’re computing workspace based on max batch instead of realized? for these long-prefill runs we ramp concurrency but batch assembly is small-ish.

Omar El-Sayed: +1. also note long-prefill workload is heavy prefill, modest decode. if we’re pessimistically allocating for worst-case it’ll trip the cap.

Hiro Tanaka: for L40S we do have a tighter workspace cap vs A100/H100. but rejecting flash_attn2 entirely seems wrong. should degrade to a smaller tile config, not bail to triton.

Mateo Alvarez: what’s the impact on baseline deliverables? we can’t have “random kernel fallback” in the golden runs.

Marcus Reed: impact is material. quick compare from the tracker:
- H100: p99 TTFT ~ 1.8s
- L40S: p99 TTFT ~ 3.4s (when it picks triton)
variance is also worse.

Yuki Sato: and it’s not 100% deterministic. with same workload seed, different hosts sometimes pick flash_attn2 (passes cap) sometimes not. so it’s likely depending on batch shape / max_seq_len observed in the warmup window.

Hiro Tanaka: ok. for Q1 baseline we should pin the kernel so the measurement is attributable and repeatable.

Rina Kobayashi: we can do that in the harness via manifest override. today we have:
```json
kernel_selection_mode: auto
```
we can add something like `kernel_override: flash_attn2` (or whatever exact enum the runtime expects).

Grace Kim: what’s the safest “known good” on L40S for these models? flash_attn2?

Hiro Tanaka: yeah, flash_attn2 is what we want for this regime. fused_sdpa path is ok but we’ve seen cliffs around 8k depending on head_dim.

Mateo Alvarez: decision: **pin flash_attn2 on L40S for baseline runs** until auto-selection is fixed. but make sure we still *report* what would’ve been selected in auto (so we can track the bug).

Omar El-Sayed: can we do both? like run with override but also log the auto decision as “would_select”? or at least record the rejection reasons.

Hiro Tanaka: we can do “dry-run selection” logging. not in place yet but we can hack it for benchmark mode.

Yuki Sato: short-term proposal:
1) for baseline sweeps: set `RW_KERNEL_OVERRIDE=flash_attn2` in the bench job env **only for L40S**
2) keep `--dump-kernel-selection` enabled so we capture the rejection reasons when override is not set (we can schedule a nightly auto run separately)
3) open bug + fix in auto-selection: workspace limit calc + add a smaller workspace config for flash_attn2 on L40S

Rina Kobayashi: works for me. i’ll patch the workload runner to accept a per-SKU override in the run matrix, and write it into the manifest as:
- `kernel_selection_mode=override`
- `kernel_override=flash_attn2`
Also adding a big note in the run tracker so nobody compares L40S auto vs pinned apples-to-oranges.

Marcus Reed: +1 and for dashboards we should tag these runs `kernel=pinned` so we don’t mix with future auto.

Mateo Alvarez: :thumbsup: please do. and add an explicit “baseline exception” entry in the plan doc: L40S pinned kernel due to known auto-selection instability.

Grace Kim: can someone file the issue and assign? i can help test once there’s a patch.

Yuki Sato: filing now. will tag you + Hiro. ETA: initial fix PR by end of week if we can reproduce reliably on the L40S bench nodes.

Hiro Tanaka: cool. also let’s capture a minimal repro command for the issue.

Rina Kobayashi: repro from the harness (current head) that hits the fallback ~50%:
```bash
benchlab run \
  --model qwen2.5-7b-instruct \
  --workload long_prefill_v1 \
  --prefill-tokens 8192 \
  --decode-tokens 256 \
  --concurrency 24 \
  --region eu-west \
  --gpu-sku l40s \
  --kernel-selection auto \
  --dump-kernel-selection
```

Omar El-Sayed: thanks. also pls collect the scheduler batch size histograms for the “good” vs “bad” runs. if the auto-selection is using a max batch estimate derived from early batches, that would explain the flip.

Marcus Reed: i can pull those from the run artifacts and attach to the issue.

Mateo Alvarez: locking decision so we don’t churn: **pin kernel on L40S for baseline**, track bug separately, and run an auto-selection nightly for visibility (non-gating) until fixed.

Hiro Tanaka: agreed. :thumbsup:

Yuki Sato: :thumbsup: will update once issue is in and we have a branch.

Rina Kobayashi: i’ll land the harness override today + rerun the affected L40S cells overnight.

source.messages:
Benji Okafor: we need to expose metrics for hardware profile selection + overrides before we widen H100 rollout. request from SRE/oncall: “what profile did this node pick and why” (without sshing).

Allison Grant: +1. please align w our metrics naming conventions (prom-ish). also pls think about label cardinality. no per-customer labels.

Aisha Bello: dashboards team here. what we need for rollout views:
- share of nodes by selected profile (per pool/region)
- mismatch cases (unknown sku / fallback)
- override source distribution (fleet default vs helm values vs env var)
- tail latency segmented by selected profile (we’re adding panels in observability-pack)

Benji Okafor: proposal: runtime emits
1) `redwood_runtime_profile_selected` (gauge)
labels: `gpu_sku`, `profile_id`, `source`, `mode`
value: 1

2) `redwood_runtime_profile_fallback_total` (counter)
labels: `reason`, `gpu_sku`

3) `redwood_runtime_profile_incompatible_total` (counter)
labels: `profile_id`, `runtime_version`, `reason`

thoughts?

Kira Volkov: for perf-canary we mostly need stable series + easy filters. `profile_id` label is ok if bounded. just make sure profile ids are short + versioned in a controlled way.

Ethan Park: schema side we already have `id` and `version`. pls don’t put full semver in a label if we rotate frequently. maybe `profile_id` (e.g. `h100-balanced`) and a separate `profile_pack_version` label? pack version should be pretty low churn.

Allison Grant: careful: adding both `profile_id` + `profile_pack_version` multiplies series. maybe ok but please bound. also avoid `runtime_version` as a label on a counter (too many). prefer logging the runtime/profile compatibility matrix at startup, and for metrics keep runtime version out.

Noah Patel: agree w Allison. runtime_version label will explode across canaries / backports. for incompatibility we can label `reason` and `profile_id` and have runtime version in logs.

Benji Okafor: ok. revise:
- `redwood_runtime_profile_selected_info` gauge 1
labels: `gpu_sku`, `profile_id`, `profile_pack_version`, `source`, `deployment_mode`

- `redwood_runtime_profile_fallback_total` counter
labels: `gpu_sku`, `reason`

- `redwood_runtime_profile_override_applied_total` counter
labels: `key`, `source`
(so we can see if ppl are overriding knobs a lot)

Aisha Bello: naming: we generally use `_info` for these “identity” gauges. +1 on `*_selected_info`.

Allison Grant: `deployment_mode` label is fine if it’s one of {hosted,dedicated,private}. for hosted, do we even have “nodes” for metrics? (we do per pool). ok.

Sofia Ivanova: can we add a metric for the scheduler preset? it’s the thing that bites tail latency. profile_id alone might not tell us if we’re in throughput-first vs balanced.

Benji Okafor: scheduler preset is encoded in the profile id today (`h100-balanced`, `h100-throughput`). but if someone overrides scheduler knobs via env var we should see that.

Marcus Reed: override visibility is key. but please don’t emit 1 metric per overridden key per request. do it once at startup (or config reload) so the counters increment once.

Benji Okafor: yup, plan is:
- at runtime init, after final config resolution, emit `selected_info` gauge
- increment `override_applied_total{key=...,source=...}` for each non-default override encountered during merge
- increment fallback counters on selection failure

Allison Grant: counter “increment once per node startup” is ok. but `key` label needs to be controlled list. pls don’t put arbitrary config path strings.

Ethan Park: we can expose override keys as an enum list from schema: e.g. `scheduler.mode`, `scheduler.max_batch_tokens`, `kv_cache.budget_gb`, `kernel.autoselect.enabled` etc.

Kira Volkov: perf-canary wants a direct signal for “effective batching knob values”. any chance we expose a few key numeric gauges? ex:
- `redwood_runtime_scheduler_max_batch_tokens`
- `redwood_runtime_kv_cache_budget_bytes`
then canary dashboards can assert within bounds.

Allison Grant: numeric gauges are ok but be careful about churn. pick a very small set of “tier-1 knobs”. also add units suffix (`_bytes`) and keep consistent.

Omar El-Sayed: kv cache budget is a big one esp L40S. having `_kv_cache_budget_bytes` would help correlate OOM vs config.

Benji Okafor: ok add 3 numeric gauges:
- `redwood_runtime_scheduler_max_batch_tokens` (gauge)
- `redwood_runtime_scheduler_queue_limit` (gauge)
- `redwood_runtime_kv_cache_budget_bytes` (gauge)

and maybe `redwood_runtime_kernel_autoselect_enabled` (gauge 0/1)

Aisha Bello: for dashboards, we’d rather use `*_info` + the numeric gauges. `kernel_autoselect_enabled` as gauge is fine.

Sean Gallagher: SRE ask: include “override source” categories that match precedence docs. we’ll need to know when a pool is pinned via fleet default vs someone set env var on a canary.

Benji Okafor: sources I have from loader:
- `compiled_default`
- `profile_pack_default`
- `fleet_default`
- `pool_override`
- `deployment_override` (helm values)
- `env_override`
- `cli_override` (debug)

for `selected_info.source` it’d be the highest precedence thing that selected/overrode profile id. for `override_applied_total.source` it’s per key.

Allison Grant: pls rename to simpler, consistent names. ex `default`, `fleet`, `pool`, `deployment`, `env`, `cli`. and document mapping in code comment.

Noah Patel: +1. we don’t want to teach oncall 7 categories. also “compiled_default” vs “profile_pack_default” is confusing.

Benji Okafor: ok:
- `source=default` means pack default for that sku
- `source=fleet`
- `source=pool`
- `source=deployment`
- `source=env`
- `source=cli`

Aisha Bello: for `profile_pack_version`, format? if it’s `2025.12.0` it’s fine. but don’t put git sha.

Ethan Park: pack version is semver, e.g. `1.3.0`. we can keep that.

Kira Volkov: last ask: expose a “detection result” label? we had issues w SKU string variants.

Benji Okafor: maybe a counter for detection normalization:
`redwood_runtime_gpu_sku_detected_total{raw=..., normalized=...}` would be cardinality explosion. better: log raw sku in startup logs + emit metric only for normalized.

Allison Grant: yes, no raw label. if you need signal, add `redwood_runtime_gpu_sku_unknown_total{provider=...}` with a tiny provider label set.

Benji Okafor: we have provider = {aws, gcp, azure, baremetal, unknown}. will add:
`redwood_runtime_gpu_sku_unknown_total{provider=...}` counter

Sean Gallagher: this covers oncall. also please make sure metrics include existing node labels (pool/region) via our standard exporter (so we can filter in grafana).

Aisha Bello: once merged, ping me w exact metric names + label keys. we’ll wire panels:
- % nodes by `profile_id`
- fallback/incompat counters
- kv_cache_budget distribution per sku

Benji Okafor: will do. I’ll drop a snippet here for review before PR.

Benji Okafor: draft snippet:
```
# identity
redwood_runtime_profile_selected_info{gpu_sku="h100",profile_id="h100-balanced",profile_pack_version="1.3.0",source="fleet",deployment_mode="hosted"} 1

# knobs
redwood_runtime_scheduler_max_batch_tokens 8192
redwood_runtime_scheduler_queue_limit 256
redwood_runtime_kv_cache_budget_bytes 51539607552
redwood_runtime_kernel_autoselect_enabled 1

# events
redwood_runtime_profile_fallback_total{gpu_sku="b200",reason="profile_disabled"} 3
redwood_runtime_profile_override_applied_total{key="kv_cache.budget_bytes",source="deployment"} 1
redwood_runtime_gpu_sku_unknown_total{provider="baremetal"} 2
```

Allison Grant: looks good. nit: `kv_cache.budget_bytes` as key value ok if it’s in a fixed allowlist. also prefer consistent key naming: `kv_cache.budget_bytes` vs `kv_cache_budget_bytes`. (either is fine but pick one for the key namespace.)

Ethan Park: allowlist is doable in schema validator. we’ll reject unknown override keys from being counted (or bucket to `key="other"`).

Kira Volkov: +1 bucket unknown to `other` so metrics don’t break if someone adds a knob.

Aisha Bello: ship it. and pls add a short section in the runbook “metrics to check” w these names.

Benji Okafor: ack. I’ll implement in the runtime metrics registry + update docs. will ping when PR is up.

Sean Gallagher: thanks. this unblocks the rollout checklist item for “profile visibility”.
