- `redwood_runtime_scheduler_max_batch_tokens` (gauge)
- `redwood_runtime_scheduler_queue_limit` (gauge)
- `redwood_runtime_kv_cache_budget_bytes` (gauge)

and maybe `redwood_runtime_kernel_autoselect_enabled` (gauge 0/1)

Aisha Bello: for dashboards, we’d rather use `*_info` + the numeric gauges. `kernel_autoselect_enabled` as gauge is fine.

Sean Gallagher: SRE ask: include “override source” categories that match precedence docs. we’ll need to know when a pool is pinned via fleet default vs someone set env var on a canary.

Benji Okafor: sources I have from loader:
- `compiled_default`
- `profile_pack_default`
- `fleet_default`
- `pool_override`
- `deployment_override` (helm values)
- `env_override`
- `cli_override` (debug)

for `selected_info.source` it’d be the highest precedence thing that selected/overrode profile id. for `override_applied_total.source` it’s per key.

Allison Grant: pls rename to simpler, consistent names. ex `default`, `fleet`, `pool`, `deployment`, `env`, `cli`. and document mapping in code comment.

Noah Patel: +1. we don’t want to teach oncall 7 categories. also “compiled_default” vs “profile_pack_default” is confusing.

Benji Okafor: ok:
- `source=default` means pack default for that sku
- `source=fleet`
- `source=pool`
- `source=deployment`
- `source=env`
- `source=cli`

Aisha Bello: for `profile_pack_version`, format? if it’s `2025.12.0` it’s fine. but don’t put git sha.

Ethan Park: pack version is semver, e.g. `1.3.0`. we can keep that.

Kira Volkov: last ask: expose a “detection result” label? we had issues w SKU string variants.

Benji Okafor: maybe a counter for detection normalization:
`redwood_runtime_gpu_sku_detected_total{raw=..., normalized=...}` would be cardinality explosion. better: log raw sku in startup logs + emit metric only for normalized.

Allison Grant: yes, no raw label. if you need signal, add `redwood_runtime_gpu_sku_unknown_total{provider=...}` with a tiny provider label set.

Benji Okafor: we have provider = {aws, gcp, azure, baremetal, unknown}. will add:
`redwood_runtime_gpu_sku_unknown_total{provider=...}` counter

Sean Gallagher: this covers oncall. also please make sure metrics include existing node labels (pool/region) via our standard exporter (so we can filter in grafana).

Aisha Bello: once merged, ping me w exact metric names + label keys. we’ll wire panels:
- % nodes by `profile_id`
- fallback/incompat counters
- kv_cache_budget distribution per sku

Benji Okafor: will do. I’ll drop a snippet here for review before PR.

Benji Okafor: draft snippet:
```
# identity
redwood_runtime_profile_selected_info{gpu_sku="h100",profile_id="h100-balanced",profile_pack_version="1.3.0",source="fleet",deployment_mode="hosted"} 1

# knobs
redwood_runtime_scheduler_max_batch_tokens 8192
redwood_runtime_scheduler_queue_limit 256
redwood_runtime_kv_cache_budget_bytes 51539607552
redwood_runtime_kernel_autoselect_enabled 1

# events
redwood_runtime_profile_fallback_total{gpu_sku="b200",reason="profile_disabled"} 3
redwood_runtime_profile_override_applied_total{key="kv_cache.budget_bytes",source="deployment"} 1
redwood_runtime_gpu_sku_unknown_total{provider="baremetal"} 2
```

Allison Grant: looks good. nit: `kv_cache.budget_bytes` as key value ok if it’s in a fixed allowlist. also prefer consistent key naming: `kv_cache.budget_bytes` vs `kv_cache_budget_bytes`. (either is fine but pick one for the key namespace.)

Ethan Park: allowlist is doable in schema validator. we’ll reject unknown override keys from being counted (or bucket to `key="other"`).

Kira Volkov: +1 bucket unknown to `other` so metrics don’t break if someone adds a knob.

Aisha Bello: ship it. and pls add a short section in the runbook “metrics to check” w these names.

Benji Okafor: ack. I’ll implement in the runtime metrics registry + update docs. will ping when PR is up.

Sean Gallagher: thanks. this unblocks the rollout checklist item for “profile visibility”.

source.messages:
Noah Patel: seeing a weird regression only under `quant_profile=q4_kvpack_v2` + long-context prompts. It *does not* reproduce on fp16 or `q8_dynamic`.

Noah Patel: symptoms: output goes off-rails after ~14-18k tokens (repeats / drops tool args), but only when we have KV cache enabled and we hit a few decode steps with batch>1.

Sofia Ivanova: is this on continuous batching path or single request?

Noah Patel: continuous batching. single-request decode (batch=1) looks fine even at 24k.

Grace Kim: which GPU SKU? A100 vs H100 can differ for q4 kernels.

Noah Patel: H100 SXM. repro not observed on A100 yet (haven’t tried hard).

Arjun Desai: `q4_kvpack_v2` is the new profile we wired for H100 in pr-19558, right? we changed baseline selection + some kernel prefs.

Omar El-Sayed: “KV pack v2” also changes how we lay out the KV blocks (and page size). long context is where it gets spicy.

Noah Patel: yep. here’s a minimal repro from staging:
```
route: /v1/chat/completions
model: redwood/llama-3.1-70b-instruct
context: 22k (synthetic doc + Q&A)
settings:
  quant_profile: q4_kvpack_v2
  kv_cache: on
  max_batch_tokens: 8192
  max_concurrent_seqs: 16

observed:
  - at ~15.6k, assistant starts repeating a 3-token loop
  - tool call JSON becomes invalid (missing closing brace)
```

Sofia Ivanova: can you capture the scheduler state around when it flips? like, do we start mixing long seq with short seq in same batch?

Noah Patel: good call. looks like a short request joins mid-decode. then we see the long seq degrade.

Grace Kim: that smells like a kernel using wrong stride assumptions when seq lengths diverge (esp with packed KV). do we have logs for which attention kernel is selected?

Noah Patel: grabbed trace spans. kernel tag switches right before failure:
```
attn_kernel=flash_attn_v3_kvpack
kv_format=packed_v2
page_size=128
```
then we see `nan_guard=triggered` once (but doesn’t abort), and next 20 tokens are garbage.

Mateo Alvarez: we should treat any `nan_guard` in decode as “hard fail” for gating purposes tbh. but for now: can we reproduce with `nan_guard=abort` to pinpoint?

Noah Patel: yep I can flip that and rerun.

Omar El-Sayed: also check if this happens when `prefix_cache` is enabled. long contexts + prefix reuse might stress the same codepaths.

Noah Patel: prefix cache off in this repro.

Arjun Desai: I’m worried our gating prompt sets don’t include “long context + mid-flight batch join” cases. the smoke gates are tiny and mostly <4k.

Selene Huang: +1. for quant gating we have some 8k docs but I don’t think we have >16k. if the regression is at 15k+ we’ll never catch it.

Jonas Weber: current quant suite coverage (for llama3.1-70b) tops out at 8k due to runtime constraints. we can add a *single* long-context canary suite though (small sample size) and run it only for quant profile diffs.

Grace Kim: if we add exactly one “hard case” prompt set with 20k context, we should ensure it’s not flaky. use deterministic prompt + fixed seed.

Diego Ramos: do we have online signal for long context? any canary metrics show increased “invalid_tool_call_json” or “repeat_rate” for this profile?

Noah Patel: looked at a couple internal canary routes. nothing obvious because we aren’t using `q4_kvpack_v2` on long-context heavy customers yet. (this is why gating needs coverage…)

optimize-bot: FYI: Optimize rec `rec_8d1f` proposed switching `quant_profile: q8_dynamic -> q4_kvpack_v2` on 3 routes (est. -23% $/1k tok). Quality gate status: PASSED (fast gates), full gates: SKIPPED (policy: quant-fast-only).

Mateo Alvarez: oof. that policy is now clearly insufficient for long context. we should update: quant changes require full gates OR at least include a long-context suite even in “fast.”

Sofia Ivanova: can we quickly confirm root cause is kvpack kernel? try forcing kernel fallback to non-packed format but keep q4 weights.

Grace Kim: yep: set `kv_format=standard` while keeping weights quantized, if supported. if regression disappears, it’s kvpack path.

Noah Patel: will try:
- run A: q4_kvpack_v2 + force `attn_kernel=flash_attn_v3` (no kvpack)
- run B: q4_kvpack_v2 + `nan_guard=abort`
- run C: q4_kvpack_v2 but disable continuous batching (single stream)

Arjun Desai: and please log: `kv_page_idx`, `block_table` sizes. if we’re overflowing an index at long seq, that would explain a sudden flip.

Omar El-Sayed: + check page size 128 vs 256. we had a known issue last quarter where page_size=128 overflowed for 16k+ on one path.

Noah Patel: (reaction :eyes: from Noah Patel)

Selene Huang: proposal for coverage (gating):
- add prompt set `longctx_tool_json_v1` (20k context doc + forced tool call w/ JSON args)
- metric: tool JSON validity + exact-match on key fields (not full string)
- run only for quant profile diffs + only on llama3.1 70b/405b families
- sample size: 20 prompts, deterministic

Jonas Weber: +1. we can keep cost bounded by:
- only 1 model variant
- max_new_tokens=256
- run on H100 only (since the bug is H100-specific)

Grace Kim: we should also add a “repeat loop detector” metric; the behavior described is classic repetition. even a cheap n-gram repeat rate threshold works.

Diego Ramos: I can wire that metric into online canary too (repeat_rate spike alert) once we roll this profile anywhere.

Mateo Alvarez: action items:
1) Noah: run the A/B/C experiments and post results here.
2) Grace/Omar: inspect kvpack kernel assumptions for mixed-length batching. prioritize a patch if A confirms.
3) Jonas/Selene: create the `longctx_tool_json_v1` suite + propose thresholds for gating v1.
4) Arjun: update quant profile gating policy: quant diffs must include long-context suite on H100.

Noah Patel: results:
- run A (forced non-kvpack kernel): ✅ no regression at 22k, tool JSON valid.
- run B (nan_guard=abort): aborts at step where we previously saw the first bad token; logs show NaN in attention output.
- run C (no continuous batching): ✅ no regression.
So it’s kvpack + mixed-length batching.

Grace Kim: that’s pretty definitive. I’ll open a runtime bug + patch path: likely need to gate kvpack kernel selection when batch has high variance in seq len OR fix stride calc.

Omar El-Sayed: I’ll check the kv block table update when a new seq joins mid-decode. might be writing past bounds for the long seq.

Arjun Desai: thanks. I’m going to ping product/Optimize folks too because `fast-only` quant gating is dangerous here. we should at least require the longctx suite for any profile flip to `q4_kvpack_v2`.

Selene Huang: once suite exists, we can set it as “required for q4_kvpack_v2 on H100” and not for other profiles to keep costs bounded.

Jonas Weber: I’ll draft the prompt set today and share ID + expected outputs. we can start with 10 prompts in internal beta and expand after we confirm non-flaky.

Mateo Alvarez: great. also, we should add a static pre-gate validation: if `quant_profile=q4_kvpack_v2` and `continuous_batching=on`, require `kvpack_safe_mixed_lengths=true` (or force fallback). defense-in-depth.

Noah Patel: (reaction :thumbsup: from Noah Patel) I’ll file the linear ticket + link it once created.

source.messages:
Rina Kobayashi: First full sweep is in ✅ (top models x {us-east, eu-west} x {H100, A100, L40S} where supported). This is the "we can run end-to-end" milestone, not a final baseline freeze.

High-level takeaways (numbers are *directional*, see tracker for exact):
• Chat streaming cohort (short prompt, variable decode): H100 is stable-ish on p50, but p99 is still noisy. Typical p50 TTFT ~150–220ms; p99 TTFT ~550–900ms depending on model + region. TPOT p50 is in the ~12–20ms/token band; p99 is what swings.
• Long-context prefill cohort (8–16k prefill, small decode): most of the pain is TTFT p99, and the gap between H100 and A100 is larger than we expected on a couple configs. Prefill-heavy runs are the most sensitive to placement + backpressure.
• Embeddings high-QPS cohort: throughput scales nicely, but we’re seeing occasional tail spikes that correlate with background GC / host contention (more below).

Biggest variance drivers we saw across the sweep:
1) GPU clocks not pinned (esp A100 pool): p99 TTFT moves 8–15% run-to-run when clocks drift. SRE suggestion to lock clocks helps (see Sean’s notes in tracker).
2) Container/host placement: runs landing on certain hosts show worse prefill tail; looks like noisy neighbor / NUMA-ish. We tagged affected nodes in the tracker.
3) Warmup + cache state: cold vs warm is a real delta. For chat, warmup primarily reduces TTFT variance; for long-prefill it changes both mean and tail if KV cache isn’t reset consistently.
4) Kernel selection fallback on one SKU: a subset of L40S runs fall back to the conservative kernel in the long-prefill regime → p99 TTFT cliff. We pinned for sweep B, but we need to fix auto-select.
5) Streaming measurement boundary: a few early runs were accidentally measuring client read bottlenecks (TPOT looked “bad” but server-side was fine). We’ve corrected harness settings now.

Tracker + links:
• Drive tracker (single source of truth): https://drive.google.com/drive/u/0/folders/ENGINEERING_SERVING_RUNTIME_Q1_2026_BASELINE (tab: "Full sweep v0")
• Noise scratchpad (hypotheses + quick experiments): https://drive.google.com/drive/u/0/folders/ENGINEERING_SERVING_RUNTIME_NOISE_SCRATCHPAD

Next actions I’m proposing:
A) Re-run a minimal subset (2 models x 2 regions x 2 SKUs) with (1) clock lock + (2) strict host allowlist + (3) standardized warmup to quantify variance reduction.
B) Long-prefill: add explicit scheduler/backpressure counters to every run artifact (queue wait, batch-assembly time, KV evictions) so we can stop guessing.
C) L40S kernel fallback: Yuki/Hiro to confirm the selection regime + what we should pin for the baseline anchor.

If anyone wants a specific cut of the data (per model family, per region), tell me what question you’re trying to answer and I’ll pull it.

Mateo Alvarez: This is great. Thanks Rina. The p99 spread lines up with what we’ve been feeling anecdotally.

For A): can we include one long-prefill run in that subset? That’s where decisions are going to get contentious.

Sofia Ivanova: +1 on making scheduler counters mandatory in artifacts. Without queue wait + batch assembly we can’t attribute tail.

Also re: cold vs warm, we should probably report both for baseline (cold-start and warm-steady). Otherwise we’re going to fight about what “real” is every time.

Sean Gallagher: On clocks: A100 pool is currently mixed policies. We can do `nvidia-smi -lgc` in the benchmark job prologue but we need to make sure we’re not violating any shared fleet constraints.

If we want isolation, I can give you an allowlist of the "quiet" hosts + set up a dedicated node label for the next week’s runs. That should remove a big chunk of the noise.

Sana Farid: The 8–15% p99 swing is exactly why we need the noise model calibrated *before* we set any gating thresholds.

If we can get A) done with/without clock lock + host allowlist, I can fit a simple variance model per cohort and propose initial “regression vs noise” bands (likely asymmetric for p99).

Claire Dubois: Quick methodology note: please keep the sample size consistent when we do the A) comparison. Right now some of the long-prefill runs have fewer reps because they’re expensive.

If we under-sample, we’ll conclude “variance improved” when really we just got lucky.

Hiro Tanaka: Re kernel fallback: confirmed on L40S long-prefill > ~8k prefill we’re hitting the fallback path more often than expected.

Root cause looks like an overly conservative guard in the selector (sequence-length regime threshold) + driver/CUDA minor version mismatch in eu-west pool.

Short term: pin kernel K2 for baseline runs on L40S (I’ll drop the exact flag in the tracker). Medium term: fix the selector + add a debug dump so we can see selection per run.

Yuki Sato: I’ll put together a tiny “selector sanity” job that prints the chosen kernel across the length bins so we can catch this automatically.

Also +1 on pinning for the baseline anchor; we shouldn’t freeze until we can explain the fallback.

Omar El-Sayed: For long-prefill tail: I looked at a couple traces and the worst cases correlate with KV cache pressure + evictions. We need to tag “KV evictions/sec” + “cache residency” in the run artifacts.

Otherwise p99 TTFT looks like "scheduler" but it’s actually memory pressure.

Sahana Iyer: FYI the harness change that removed client-read bottleneck is now in main. If anyone reruns locally, make sure you’re on the latest benchmark-lab commit; older versions will over-report TPOT in streaming.

Jonas Weber: From the Applied ML side: thanks for calling out “directional.” As soon as you have the rerun subset, I want to validate that the warmup procedure isn’t inadvertently changing decoding settings (we’ve been bitten by “perf knobs” changing behavior).

Not asking for a full eval, just the lightweight must-not-break spotcheck.

Sergio Costa: When you all agree on the “subset rerun” list, ping me and I’ll start shaping the dashboard view around those cohorts so we can compare apples-to-apples (with links dashboard → manifest → trace).

Rina Kobayashi: Yep, we’ll include one long-prefill in A). Proposed subset:
• Chat streaming: Llama-3.x 70B + Qwen2.5 32B
• Long-prefill: Llama-3.x 70B
Across us-east/eu-west and H100/A100 (plus L40S only for the kernel pin validation run).

I’ll update the tracker with an "A) rerun plan" tab + the exact run IDs today. Sean I’ll DM you about the host allowlist + clock lock constraints.

(Also: I added a note in tracker where we think we accidentally hit client bottleneck in the early streaming runs so nobody uses those for baseline numbers.)

source.messages:
Rina Kobayashi: heads up, 2 perf-suite PRs need runtime/SRE eyes today so we can keep the baseline sweep on track.

1) benchmark-lab PR #21432 (run manifest schema + validator)
2) redwood PR #21503 (scheduler queue time + batch assembly metrics)

links:
- https://github.com/redwood-inference/benchmark-lab/pull/21432
- https://github.com/redwood-inference/redwood/pull/21503

review focus:
- schema versioning/backwards compat (we have a bunch of old manifests in the Drive tracker already)
- metric names/tags + overhead guardrails (this is intended for perf runs but some codepath is shared)

CI is currently noisy on both; please comment w/ requested changes and I’ll batch fixes + rerun.

Marcus Reed: looking at #21432 now. first nit: `manifest_version` vs `schema_version` naming is inconsistent between README and examples. I’d prefer `schema_version` everywhere (and explicitly semver-ish, like `1.0`). also: examples include `git.sha` but schema calls it `git_commit`.

Rina Kobayashi: +1, good catch. i’ll align naming. do we want `schema_version: "1"` or `"1.0"`?

Marcus Reed: I think `"1"` is fine if we commit to only breaking changes bumping major. but then be explicit in ADR. also need `validator` to accept unknown fields (we will add stuff like `kernel_selection_dump_id` later).

Sahana Iyer: re CI: #21432 is failing on `python -m jsonschema` step because one example manifest has trailing commas. that’s blocking main, pls fix + rerun. also the lint job is failing due to `black` not applied to `tools/manifest_validator.py`.

Rina Kobayashi: oof. ok, will fix formatting + rerun once i push.

Sofia Ivanova: reviewing #21503. two things:
1) metric names: `scheduler.queue_time_ms` vs `scheduler.queue_wait_ms` — prefer “wait” since queue time could include other stuff.
2) tags: please include `workload_id` and `route` (or `endpoint`) so we can slice perf-suite dashboards the same way as prod.

Hiro Tanaka: +1 on tags. also for perf attribution we should tag `seq_len_regime` if available (short/med/long) OR at least `prefill_tokens_bucket`. not sure if that’s in scope.

Sofia Ivanova: we don’t have token buckets in scheduler layer cleanly. but we *do* have `request.prefill_tokens` at enqueue time. we could bucket it there with very low overhead.

Sean Gallagher: SRE angle on #21503: please ensure these metrics are behind the perf flag OR sampled. we can’t add high-cardinality tags in prod by accident.

Sean Gallagher: specifically `request_id` / correlation id must not be a tag in statsd/prom, only in logs/traces. I saw `correlation_id` in the diff, pls confirm it’s log-only.

Rina Kobayashi: for #21503 i think Omar added correlation_id to the scheduler debug log line, not metric tags. but will confirm w/ Sofia.

Allison Grant: on metric naming/tags: can we align with the metrics dictionary draft? i.e.
- `rw.scheduler.queue_wait_ms` (unit in name optional but consistent)
- `rw.scheduler.batch_assembly_ms`
- `rw.scheduler.batch_size` histogram
and tags: `model`, `model_family`, `gpu_sku`, `region`, `workload`, `cache_state` (cold/warm) where applicable.

Allison Grant: I’m ok if not all tags are in scheduler code, but at least `model` + `workload` are needed for the baseline dashboards spec.

Connor O'Brien: skimmed #21432: big +1 on allowing unknown fields.
also please add a note in validator: “strict mode” for CI vs “compat mode” for local reruns.
reason: we’ll have old artifacts we still need to parse for comparisons.

Marcus Reed: another #21432 comment: env capture needs to include driver + CUDA runtime version. schema has `cuda_version` but not `nvidia_driver_version`. we keep chasing variance and then realize fleet nodes drifted.

Rina Kobayashi: yep, we can add `nvidia_driver_version` and `container_image_digest` (for the harness image). ok if optional?

Sean Gallagher: optional is fine, but for baseline sweeps we should mark them “required in practice”. maybe add `required_for_tier: baseline` doc note rather than schema hard requirement.

Sahana Iyer: on CI reruns: if you push fixes to #21432, please rebase first. there’s a merge commit in the branch and it’s confusing the “changed files” view. also the schema tests cache badly; use “Re-run all jobs” after rebase.

Rina Kobayashi: got it. i’ll clean up history.

ci-bot: CI for benchmark-lab#21432 failed: `schema_examples` (invalid JSON), `lint` (black formatting), `unit_tests` (1 failed)

Rina Kobayashi: pushed fix commit to #21432:
- removed trailing commas in examples
- ran black
- renamed to `schema_version`
- made validator default to compat (allows unknown fields), `--strict` to fail on unknown
- added optional `nvidia_driver_version` + `container_image_digest`

re-running CI now.

Sofia Ivanova: confirmed on #21503: correlation_id is log-only, not a metric label.
I’ll update metric names to `queue_wait_ms` + add `workload` tag plumbed from request context (already present for perf runs).

Hiro Tanaka: for sequence length: can we add a simple bucket label just for perf mode? something like `prefill_bucket={0-256,256-1k,1k-4k,4k+}`.

Sofia Ivanova: ok for perf mode only. I’ll guard it behind `RW_PERF_METRICS=1` and default off.

Allison Grant: please document the guard in the PR description + add a unit test that perf-mode labels are absent when flag is off.

Connor O'Brien: for release gating: in #21432 add a stable field for `runtime_build_id` or `runtime_git_sha` and `harness_git_sha` separately. we need to know which repo changed for regression triage.

Rina Kobayashi: yep. added `repos.runtime.git_sha` and `repos.benchmark_lab.git_sha` in examples; schema uses `repos.*` object though—wait, we can’t do nested JSON per our output format but schema file can have nested. sorry, mixing contexts.

Marcus Reed: schema can be nested, that’s fine. (our doc store constraint is only for these generated docs). in manifests, nested is good.

Rina Kobayashi: cool. i’ll add those fields + keep old `git_sha` accepted for compat.

ci-bot: CI for benchmark-lab#21432: `schema_examples` passed, `lint` passed, `unit_tests` passed. All checks successful.

Rina Kobayashi: #21432 green now. thanks all.

Sofia Ivanova: pushed updates to #21503:
- renamed metrics to `rw.scheduler.queue_wait_ms` + `rw.scheduler.batch_assembly_ms`
- added `workload` tag (perf context only)
- added optional `prefill_bucket` label behind `RW_PERF_METRICS=1`
- clarified in PR desc that correlation_id is log-only

CI is running; Sean/Allison can you re-check the tag/flag bits?

Sean Gallagher: reviewed latest diff, looks good. perf flag default off + no high-cardinality tags. :thumbsup:

Allison Grant: +1. one last nit: please add units in help text / comments since prom scrapes don’t encode units. otherwise good.

Sahana Iyer: CI note: #21503 failed once due to flaky integration test (gpu runner timed out). I re-ran the failed job and it passed. no code change needed.

Rina Kobayashi: awesome. marking thread resolved once #21503 merges. thanks everyone.

and tags: `model`, `model_family`, `gpu_sku`, `region`, `workload`, `cache_state` (cold/warm) where applicable.

Allison Grant: I’m ok if not all tags are in scheduler code, but at least `model` + `workload` are needed for the baseline dashboards spec.

Connor O'Brien: skimmed #21432: big +1 on allowing unknown fields.
also please add a note in validator: “strict mode” for CI vs “compat mode” for local reruns.
reason: we’ll have old artifacts we still need to parse for comparisons.

Marcus Reed: another #21432 comment: env capture needs to include driver + CUDA runtime version. schema has `cuda_version` but not `nvidia_driver_version`. we keep chasing variance and then realize fleet nodes drifted.

Rina Kobayashi: yep, we can add `nvidia_driver_version` and `container_image_digest` (for the harness image). ok if optional?

Sean Gallagher: optional is fine, but for baseline sweeps we should mark them “required in practice”. maybe add `required_for_tier: baseline` doc note rather than schema hard requirement.

Sahana Iyer: on CI reruns: if you push fixes to #21432, please rebase first. there’s a merge commit in the branch and it’s confusing the “changed files” view. also the schema tests cache badly; use “Re-run all jobs” after rebase.

Rina Kobayashi: got it. i’ll clean up history.

ci-bot: CI for benchmark-lab#21432 failed: `schema_examples` (invalid JSON), `lint` (black formatting), `unit_tests` (1 failed)

Rina Kobayashi: pushed fix commit to #21432:
- removed trailing commas in examples
- ran black
- renamed to `schema_version`
- made validator default to compat (allows unknown fields), `--strict` to fail on unknown
- added optional `nvidia_driver_version` + `container_image_digest`

re-running CI now.

Sofia Ivanova: confirmed on #21503: correlation_id is log-only, not a metric label.
I’ll update metric names to `queue_wait_ms` + add `workload` tag plumbed from request context (already present for perf runs).

Hiro Tanaka: for sequence length: can we add a simple bucket label just for perf mode? something like `prefill_bucket={0-256,256-1k,1k-4k,4k+}`.

Sofia Ivanova: ok for perf mode only. I’ll guard it behind `RW_PERF_METRICS=1` and default off.

Allison Grant: please document the guard in the PR description + add a unit test that perf-mode labels are absent when flag is off.

Connor O'Brien: for release gating: in #21432 add a stable field for `runtime_build_id` or `runtime_git_sha` and `harness_git_sha` separately. we need to know which repo changed for regression triage.

- added `workload` tag (perf context only)
- added optional `prefill_bucket` label behind `RW_PERF_METRICS=1`
- clarified in PR desc that correlation_id is log-only

CI is running; Sean/Allison can you re-check the tag/flag bits?

Sean Gallagher: reviewed latest diff, looks good. perf flag default off + no high-cardinality tags. :thumbsup:

Allison Grant: +1. one last nit: please add units in help text / comments since prom scrapes don’t encode units. otherwise good.

text:
Rina Kobayashi: heads up, 2 perf-suite PRs need runtime/SRE eyes today so we can keep the baseline sweep on track.

1) benchmark-lab PR #21432 (run manifest schema + validator)
2) redwood PR #21503 (scheduler queue time + batch assembly metrics)

links:
- https://github.com/redwood-inference/benchmark-lab/pull/21432
- https://github.com/redwood-inference/redwood/pull/21503

review focus:
- schema versioning/backwards compat (we have a bunch of old manifests in the Drive tracker already)
- metric names/tags + overhead guardrails (this is intended for perf runs but some codepath is shared)

CI is currently noisy on both; please comment w/ requested changes and I’ll batch fixes + rerun.

Marcus Reed: looking at #21432 now. first nit: `manifest_version` vs `schema_version` naming is inconsistent between README and examples. I’d prefer `schema_version` everywhere (and explicitly semver-ish, like `1.0`). also: examples include `git.sha` but schema calls it `git_commit`.

Rina Kobayashi: +1, good catch. i’ll align naming. do we want `schema_version: "1"` or `"1.0"`?

Marcus Reed: I think `"1"` is fine if we commit to only breaking changes bumping major. but then be explicit in ADR. also need `validator` to accept unknown fields (we will add stuff like `kernel_selection_dump_id` later).

Sahana Iyer: re CI: #21432 is failing on `python -m jsonschema` step because one example manifest has trailing commas. that’s blocking main, pls fix + rerun. also the lint job is failing due to `black` not applied to `tools/manifest_validator.py`.

Rina Kobayashi: oof. ok, will fix formatting + rerun once i push.

Sofia Ivanova: reviewing #21503. two things:
1) metric names: `scheduler.queue_time_ms` vs `scheduler.queue_wait_ms` — prefer “wait” since queue time could include other stuff.
2) tags: please include `workload_id` and `route` (or `endpoint`) so we can slice perf-suite dashboards the same way as prod.

Hiro Tanaka: +1 on tags. also for perf attribution we should tag `seq_len_regime` if available (short/med/long) OR at least `prefill_tokens_bucket`. not sure if that’s in scope.

Sofia Ivanova: we don’t have token buckets in scheduler layer cleanly. but we *do* have `request.prefill_tokens` at enqueue time. we could bucket it there with very low overhead.

Sean Gallagher: SRE angle on #21503: please ensure these metrics are behind the perf flag OR sampled. we can’t add high-cardinality tags in prod by accident.

Sean Gallagher: specifically `request_id` / correlation id must not be a tag in statsd/prom, only in logs/traces. I saw `correlation_id` in the diff, pls confirm it’s log-only.

Rina Kobayashi: for #21503 i think Omar added correlation_id to the scheduler debug log line, not metric tags. but will confirm w/ Sofia.

Allison Grant: on metric naming/tags: can we align with the metrics dictionary draft? i.e.
- `rw.scheduler.queue_wait_ms` (unit in name optional but consistent)
- `rw.scheduler.batch_assembly_ms`
- `rw.scheduler.batch_size` histogram
and tags: `model`, `model_family`, `gpu_sku`, `region`, `workload`, `cache_state` (cold/warm) where applicable.

Allison Grant: I’m ok if not all tags are in scheduler code, but at least `model` + `workload` are needed for the baseline dashboards spec.

source.messages:
Claire Dubois: flagging a disagreement from yesterday’s sync: do we include prefix/KV caching in the Q1 baseline numbers or force everything “cold”? I’m worried we’ll bake in a config-dependent speedup that’s not comparable across runs.

Rina Kobayashi: +1. from harness POV, “cache on” is ambiguous unless we define *exactly* what’s warmed (prefix cache vs per-session KV), hit rate target, and how long state persists. otherwise repro is hard.

Mateo Alvarez: but if we ship a baseline that’s always cold, it’s not representative for the dominant prod cohorts (multi-turn chat). folks will optimize the wrong thing.

Omar El-Sayed: it’s also two different mechanisms:
- prefix cache (shared / cross-request) affects prefill
- KV cache (per session) affects continuation across turns
We should not treat them as one checkbox.

Sofia Ivanova: scheduler interactions too. with warm KV, decode phase dominates; with cold prefill, prefill queueing + overlap matters. baselines need to tell us *where* the tail is coming from.

Jonas Weber: from representativeness: telemetry shows a big chunk of traffic is first-turn / new session (cold) AND a big chunk is later turns (warm KV). so either way, single number is misleading.

Claire Dubois: ok but what’s the canonical definition? I don’t want “warm” to mean “we happened to run it after another job and L2 was hot” :grimacing:

Sean Gallagher: SRE take: in prod, caches exist and matter, but we also care about worst-case cold start behavior (new pod, evictions). For gating, it’s actually useful to have two scenarios with strict isolation rules.

Allison Grant: if we do two, we need tags + dashboards to separate them. otherwise people will cherry-pick. please don’t create “baseline_ttft” without cache_mode in the label set.

Sana Farid: variance note: caching can *reduce* variance but can also create bimodal distributions if hit rate drifts. If we do warm, we need to enforce/measure hit rate and fail the run if it’s outside a band.

Hiro Tanaka: kernel cliffs show up differently too. long-prefill kernel selection is the main risk area; warm KV reduces prefill contribution so we might miss regressions in that path.

Mateo Alvarez: sounds like we’re converging on “both”. question is where to draw boundary. I propose:
1) Cold: caching disabled (prefix cache off, KV cache off), every request independent.
2) Warm: caching enabled with a *defined* warm procedure (seeded conversations) + explicit cache metrics captured.

Rina Kobayashi: can we make it concrete for the harness?
- add `cache_mode: cold|warm` to manifest
- for cold: set runtime flags `--prefix-cache=0 --kv-cache=0`
- for warm: `--prefix-cache=1 --kv-cache=1` but warmup phase is deterministic

Omar El-Sayed: KV cache “warm” needs session continuity. Suggest: each virtual user does N turns, where turn1 is cold prefill, turns2..N reuse KV. Report metrics for turn1 separately from continuation turns.

Sofia Ivanova: +1. Otherwise warm can hide queueing issues. For chat streaming workload: we should have metrics buckets:
- TTFT_turn1 (cold)
- TTFT_turnK (warm)
- TPOT_turnK
And for tool-calling: bursts where KV may/may not help.

Claire Dubois: I like that but then we have “warm” scenario still containing a cold component (turn1). Is that ok?

Jonas Weber: yes, that’s actually representative: a session starts somewhere. just label it explicitly.

Allison Grant: please keep the naming consistent: `cache_mode` + `turn_index` or `turn_bucket=first|followup`. We can build Grafana panels off that.

Sean Gallagher: also define eviction behavior. For baseline warm, we should *avoid* evictions by sizing cache generously, otherwise hit rate becomes a function of concurrency. Then have a separate “stress” workload later if we want eviction.

Sana Farid: agreeing with Sean. For Q1 baseline, warm should target >= 95% KV hit rate on followup turns (or whatever we can reliably achieve) and we record actual hit rate. If it drops under, fail the run as invalid (not a perf regression).

Hiro Tanaka: warm prefix cache is trickier (shared). Are we enabling cross-request prefix cache in baseline warm? That could be unrealistic depending on tenant isolation.

Mateo Alvarez: good call. Let’s separate:
- Warm KV: yes (per-session)
- Prefix cache: report both off/on? or keep it off for baseline?

Rina Kobayashi: I’d keep prefix cache OFF in baseline unless we can define a stable, non-leaky corpus. prefix cache hit rate depends on prompt reuse patterns and could be sensitive.

Jonas Weber: from telemetry, prefix reuse exists but it’s very cohort-specific. I’m ok leaving prefix cache out of baseline v1 and adding it as an explicit “optional scenario” later.

Omar El-Sayed: +1. also prefix cache can raise privacy questions. we can still capture a “prefix_cache_enabled=false” in manifest so it’s explicit.

Claire Dubois: so decision:
- publish baseline numbers for two scenarios: cold and warm-KV
- warm-KV defined as multi-turn sessions with deterministic warmup; prefix cache remains disabled (for Q1 baseline)
- dashboards + manifest capture the flags + hit rate

Connor O'Brien: from release gating POV, two scenarios is fine as long as thresholds are per-scenario. We can gate on cold-turn1 TTFT p99 and warm-followup TPOT p99 separately.

Allison Grant: :thumbsup: as long as the metric taxonomy is crisp. I’ll add `cache_mode`, `kv_cache_enabled`, `prefix_cache_enabled`, `kv_hit_rate`, and `turn_bucket` to the metrics dictionary draft.

Sean Gallagher: and add to runbook: for cold runs, we should flush caches / restart worker between runs (or run with flags so state can’t persist). for warm runs, explicitly *don’t* restart between warmup and measure phases.

Rina Kobayashi: I’ll file a harness task:
- manifest schema update (add cache fields)
- workload generator update for multi-turn + labeling
- validator checks for warm run must include hit rate + min turns

Mateo Alvarez: cool. Claire can you update methodology doc to reflect “report both cold and warm-KV” and call out prefix cache deferred?

Claire Dubois: yup. will update the Confluence page + link ADR-0172 section on run manifest fields. Also adding a note: baseline tables must show cold + warm columns, no single blended number.

Sofia Ivanova: :white_check_mark: thank you. this will save us a ton of confusion when someone says “TTFT regressed” but only for cold prefill.

Hiro Tanaka: works for me. kernel baselines will still catch long-prefill regressions via cold.

Mateo Alvarez: Decision locked then: Baseline reporting includes BOTH cold and warm-KV scenarios; prefix cache stays off in baseline v1; all runs must record cache config + hit rate and label first vs followup turns.

(Sean Gallagher reacted with :thumbsup:)
(Allison Grant reacted with :white_check_mark:)
(Rina Kobayashi reacted with :hammer_and_wrench:)

Jonas Weber: from telemetry, prefix reuse exists but it’s very cohort-specific. I’m ok leaving prefix cache out of baseline v1 and adding it as an explicit “optional scenario” later.

Omar El-Sayed: +1. also prefix cache can raise privacy questions. we can still capture a “prefix_cache_enabled=false” in manifest so it’s explicit.

Claire Dubois: so decision:
- publish baseline numbers for two scenarios: cold and warm-KV
- warm-KV defined as multi-turn sessions with deterministic warmup; prefix cache remains disabled (for Q1 baseline)
- dashboards + manifest capture the flags + hit rate

Connor O'Brien: from release gating POV, two scenarios is fine as long as thresholds are per-scenario. We can gate on cold-turn1 TTFT p99 and warm-followup TPOT p99 separately.

Allison Grant: :thumbsup: as long as the metric taxonomy is crisp. I’ll add `cache_mode`, `kv_cache_enabled`, `prefix_cache_enabled`, `kv_hit_rate`, and `turn_bucket` to the metrics dictionary draft.

Sean Gallagher: and add to runbook: for cold runs, we should flush caches / restart worker between runs (or run with flags so state can’t persist). for warm runs, explicitly *don’t* restart between warmup and measure phases.

Rina Kobayashi: I’ll file a harness task:
- manifest schema update (add cache fields)
- workload generator update for multi-turn + labeling
- validator checks for warm run must include hit rate + min turns

Mateo Alvarez: cool. Claire can you update methodology doc to reflect “report both cold and warm-KV” and call out prefix cache deferred?

Claire Dubois: yup. will update the Confluence page + link ADR-0172 section on run manifest fields. Also adding a note: baseline tables must show cold + warm columns, no single blended number.

Sofia Ivanova: :white_check_mark: thank you. this will save us a ton of confusion when someone says “TTFT regressed” but only for cold prefill.

Hiro Tanaka: works for me. kernel baselines will still catch long-prefill regressions via cold.

Mateo Alvarez: Decision locked then: Baseline reporting includes BOTH cold and warm-KV scenarios; prefix cache stays off in baseline v1; all runs must record cache config + hit rate and label first vs followup turns.

(Sean Gallagher reacted with :thumbsup:)
(Allison Grant reacted with :white_check_mark:)
(Rina Kobayashi reacted with :hammer_and_wrench:)

Mateo Alvarez: yep, let’s lock it down today. proposal:
- baseline freeze: **Mon 2026-01-26 18:00 PT** (no more perf-affecting changes included in “Q1 2026 baseline” after that)
- baseline anchor: runtime tag **`runtime-perf-baseline-q1-2026`** pointing at the release build we cut that afternoon

Connor O'Brien: works for gating too. can we make sure the anchor is *both* a git tag + an image digest? perf-canary wants something immutable.

Sahana Iyer: +1. tagging is fine but we should pin the docker digest in CI configs. suggest:
- git tag: `runtime-perf-baseline-q1-2026`
- image tag: `ghcr.io/redwoodinference/runtime:baseline-q1-2026`

Mateo Alvarez: good call. baseline anchor should be a tuple:
- runtime tag
- benchmark-lab tag
- scheduler baseline config rev
- kernel selection rules rev

Connor O'Brien: +1, and in perf-canary gating mode we’ll compare PR builds against that baseline bucket.

Sahana Iyer: I’ll update CI templates to accept both `baseline_tag` and `baseline_image_digest` (and fail if only a mutable tag is provided).

Noah Patel: great. can we also tag benchmark-lab the same day? otherwise people will keep rerunning with slightly different workload params.

Rina Kobayashi: yep. I’ll cut **`benchmark-lab-perf-suite-q1-2026-v1`** at the same freeze time and post the commit SHA + the manifest schema version.

Sahana Iyer: :thumbsup:

source.text:
Marcus Reed: seeing a tail latency regression in perf-canary on H100 pool w/ `h100-balanced` preset enabled. p95 went +18-25% on streaming chat workloads (decode-heavy) vs baseline. anyone else looking at this?

Aisha Bello: yep, alert fired in #perf-canary. it’s isolated to builds with profile pack `0.3.1` + runtime `1.18.0-rc2`. p50 stable, p95/p99 up. tokens/sec roughly flat.

Sofia Ivanova: which gate? the “streaming_chat_p95” one?

Aisha Bello: gate is `h100_streaming_chat_p95_ms` (threshold +10%). we’re at +22% right now. dashboard: https://grafana.redwood.ai/d/perf-canary/hw-profiles?var=sku:h100&var=workload:stream-chat (internal)

Marcus Reed: attaching snippet from runner summary (H100 SXM):
```
workload=stream-chat
concurrency=64
prompt_tokens_p50=520
completion_tokens_p50=210
baseline_p95_ms=418
profile_p95_ms=510
baseline_tps=142.3
profile_tps=141.1
errors=0
retries=0
```
Also saw queue time component spike (server-side).

Lena Fischer: queue time spike suggests scheduler fairness / backpressure config. what changed in the preset between baseline and `h100-balanced`?

Marcus Reed: mostly the prefill/decode fairness knobs + queue limits. from the preset:
- `scheduler.prefill_decode_fairness=true`
- `scheduler.prefill_budget_tokens_per_tick=16384`
- `scheduler.decode_min_quantum_tokens=8`
- `scheduler.max_queue_depth=2048` (baseline 4096)
- `scheduler.backpressure.enable=true`

Sofia Ivanova: that fairness toggle is the main suspect. It can improve mean throughput under mixed workloads, but under decode-heavy streaming it can serialize too much and inflate tail if prefill bursts keep cutting in.

Claire Dubois: which scenario in the workload matrix is this? the one with tool-call-ish longer prompts?

Marcus Reed: matrix case S-CHAT-3 (prompt length bucket 512-1k, streaming, 70/30 mix of short/medium completions). concurrency 64 and 96 both regressed. non-streaming is fine.

Rina Kobayashi: i can repro locally on the h100 runner using matrix runner `--case S-CHAT-3 --profile h100-balanced`. also see increased `scheduler.dequeue_wait_ms_p95`.

Noah Patel: can we confirm it’s not kernel selection? any change in attention kernel bucket selection for this seq len?

Yuki Sato: kernel autoselect shouldn’t be touching this workload yet (seq len mostly <1k). also the regression is in queue wait, not GPU compute. but i can pull `kernel_selected` metrics to be sure.

Hiro Tanaka: +1, if GPU time unchanged and queue time up, it’s scheduler. do we have per-stage breakdown? prefill vs decode latency components?

Marcus Reed: from debug log (sampling 1/1000 requests), profile build:
```
request_id=... stage=prefill gpu_ms=21.4
request_id=... stage=decode_total gpu_ms=188.2
request_id=... stage=queue_wait ms=92.7 (p95)
request_id=... stage=queue_wait ms=41.1 (p95 baseline)
```
GPU ms basically same.

Sofia Ivanova: hypothesis: fairness loop is doing “prefill priority windows” too aggressively. decode streams are getting chopped into smaller quanta, increasing requeue overhead and queue contention, esp at high conc.

Lena Fischer: also `max_queue_depth=2048` could cause earlier backpressure and more churn. but that would typically show as rejections/retries; we’re not seeing errors.

Sean Gallagher: oncall question: do we need to flip the preset off in canary pools right now or can we safely let it run while we patch?

Neha Kapoor: if it’s a clear +22% p95 regression on a customer-ish workload, i’d prefer we stop the blast radius. can we disable just the fairness toggle via override for the canary pool?

Benji Okafor: yes, override is possible via env var `RW_SCHEDULER_PREFILL_DECODE_FAIRNESS=0` (takes precedence over profile). we can apply at pool level in the canary deployment.

Sofia Ivanova: let’s do that as mitigation. but we still want a real fix because we want fairness on mixed workloads.

Marcus Reed: agreed. i’ll ask infra to apply override in the canary pool (H100-us-east-1-canary).

Sean Gallagher: i can take that. drop the exact override + pool name in a message and i’ll patch the config.

Marcus Reed: pool: `api-h100-use1-canary-a`. override:
`RW_SCHEDULER_PREFILL_DECODE_FAIRNESS=0`.

Sean Gallagher: ack. will roll in ~15 mins. :thumbsup:

Rina Kobayashi: meanwhile, i ran A/B with only fairness toggled off (keeping other preset knobs). p95 comes back within +3% of baseline. so it’s almost entirely fairness.

Sofia Ivanova: ok. next: find the specific fairness behavior. Marcus, can you share the scheduler stats? like number of context switches, prefill bursts, etc.

Marcus Reed: grabbed counters:
```
scheduler.prefill_rounds_per_sec: +35%
scheduler.decode_rounds_per_sec: +8%
scheduler.stream_requeues_per_request_p95: 14 (baseline 6)
scheduler.avg_decode_quantum_tokens: 8 (baseline ~22)
```
So decode quantum shrinking is real.

Lena Fischer: decode quantum pinned to 8 via `decode_min_quantum_tokens=8`. but baseline getting ~22 suggests that with fairness on, we’re not letting it grow. maybe the “prefill always wins if waiting” logic is too strict.

Sofia Ivanova: yep. we changed logic last week to avoid prefill starvation in mixed traffic: “if any prefill pending, cap decode quantum to min.” That’s probably wrong for streaming heavy because tiny decode quanta add overhead and increase contention.

Noah Patel: is there a config to set a prefill/decode ratio instead of hard cap? e.g., allow decode quantum to expand if queue_wait is high.

Sofia Ivanova: in code, there’s `prefill_fairness_mode` with a “soft” path we didn’t enable. might be safest to patch: only force min quantum if prefill backlog > X or if prefill wait > Y ms.

Hiro Tanaka: keep it simple: guard the cap with backlog threshold, default tuned per sku. that’s a safe fix.

Claire Dubois: make sure the workload matrix includes a decode-heavy streaming case (this one) as a regression test once fixed.

Rina Kobayashi: it already does (S-CHAT-3), but we weren’t gating it tightly enough before enabling fairness. can tighten the gate once patch lands.

Sofia Ivanova: action items:
1) mitigation: disable fairness on canary pools (Sean doing)
2) fix: change fairness logic to avoid min-quantum cap unless prefill backlog is “material”
3) add regression test for stream requeue count / queue_wait p95 maybe

Marcus Reed: i can do the fix w/ Sofia reviewing. proposed patch:
- introduce `scheduler.prefill_backlog_threshold_tokens` (default 8192 for H100)
- only apply decode quantum cap when `prefill_backlog_tokens > threshold` OR `prefill_wait_p95 > 50ms`
- log both values at debug level for canary

Sofia Ivanova: sounds good. add a unit test around the decision boundary + an integration perf check in perf-canary notes.

Benji Okafor: re: profile, do we want to keep `prefill_decode_fairness=true` in the H100 preset, but rely on the safer runtime behavior? or should we also adjust profile values?

Noah Patel: i’d keep the profile intention, but we need the runtime guard so profiles don’t encode unsafe behavior. might still bump `decode_min_quantum_tokens` from 8 -> 16 after we verify.

Lena Fischer: +1, 8 seems too low. but let’s land the logic fix first, then tune.

Sean Gallagher: mitigation deployed to `api-h100-use1-canary-a`. perf-canary should reflect within next run.

Aisha Bello: seeing improvement already on latest canary run with override. p95 back to +4.5% (still a hair high but within gate if it holds).

Marcus Reed: great. i’ll open PR in redwood runtime now. ETA 1-2 hrs.

Sofia Ivanova: please link it here once up.

Marcus Reed: PR is up: https://github.com/redwood/redwood/pull/22033 ("Fix tail latency regression in prefill/decode fairness"). includes config threshold, behavior change, and adds a regression assertion to scheduler tests.

Hiro Tanaka: skimmed. looks reasonable. one comment: make sure threshold defaults are SKU-specific via profile or via runtime default table.

Marcus Reed: currently runtime default table keyed by detected SKU; profile can override. i set H100 default 8192 tokens, L40S 4096, B200 same as H100 for now behind flag.

Neha Kapoor: once PR merges, what’s plan to re-enable fairness in canary? i want a clear rollback point.

Sofia Ivanova: plan: after merge, ship to canary with fairness ON (remove override) but add stricter alert on `queue_wait_p95` and `stream_requeues_p95`. rollback criteria: p95 > +10% for 2 consecutive runs OR queue_wait_p95 > 75ms.

Aisha Bello: i can add temporary panels/alerts for those counters for the canary dashboard.

Claire Dubois: please also annotate the expected behavior doc: fairness improves mixed traffic but shouldn’t regress decode-heavy streaming beyond +5% p95.

Marcus Reed: will do. also adding notes to the H100 profile ref once fix is in.

Sofia Ivanova: :thumbsup: i’ll review PR now.

Sean Gallagher: leaving override in place until PR is merged + we do a controlled re-enable. ping me when ready.

Marcus Reed: thanks all. will update thread after perf-canary on the patched build.

Lena Fischer: also `max_queue_depth=2048` could cause earlier backpressure and more churn. but that would typically show as rejections/retries; we’re not seeing errors.

Sean Gallagher: oncall question: do we need to flip the preset off in canary pools right now or can we safely let it run while we patch?

Neha Kapoor: if it’s a clear +22% p95 regression on a customer-ish workload, i’d prefer we stop the blast radius. can we disable just the fairness toggle via override for the canary pool?

Benji Okafor: yes, override is possible via env var `RW_SCHEDULER_PREFILL_DECODE_FAIRNESS=0` (takes precedence over profile). we can apply at pool level in the canary deployment.

Sofia Ivanova: let’s do that as mitigation. but we still want a real fix because we want fairness on mixed workloads.

Marcus Reed: agreed. i’ll ask infra to apply override in the canary pool (H100-us-east-1-canary).

Sean Gallagher: i can take that. drop the exact override + pool name in a message and i’ll patch the config.

Marcus Reed: pool: `api-h100-use1-canary-a`. override:
`RW_SCHEDULER_PREFILL_DECODE_FAIRNESS=0`.

Sean Gallagher: ack. will roll in ~15 mins. :thumbsup:

Rina Kobayashi: meanwhile, i ran A/B with only fairness toggled off (keeping other preset knobs). p95 comes back within +3% of baseline. so it’s almost entirely fairness.

Sofia Ivanova: ok. next: find the specific fairness behavior. Marcus, can you share the scheduler stats? like number of context switches, prefill bursts, etc.

Marcus Reed: grabbed counters:
```
scheduler.prefill_rounds_per_sec: +35%
scheduler.decode_rounds_per_sec: +8%
scheduler.stream_requeues_per_request_p95: 14 (baseline 6)
scheduler.avg_decode_quantum_tokens: 8 (baseline ~22)
```
So decode quantum shrinking is real.

Lena Fischer: decode quantum pinned to 8 via `decode_min_quantum_tokens=8`. but baseline getting ~22 suggests that with fairness on, we’re not letting it grow. maybe the “prefill always wins if waiting” logic is too strict.

Sofia Ivanova: yep. we changed logic last week to avoid prefill starvation in mixed traffic: “if any prefill pending, cap decode quantum to min.” That’s probably wrong for streaming heavy because tiny decode quanta add overhead and increase contention.

Noah Patel: is there a config to set a prefill/decode ratio instead of hard cap? e.g., allow decode quantum to expand if queue_wait is high.

Sofia Ivanova: in code, there’s `prefill_fairness_mode` with a “soft” path we didn’t enable. might be safest to patch: only force min quantum if prefill backlog > X or if prefill wait > Y ms.

Hiro Tanaka: keep it simple: guard the cap with backlog threshold, default tuned per sku. that’s a safe fix.

Claire Dubois: make sure the workload matrix includes a decode-heavy streaming case (this one) as a regression test once fixed.

Rina Kobayashi: it already does (S-CHAT-3), but we weren’t gating it tightly enough before enabling fairness. can tighten the gate once patch lands.

Sofia Ivanova: action items:
1) mitigation: disable fairness on canary pools (Sean doing)
2) fix: change fairness logic to avoid min-quantum cap unless prefill backlog is “material”
3) add regression test for stream requeue count / queue_wait p95 maybe

Marcus Reed: i can do the fix w/ Sofia reviewing. proposed patch:
- introduce `scheduler.prefill_backlog_threshold_tokens` (default 8192 for H100)
- only apply decode quantum cap when `prefill_backlog_tokens > threshold` OR `prefill_wait_p95 > 50ms`
- log both values at debug level for canary

Sofia Ivanova: sounds good. add a unit test around the decision boundary + an integration perf check in perf-canary notes.

Benji Okafor: re: profile, do we want to keep `prefill_decode_fairness=true` in the H100 preset, but rely on the safer runtime behavior? or should we also adjust profile values?

Noah Patel: i’d keep the profile intention, but we need the runtime guard so profiles don’t encode unsafe behavior. might still bump `decode_min_quantum_tokens` from 8 -> 16 after we verify.

Lena Fischer: +1, 8 seems too low. but let’s land the logic fix first, then tune.

Sean Gallagher: mitigation deployed to `api-h100-use1-canary-a`. perf-canary should reflect within next run.

Aisha Bello: seeing improvement already on latest canary run with override. p95 back to +4.5% (still a hair high but within gate if it holds).

Marcus Reed: great. i’ll open PR in redwood runtime now. ETA 1-2 hrs.

Sofia Ivanova: please link it here once up.

Marcus Reed: PR is up: https://github.com/redwood/redwood/pull/22033 ("Fix tail latency regression in prefill/decode fairness"). includes config threshold, behavior change, and adds a regression assertion to scheduler tests.

Hiro Tanaka: skimmed. looks reasonable. one comment: make sure threshold defaults are SKU-specific via profile or via runtime default table.

Marcus Reed: currently runtime default table keyed by detected SKU; profile can override. i set H100 default 8192 tokens, L40S 4096, B200 same as H100 for now behind flag.

Neha Kapoor: once PR merges, what’s plan to re-enable fairness in canary? i want a clear rollback point.

Sofia Ivanova: plan: after merge, ship to canary with fairness ON (remove override) but add stricter alert on `queue_wait_p95` and `stream_requeues_p95`. rollback criteria: p95 > +10% for 2 consecutive runs OR queue_wait_p95 > 75ms.

Aisha Bello: i can add temporary panels/alerts for those counters for the canary dashboard.

Claire Dubois: please also annotate the expected behavior doc: fairness improves mixed traffic but shouldn’t regress decode-heavy streaming beyond +5% p95.

Marcus Reed: will do. also adding notes to the H100 profile ref once fix is in.

Sofia Ivanova: :thumbsup: i’ll review PR now.

Sean Gallagher: leaving override in place until PR is merged + we do a controlled re-enable. ping me when ready.

Marcus Reed: thanks all. will update thread after perf-canary on the patched build.

source.messages:
Benji Okafor: quick Q on profile loader edge cases before I lock the selection semantics: MIG partitions + multi-GPU nodes. Do we treat profile selection per *device* or per *node*? (and what do we do when devices differ)

Nina Petrova: depends on where we’re deploying. most of our dedicated pools are homogeneous nodes, but we do have multi-gpu hosts (8x H100 SXM) and some oddball mixed configs in lab. MIG is the bigger gotcha.

Yuki Sato: +1. for MIG you can end up with `GPU 0: H100-SXM5-80GB (MIG 1g.10gb)` etc. SKU string is not stable. sometimes NVML reports the parent, sometimes only MIG device name.

Ethan Park: schema-wise we assumed `hardware.gpu_sku` maps to a single preset. if we go per-node and you have heterogeneous devices, we’d need either (a) disallow, or (b) define merge rules. merge rules sound like footguns.

Diego Santos: per-node is way simpler for deployment modes (esp Private via helm values). but runtime actually instantiates per-device workers, right? so profile can be *derived* per device but config file is per node.

Noah Patel: I’d frame it as: “effective runtime config” is node-level, but selected profile should be the min-risk compatible target across devices. i.e., if any device is unknown, fall back to generic safe profile.

Benji Okafor: ok but in MIG case you have like 7 MIG instances on same physical GPU. do we pick H100 profile or something MIG-specific?

Samir Qureshi: MIG behaves like “smaller GPU” from memory/KV cache perspective. if we apply H100-80GB defaults (kv budget etc) to a MIG 1g slice, we’ll blow up immediately.

Omar El-Sayed: +1. KV cache budgets must be tied to available HBM *per device*. we already compute a memory budget at startup from `total_device_mem_bytes`. so profile can set ratios/ceilings but should not hardcode absolute sizes unless guarded.

Sofia Ivanova: scheduling knobs also change under MIG. continuous batching + queue depth tuned for H100 can create crazy tail latency if the slice is small. we should treat MIG as a distinct “class” and be conservative.

Yuki Sato: detection: NVML exposes MIG mode + per-instance memory. we can detect `is_mig=true` and include `mig_profile: 1g.10gb` / `2g.20gb` etc if present. but string variants are messy.

Quentin Harris: compatibility matrix: if profile requires `gpu_sku=H100` AND `mig=false`, loader should refuse when MIG enabled. log: “profile incompatible with MIG mode; using fallback.”

Benji Okafor: so proposed rule: selection key includes `{normalized_sku, mig_enabled, device_mem_bucket}`? or at least mig_enabled.

Ethan Park: I’m ok with mig_enabled as a first cut. mem bucket might explode the profile count. better: keep profile stable and enforce guardrails via computed budgets (like Omar said).

Marcus Reed: for multi-GPU nodes: do we assume all devices same SKU? usually yes but not always. for runtime, if devices differ, scheduler per worker might diverge.

Nina Petrova: in prod fleet we should not have mixed SKUs within a node. but we *can* have mixed partitions (some MIG enabled, some not) depending on how nodes were configured (rare, but I’ve seen it in test).

Sean Gallagher: from SRE/oncall POV: I’d rather we fail closed than silently apply “best effort” across mixed configs. logs + metric should clearly show “heterogeneous hardware detected -> safe profile.” otherwise debugging becomes impossible.

Benji Okafor: agree. was thinking: if devices don’t agree on (sku, mig_enabled), we select `generic-safe` and emit a high-cardinality event? maybe just a counter.

Noah Patel: don’t do high-cardinality events tied to hostnames. do: `runtime_profile_selection_heterogeneous=1` + include a short list in startup logs.

Yuki Sato: we can log something like:
```
Detected GPUs:
- idx=0 sku=H100-SXM mig=false mem=80GB
- idx=1 sku=H100-SXM mig=true  mem=10GB (1g.10gb)
Selection: generic-safe (reason=heterogeneous_devices)
```

Ethan Park: yep. schema can encode “requires homogeneous devices” as a compatibility constraint. for now we can hardcode that rule in loader and add to schema later.

Diego Santos: question: if node is homogeneous 8x H100, do we select H100 profile once and apply globally? or can per-device compute some derived knobs (eg NUMA/CPU thread pinning)?

Sofia Ivanova: scheduler settings should be same across workers ideally. per-device differences would complicate fairness/backpressure. I’d keep scheduler knobs node-level.

Samir Qureshi: memory/KV budgets *must* be per-device computed anyway. that’s already per worker. profile should specify policy like `kv_cache.max_fraction_of_hbm=0.55` and worker applies it.

Omar El-Sayed: +1. the only per-device knob I’d allow is any knob already expressed as a fraction of device memory or derived from `sm_count`.

Marcus Reed: multi-GPU + model parallel: sometimes NCCL settings depend on topology (NVLink vs PCIe). those are node-level and assume devices identical. heterogeneous node would be unsupported.

Nina Petrova: our topology assumptions: H100 SXM nodes = NVLink full mesh. L40S nodes are usually PCIe + smaller mem; also sometimes 4x not 8x. keep those as separate profiles.

Benji Okafor: ok. tentative decision:
1) profile selection evaluates *per device* to detect sku/mig mode, but must be consistent across devices.
2) if inconsistent -> select `generic-safe` and set metric + loud startup log.
3) MIG enabled -> do not use H100/B200/L40S tuned profiles; use MIG-safe profile (conservative scheduler + stricter kv caps) OR generic-safe if we don’t have MIG-safe yet.

Quentin Harris: + add explicit compatibility message: “profile pack vX does not support MIG; supported=[mig=false].” helps Private users.

Sean Gallagher: and make it easy to override. oncall should be able to set `RUNTIME_PROFILE=generic-safe` or disable selection entirely. (I know you’re already doing override order work.)

Benji Okafor: yep, env var override is already top precedence. will also add `--dump-effective-config` output later.

Ethan Park: can you document the MIG limitation in the schema doc + known issues? we’ll get tickets otherwise.

Benji Okafor: will do. also adding a unit test for “mixed mig mode across devices -> fallback”.

Yuki Sato: one more: what about “MIG disabled but GPU memory is 94GB vs 80GB” (B200 variants)? if mem differs slightly across devices, do we consider heterogeneous? maybe treat mem as informational only.

Noah Patel: agree. heterogeneity criteria should be strict on sku + mig_enabled, not on mem. mem is used only for guardrails.

Sofia Ivanova: :thumbsup: and please make sure fallback profile uses safer queue limits. we’ve seen p95 blowups when queue is too deep on small slices.

Benji Okafor: ack. I’ll ping you when I have the default knobs for generic-safe.

Keiko Yamamoto: FYI autoscaling/guardrails: if MIG-safe profile clamps throughput, some pools may appear “underperforming” to autoscaler. please include profile tag in metrics so we can segment.

Benji Okafor: good call. I’ll emit `runtime_selected_profile{name=..., source=...}` and `runtime_profile_compat_reason` as a label? (careful on cardinality)

Sean Gallagher: labels fine if bounded set of reasons. keep it like 5-10 reasons max.

Benji Okafor: got it. reasons will be: `override`, `exact_match`, `incompatible_mig`, `heterogeneous_devices`, `unknown_sku`, `ambiguous_detection`.

Ethan Park: sounds good. please drop summary into the ADR thread later (or at least a note in the PR) so we don’t re-litigate.

Benji Okafor: will add a comment in PR-21991 draft + link this thread. thanks all.

source.content:
Marcus Reed: quick q on the chat streaming workload generator — how are we pacing tokens on the stream right now? I'm seeing TPOT jump around a lot and I *think* we might be measuring the client more than the runtime.

Rina Kobayashi: yeah, current harness uses an async reader but it basically does `for chunk in resp.aiter_bytes(): ...` and we do some parsing + metrics aggregation inline.

Marcus Reed: that’s what I suspected. if parsing/aggregation stalls the loop we’ll inflate “inter-token” time even if server is fine.

Claire Dubois: +1. we need to be explicit what boundary we measure: server push vs client read. otherwise TPOT is “network + client drain speed + python overhead”.

Sofia Ivanova: also: server side will naturally backpressure if client doesn’t read fast enough (TCP window). so a slow reader can literally slow generation and change batching behavior.

Sean Gallagher: for perf suite we probably want a “drain as fast as possible” client to avoid backpressure. then *optionally* a “paced” mode to mimic UI.

Allison Grant: agree. we should align with ADR-0172 definitions: TTFT = first token on wire (client received first byte) vs first token produced (server). which are we reporting?

Rina Kobayashi: right now we’re reporting:
- TTFT_client = time from request send -> first parsed token event
- TPOT_client = (time between parsed token events)
so yeah it’s client-side.

Connor O'Brien: for gating, I’d strongly prefer metrics that don’t fluctuate with client implementation details. if we change python libs we’d fail perf gates.

Marcus Reed: proposal:
1) add a “server_timing” mode where server sends timestamps in stream metadata (e.g. per chunk headers or a side-channel) so we can compute TPOT_server without client parsing affecting it.
2) meanwhile implement a “fast drain” reader that does minimal work in the read loop; push chunks into a ring buffer/queue and parse in a separate task.

Claire Dubois: (1) might be too invasive for Q1 baseline if it touches runtime. but (2) seems doable quickly in benchmark-lab.

Sofia Ivanova: careful with (1): adding timestamp per token can add overhead and change chunking. but we could timestamp per *flush* (chunk) and still get useful signal.

Allison Grant: from observability perspective, we already have server-side span timings for:
- request admitted
- prefill start/end
- first token emitted
- decode end
can we export those into the run artifacts (manifest links) so perf suite reports both client-observed and server-observed?

Rina Kobayashi: we can for perf runs. for the generator PR (21455) I can add an option to fetch trace IDs + pull timings from the tracing backend, but that adds dependency / auth. :/

Sean Gallagher: let’s keep baseline harness self-contained. but we can add an *optional* mode for internal runs to join traces.

Sahana Iyer: also note: if you run the client in the same k8s node w/ noisy neighbors you’ll see read jitter. we should pin the client to a dedicated CPU set in the benchmark job spec. I can help with the CI job template.

Marcus Reed: concrete action items:
- implement `--stream_read_mode={fast_drain, paced}`
- fast_drain: read loop only records `t_first_byte`, `t_last_byte`, raw bytes length; parsing + token counting off-thread
- paced: mimic UI, e.g. sleep 10-20ms after each chunk read (configurable), but *do not* use for gating

Claire Dubois: and report both TTFT and TPOT as:
- `ttft_client_first_byte` (first byte received)
- `ttft_client_first_token` (first token event parsed)
- `tpot_client_bytes` (time between chunk receives)
- `tpot_client_tokens` (time between parsed token events)
so we can detect “parser got slower” vs “server got slower”.

Allison Grant: naming nit: please don’t call it “tpot_client_bytes” if it’s actually per chunk; maybe `inter_chunk_ms_p50/p95` and `chunk_size_bytes` histogram.

Rina Kobayashi: makes sense. I’ll add chunk histograms + a metric for `client_read_gap_ms` (time we weren’t reading).

Sofia Ivanova: also: we should disable Nagle on the client sockets if possible (TCP_NODELAY) to avoid coalescing effects. not sure with httpx/requests.

Rina Kobayashi: we’re using aiohttp currently. I can set `tcp_nodelay=True` on the connector.

Sean Gallagher: +1. also make sure we aren’t measuring TLS handshake in TTFT (warm connections). we should do a warmup phase and keep-alive.

Marcus Reed: yeah, warmup is separate but for streaming it matters. can we do:
- pre-open N connections
- run 30s warmup
- then measure 3-5min

Rina Kobayashi: yes. I’ll add `--warmup_seconds` and `--keepalive_pool_size` to the chat workload.

Connor O'Brien: gating policy note: for release gating we should use:
- `ttft_client_first_byte_p50/p95/p99`
- `tpot_client_tokens_p50/p95` only if in fast_drain mode and client_read_gap ~ 0
and fail the run if client_read_gap exceeds threshold (i.e. invalid run).

Claire Dubois: love that: “invalidate” vs “regression”. otherwise we’ll page people for a python GC pause.

Allison Grant: suggest thresholds for invalid run:
- `client_read_gap_ms_p95 < 2ms`
- `cpu_util_client < 80%` (avoid saturation)
- stable event loop lag (if we can measure)

Sahana Iyer: event loop lag we can log with a simple periodic callback measuring drift. I can send a snippet.

Sahana Iyer: snippet:
```python
async def loop_lag_monitor(period=0.1):
    last = time.perf_counter()
    while True:
        await asyncio.sleep(period)
        now = time.perf_counter()
        lag = max(0.0, now - last - period)
        metrics.observe('client_event_loop_lag_ms', lag * 1000)
        last = now
```

Rina Kobayashi: perfect.

Marcus Reed: ok decision: baseline perf suite uses fast_drain + first_byte TTFT as canonical. we still keep “paced” mode but it’s non-gating + labeled as “ui-like”.

Claire Dubois: please write this into the methodology doc as well: “streaming client must drain continuously; paced mode is for product realism only”.

Rina Kobayashi: ack. I’ll update benchmark-lab PR and add a note in the workload README + run manifest field: `stream_read_mode` + `stream_pacing_ms`.

Sean Gallagher: :thumbsup: when you have a branch, drop a link here and I’ll sanity check on the benchmark fleet (we’ve seen weirdness with small recv buffers before).

Allison Grant: one more thing: in dashboards we should show TTFT by metric boundary (first_byte vs first_token) side-by-side so people don’t confuse them.

Marcus Reed: +1. closing the loop after Rina’s changes land.

source.text:
Talia Benson: ok folks, need to lock the baseline sweep schedule for next week (models x regions x SKUs) so we can reserve windows on the benchmark fleet. pls comment on constraints + owners.

Talia Benson: tracker sheet (please use this one, it has the capacity tab + conditional formatting): https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1rwiQ1_Engineering_ServingRuntime_Q1_2026 ("q1-2026-baseline-run-tracker")

Rina Kobayashi: thx. proposing we split into 2 tracks:
- track A: us-east (H100 + A100)
- track B: eu-west (H100 + L40S)
so we can parallelize but keep env stable per track.

Rina Kobayashi: for models, minimal set for baseline freeze:
- llama-3.1-70b-instruct (fp16 + int8 where supported)
- qwen2.5-72b-instruct
- mistral-large-ish (the one we ship in catalog, exact tag in sheet)
- embeddings: bge-large-en-v1.5 (or whatever catalog tag Jonas picked)

Mateo Alvarez: +1 on parallel tracks. pls make sure each run manifest pins runtime build/tag + scheduler baseline. we can’t have “oops different build” across regions again.

Sofia Ivanova: for scheduling: i’d like long-prefill runs to be isolated from chat streaming if we’re sharing the same node pool window. long-prefill will swing queueing metrics and makes comparison gross.

Sean Gallagher: fleet reality check:
- H100 pool is the bottleneck. we can reserve 8xH100 blocks but only in 4-hour chunks.
- A100 pool is easier (can do overnight).
- L40S pool is shared with console perf folks; need to book early.

i can reserve windows in bench-scheduler but need exact start/end + how many nodes.

BenchmarkBot: Reminder: bench-scheduler reservations require a ticket reference in the description (ENG-4821 or sub-issue). Unreferenced reservations will be auto-expired after 24h.

Talia Benson: noted. we’ll tag all reservations with ENG-4821 + workload id.

Sana Farid: sample size ask: for each (model, workload, region, sku) we need >= 30 iterations post-warmup for p99 stability. if we’re doing cold+warm caching scenarios, that doubles.

Marcus Reed: warmup: current harness default is 5 min warmup + 3 min cool-down between workloads. for long-prefill we may need 10 min warmup to stabilize GPU clocks.

Hiro Tanaka: kernel note: for Qwen on L40S we’re still seeing fallback when seq_len > 8k unless we pin the "flashattn_v3" path. if we don’t want noise, we should either (a) cap seq lengths in long-context workload on L40S or (b) explicitly pin kernel for baseline run.

Mateo Alvarez: baseline policy is “representative, but stable + attributable.” i’m ok with pinning kernel for baseline, but we must record it in the manifest and call it out in the baseline report.

Rina Kobayashi: ok. in the tracker i added a column: "kernel pin?" and "reason".

Sergio Costa: dashboard side: pls standardize run names so links work.
format i need: `q1-2026/{region}/{sku}/{model}/{workload}/{cold|warm}`
otherwise i can’t generate the drilldown URL reliably.

Sahana Iyer: CI / artifact export: if these are running in eu-west, make sure the service account can write to the eu bucket. last time we had a silent failure on artifacts.

Sean Gallagher: yep, that’s still flaky depending on which nodegroup the job lands on. i can force nodegroup `bench-eu-west-1a` for baseline windows. but it reduces capacity.

Talia Benson: can we do this schedule?
Mon:
- us-east H100: chat streaming (llama, qwen) + embeddings
- eu-west H100: long-prefill (llama) + tool-calling bursty
Tue:
- us-east A100: long-prefill (llama, qwen)
- eu-west L40S: chat streaming (llama) + embeddings
Wed:
- us-east H100: tool-calling (llama, qwen) + long-prefill (qwen if time)
- eu-west H100: chat streaming (mistral) + long-prefill (mistral)
Thu:
- cleanup/re-runs + variance investigations + anything that failed export

Rina Kobayashi: workable but i want to avoid mixing tool-calling + chat in same reservation if we can help it (burstiness messes with scheduler counters). could group by “workload family” per window.

Sofia Ivanova: +1. suggest:
- one reservation = one workload generator config (chat, long-prefill, embeddings, tool).
- swap model within the reservation, but keep the workload constant.

Mateo Alvarez: agree. that’s also cleaner for “attribution” when we compare kernel/scheduler deltas.

Sean Gallagher: if that’s the plan, i need reservation blocks like:
- H100 us-east: 4h chat
- H100 us-east: 4h long-prefill
etc.

Sana Farid: also pls don’t schedule “first run of the day” to be a baseline datapoint. we should do a throwaway stabilization run after node provision.

Marcus Reed: i can add a “burn-in” step to the harness invocation for baseline mode. basically:
`--burn_in_runs 5 --warmup_seconds 600`
then record only after.

Hiro Tanaka: if we’re doing long-prefill on H100, i want one slot to capture kernel timelines (nsys) for the p99 tail we saw last week. doesn’t have to be every run, just one per (model, sku).

Rina Kobayashi: ok but note that profiling runs should be labeled `profile=true` so they don’t contaminate the baseline stats. (they run slower)

Sergio Costa: +1. dashboard will filter out `profile=true` if tag exists.

Talia Benson: updating schedule to “workload-constant blocks.” Rina can you assign owners per block in the tracker today?

Rina Kobayashi: yep. i’ll assign:
- chat blocks: Marcus
- long-prefill blocks: Sofia + Marcus
- embeddings blocks: me
- tool-calling blocks: Mateo + Marcus
and will list Hiro’s profiling slots explicitly.

Sean Gallagher: once that’s in the sheet w/ exact times (including timezone), i’ll reserve. pls avoid overlapping H100 blocks across regions if we don’t have capacity.

Sahana Iyer: can someone verify we’re using the same runtime container digest in both regions? last week eu-west pulled latest.

Mateo Alvarez: i’ll pin digest in the harness config today and post in #eng-runtime. baseline anchor is `runtime-2026.01.14-rc3` (unless we change after freeze thread).

Talia Benson: action items:
1) Rina: fill tracker w/ block times + owners + kernel pin flags by EOD
2) Sean: reserve windows once tracker updated (ENG-4821 refs)
3) Mateo: pin runtime digest + confirm baseline tag
4) Sahana: sanity check artifact export permissions for eu-west (before Monday)

Rina Kobayashi: added a tab "Reservations" in the sheet. please don’t DM me times; put them there.

Sean Gallagher: :thumbsup: will watch the sheet. once i reserve, i’ll paste reservation IDs back here.

BenchmarkBot: Reservation guideline: include "cold" vs "warm" in job name; include `manifest_schema_version` in artifacts. See ADR-0172.

Sergio Costa: when you paste reservation IDs, also paste the run-name prefix you’ll use. i’ll wire it into the baseline dashboard panels.

Talia Benson: cool. let’s aim to have reservations locked by tomorrow 11am PT so we don’t lose H100 windows.
