2) Rina to mark the regressed datapoint in the run tracker as “expected due to inflight limit flip” + link this thread
3) Sana to update regression threshold config so that until fix lands, canary doesn’t page us repeatedly on this known issue (route to triage channel only)

Sana Farid: ack. I’ll add a temporary exception keyed by build range + workload id, with expiry (2 weeks). not turning it off entirely.

Sofia Ivanova: I’ll draft a patch: make prefill inflight limit higher when decode_target_tokens <= 512 and prompt_len_bin == long_context, OR make it proportional to expected prefill time. will share proposal in #eng-runtime later today.

Grace Kim: once scheduler fix is in, I’ll run a quick validation sweep on L40S too; we’ve seen different behavior on those cards.

Mateo Alvarez: thanks all. good triage.
text:
Rina Kobayashi: seeing a surprising p99 regression on long-prefill workload in the baseline sweep (the 16k prefill / 256 decode scenario). A100 us-east. p50 looks flat, p99 TTFT jumped ~+28-35% vs last week’s anchor. Anyone around to help triage?

Rina Kobayashi: quick numbers from the dashboard export (same workload seed, same concurrency target=12):
- build: runtime@9f2c1d7 (from last night)
- baseline ref: runtime@7c1a0b4 (12/02)
- model: llama-3.1-70b-instruct (bf16)
- p99 TTFT: 2.41s -> 3.18s (+31.9%)
- p99 TPOT: basically unchanged (+2-3%)
- throughput tokens/s: within noise

Sofia Ivanova: +31% on p99 TTFT with TPOT stable smells like queueing/backpressure or prefill kernel path change (not decode). do you have scheduler queue-wait breakdown from the new metrics?

Rina Kobayashi: yep, grabbed the run artifacts. queue_wait_p99 increased a lot.
- queue_wait_p99: 180ms -> 640ms
- batch_assembly_p99: 22ms -> 31ms
- prefill_gpu_time_p99: 1.9s -> 2.0s (small)
so most of it is “waiting to get on GPU” not GPU time.

Sean Gallagher: that’s consistent with fleet noise too (placement / noisy neighbor) BUT 640ms is big. which node pool? did we have clock locking on for that run?

Rina Kobayashi: node pool: bench-a100-80g-ue1. clock locking enabled per standard warmup (same as baseline ref). warmup completed without errors.

Omar El-Sayed: can you paste the scheduler log snippet around overload? i want to see if we’re hitting the backpressure thresholds (prefill_inflight / kv_cache pressure).

Rina Kobayashi: from `scheduler_stats.jsonl` summary (last 5 min):
```
backpressure_events_total=147
load_shed_total=0
prefill_inflight_limit_hit=139
kv_cache_evictions_total=0
batch_size_prefill_p50=3
batch_size_prefill_p95=6
prefill_queue_depth_p99=29
```
this didn’t happen in the baseline ref (backpressure_events_total was ~0-5).

Sofia Ivanova: that’s… not good. why are we hitting prefill inflight limit now on same concurrency? did default change in the runtime build?

Marcus Reed: there was a change merged last week to protect decode throughput under mixed workloads. i don’t remember it touching the long-prefill-only scenario though. let me check if that build includes the backpressure tuning.

Mateo Alvarez: I’m here. Let’s treat this as real until proven fleet noise. Rina: can you link the run manifest for both runs? want to diff scheduler defaults + kernel selection.

Rina Kobayashi: manifests:
- regressed: https://bench-artifacts.redwood-internal/runs/1763608120/manifest.json
- baseline ref: https://bench-artifacts.redwood-internal/runs/1762931102/manifest.json
(permissions: internal)

Hiro Tanaka: pulling the manifests now. if prefill GPU time p99 only +~5% but scheduler is throttling, might still be kernel-selection causing longer “GPU occupied per request” in a way that reduces batch cadence (e.g., worse overlap).

Yuki Sato: +1. also we’ve seen kernel auto-selection flip around the 12k-16k regime when seq_len crosses heuristic boundaries. if that build changed the kernel matrix, could change how long a prefill holds the stream.

Grace Kim: do we have kernel debug dumps enabled for the regressed run? if not, rerun with `RW_KERNEL_DEBUG_DUMP=1` / `RW_KERNEL_DEBUG_SAMPLE_RATE=1.0` just for this workload.

Rina Kobayashi: kernel dumps were OFF (i didn’t want the overhead). can rerun with dumps ON.

Omar El-Sayed: any profiler trace? even a quick nsys timeline screenshot would help.

Rina Kobayashi: I did a quick nsys capture on a single replica (not full harness) to get a feel. screenshots:
- https://slack-files.com/TMP-nsys_prefill_timeline.png
- https://slack-files.com/TMP-kernel_summary.png
what I notice: fewer concurrent prefill kernels in flight; more gaps between launches.

Sofia Ivanova: “more gaps” suggests we’re throttling prefill submission (inflight limit / fairness) not just slower kernels.

Marcus Reed: I found it: runtime@9f2c1d7 includes PR-21541 “tune backpressure thresholds for long prefill” (merged behind a default flag flip). It tightened prefill_inflight_limit for A100 to reduce p99 *decode* in mixed traffic. Might be biting us in prefill-only.

Mateo Alvarez: ok, that’s likely. but we still need to confirm with data: same workload, only default changed => queueing. Sofia can you validate what knobs changed between manifests?

Sofia Ivanova: diffing now. from manifest (regressed) scheduler_config:
- `prefill_inflight_limit`: 8 (was 16)
- `prefill_fairness_weight`: 0.65 (was 0.5)
- `max_wait_before_force_batch_ms`: 6 (was 10)
that would absolutely increase queue_wait under high prefill time.

Sean Gallagher: + note: if prefill inflight is 8 and concurrency=12, you’re guaranteed backlog. if per-request prefill is ~2s, queue will stack.

Sana Farid: from a noise-model POV this is too large to be “variance”. If manifests show knob delta, we should treat it as a real regression for the long-prefill suite and decide if baseline uses old knobs or we need to re-anchor.

Hiro Tanaka: still want to rule out kernel flip too. if prefill inflight limit dropped, throughput should drop / p50 TTFT might move too, unless batching hides it. but your throughput is flat, so maybe the system is still saturating GPU, just increasing tail waits.

Rina Kobayashi: throughput in tokens/s is flat because the decode is short; prefill dominates and GPU stays mostly busy. the tail is queue wait.

Omar El-Sayed: can you compute “GPU busy %” from the run? (the harness exports `gpu_active_time_ratio` right?)

Rina Kobayashi: yep:
- regressed gpu_active_time_ratio: 0.93
- baseline ref: 0.91
so GPU is busy in both.

Grace Kim: if we rerun with old prefill_inflight_limit (16), we should get back baseline TTFT p99. that would isolate scheduler knob as primary.

Sofia Ivanova: agree. Two experiments:
1) rerun regressed build with `prefill_inflight_limit=16` (override) and compare
2) rerun baseline ref build with `prefill_inflight_limit=8` and see if it reproduces

Mateo Alvarez: perfect. Rina can you run those two A/B runs on the bench pool today?

Rina Kobayashi: yep. I can kick those off now. will take ~45min each including warmup.

Yuki Sato: while that runs, I’ll prep a rerun config with kernel debug dumps enabled (sampled) just in case. if the scheduler knob isn’t the full story, we’ll need kernel attribution.

Hiro Tanaka: if kernel path changed, expect to see attention kernel name differences in dumps (flash-attn v2 vs fallback). For long prefill, we care about the prefill attention path and any rope/scaling kernels that show up.

Sean Gallagher: also sanity check: same driver/CUDA? we’ve been doing some fleet maintenance.

Rina Kobayashi: manifests show same:
- driver: 550.54
- cuda: 12.4
- triton: 3.1.0
- container image: bench-runtime:2025.12.08
so environment looks consistent.

Marcus Reed: note: PR-21541 was meant to improve p99 TTFT on long-prefill in *mixed* workloads by preventing decode starvation. But in a pure prefill stress test it might just be artificial throttling. We might need workload-specific knobs or change the representative suite to include a mixed prefill+chat scenario for gating.

Sana Farid: we should not change the suite to fit the change. suite should reflect prod. question is: do we actually see pure long-prefill saturation in prod? Applied ML said summarization cohort is spiky but not usually isolated.

Omar El-Sayed: still, the suite is intended to catch “worst-case” tails and protect us. If defaults cause huge TTFT tails under plausible concurrency, that’s actionable.

Sofia Ivanova: +1. We can decide to either (a) revert default flip for this quarter baseline, or (b) keep new default but accept the new baseline and adjust thresholding. But this looks like an unintended regression for the workload definition we already agreed on.

Mateo Alvarez: decision later. First isolate.
Action items:
- Rina: run A/B with inflight limit overrides; post results + links
- Sofia: confirm exact config delta + propose guardrail (maybe dynamic inflight based on workload type)
- Hiro/Yuki: prepare kernel dump rerun template (not necessarily run yet)
- Sean: double-check bench pool stability wasn’t compromised (clock locking, MIG config, etc)

Sean Gallagher: on it. I’ll check recent bench-a100 events. no obvious node churn but I’ll confirm.

Rina Kobayashi: starting run #1 now (regressed build + override inflight=16). Run name: `lprefill_a100_ue1_9f2c1d7_inflight16`.

[~55 min later]

Rina Kobayashi: results are in.
Run #1 (regressed build + inflight=16): https://bench-artifacts.redwood-internal/runs/1763612201/
- p99 TTFT: 3.18s -> 2.46s (basically back to baseline)
- queue_wait_p99: 640ms -> 210ms
- backpressure_events_total: 147 -> 11

Rina Kobayashi: starting run #2 (baseline ref build + override inflight=8). Run name: `lprefill_a100_ue1_7c1a0b4_inflight8`.

[~50 min later]

Rina Kobayashi: run #2 done: https://bench-artifacts.redwood-internal/runs/1763615410/
- p99 TTFT: 2.41s -> 3.12s
- queue_wait_p99: 190ms -> 615ms
- backpressure_events_total: 4 -> 133
So yeah, it’s the inflight limit change.

Sofia Ivanova: great, isolated. We should treat as regression vs our current long-prefill workload definition. I’ll open a follow-up to adjust defaults: either bump inflight for “prefill-dominant” workload mode or make it adaptive (based on observed decode share).

Marcus Reed: I’ll comment on PR-21541 thread and propose we gate the default flip behind a traffic-shape detector, not unconditional. It helped mixed traffic but hurts this extreme case.

Hiro Tanaka: given the isolation, kernel path probably not the culprit here. but we should still run a kernel dump once this is fixed to ensure no silent flip. (I’ve been burned by that before.)

Yuki Sato: agreed. once we land a scheduler fix, I’ll do one kernel-dump run per SKU for long-prefill to ensure selection matches kernel matrix expectations.

Sana Farid: for the baseline program: we need a consistent stance on “what config defines baseline.” If scheduler defaults are changing, we need to re-anchor baseline with a freeze tag + config snapshot. Otherwise we’ll keep chasing moving targets.

Mateo Alvarez: aligned. We’ll pin the baseline scheduler config (and record overrides) for Q1 2026. Sofia, please add a note to the scheduler baseline doc: long-prefill suite uses inflight=16 for now pending adaptive policy.

Sean Gallagher: checked bench pool. clocks locked, no MIG changes, no anomalies. variance here is config-driven.

Omar El-Sayed: can we add an explicit metric panel on dashboard for `prefill_inflight_limit_hit` / backpressure events? would make this obvious in future.

Rina Kobayashi: +1, I had to dig into logs. I can add it to the export template / metrics dictionary.

Mateo Alvarez: next steps:
1) Sofia to propose fix + rollout plan (keep mixed-traffic gains)
2) Rina to mark the regressed datapoint in the run tracker as “expected due to inflight limit flip” + link this thread
3) Sana to update regression threshold config so that until fix lands, canary doesn’t page us repeatedly on this known issue (route to triage channel only)

Sana Farid: ack. I’ll add a temporary exception keyed by build range + workload id, with expiry (2 weeks). not turning it off entirely.

Sofia Ivanova: I’ll draft a patch: make prefill inflight limit higher when decode_target_tokens <= 512 and prompt_len_bin == long_context, OR make it proportional to expected prefill time. will share proposal in #eng-runtime later today.

Grace Kim: once scheduler fix is in, I’ll run a quick validation sweep on L40S too; we’ve seen different behavior on those cards.

Mateo Alvarez: thanks all. good triage.

source.content:
Sofia Ivanova: Kicking off the “what knobs are safe to freeze per SKU” convo. For the profile pack we need a short list we’re comfortable pinning (H100/L40S first). Everything else should stay adaptive. What do we think is in/out?

Hiro Tanaka: +1. From kernels side: I’m nervous about freezing anything that depends on seq-len distribution. If we hard-pin kernel choices and the workload shifts, we can regress badly.

Lena Fischer: Agree. Scheduler knobs are similar. Anything that assumes a stable concurrency/streaming mix can bite us.

Yuki Sato: Maybe we classify knobs:
A) “guardrail” (safe to freeze, prevents badness)
B) “policy” (freeze only if we also declare intended envelope)
C) “adaptive” (never freeze, let runtime decide)

Grace Kim: :thumbsup: for A/B/C.

Sofia Ivanova: That’s a good framing. Let’s enumerate candidate knobs.

Marcus Reed: For scheduling/batching, candidates I’ve seen people want to pin:
- `max_batch_tokens`
- `max_concurrent_sequences`
- `batcher_tick_ms` / “microbatch window”
- prefill/decode fairness weights
- queue limits / backpressure thresholds
- streaming chunking policy (size / cadence)

Omar El-Sayed: KV-cache/memory knobs people keep asking for:
- KV cache budget as % of VRAM
- eviction policy toggles (none/LRU/segment)
- fragmentation mitigation (arena size / defrag interval)
- max context length hard cap

Hiro Tanaka: Kernels:
- enable/disable specific attention kernels (flash vs “fallback”)
- GEMM algo selection toggles
- seq-len bucket thresholds
- quantization kernel selection flags
- compilation cache sizes (CPU-side)

Noah Patel: Before diving into specific keys: product requirement is “safe-by-default, measurable, easy to roll out.” That pushes us toward freezing mostly guardrails + defaults that reduce variance, not “forcing” a fast path.

Ethan Park: +1. Also from schema/guardrails angle, anything we freeze should be:
- hardware-detectable
- not requiring model-specific tuning
- not requiring customer prompt distribution knowledge

Benji Okafor: And we need clean override semantics. If we freeze too many knobs, we’ll end up with per-customer overrides = support nightmare.

Sofia Ivanova: So my initial proposal for scheduler:
SAFE (A): queue limits/backpressure, max in-flight tokens, max pending requests
MAYBE (B): max_batch_tokens, max_concurrent_sequences
NOT SAFE (C): batcher_tick_ms, fairness weights, preemption thresholds

Lena Fischer: I’d put `max_batch_tokens` as B but with pretty conservative defaults for L40S. For H100 we can go higher, but we should still let the runtime adapt down when tail latency is threatened.

Marcus Reed: +1. We can freeze caps but let the runtime pick within cap. i.e. profile says `batch_limits.max_batch_tokens=8192` but batcher can submit smaller.

Hiro Tanaka: That pattern works for kernels too:
- freeze enable/disable *families* of kernels (e.g., allow flash-attn v2/v3)
- do NOT freeze exact kernel per bucket
Let the selector choose among “allowed set.”

Yuki Sato: Exactly. “allowlist” instead of “force.” If we’re going to expose something like `kernel.autoselect.mode`, make modes broad: `safe`, `balanced`, `throughput`.

Grace Kim: Another thought: freezing seq-len bucket thresholds seems bad. But freezing *telemetry* thresholds for when we switch might be ok? Like: “if p95 prefill ms > X then fall back.”

Noah Patel: Careful: thresholds are workload-dependent too. But we can freeze guardrails that prevent worst-case behavior (OOM, runaway queueing).

Omar El-Sayed: KV-cache budgets I feel are safe-ish to freeze per SKU because they’re about not OOMing. Especially L40S: if we don’t cap KV, long-context + concurrency spikes will blow up.

Lena Fischer: L40S is the one where pinning memory guardrails is basically required.

Hiro Tanaka: Agree. For H100 (80GB) we can be looser, but still define budgets to reduce fragmentation.

Marcus Reed: Concrete list for “A”:
- `queue.max_depth` / `queue.max_wait_ms`
- `backpressure.reject_on_overload` (or shed policy)
- `memory.kv_cache.max_bytes` or `%` cap
- `memory.activation_safety_margin_bytes`
- `oom.recovery.enabled` (restart vs reject)

Ethan Park: From schema side: we can enforce “A” knobs must have validation constraints. Example: KV cache cap must be <= (vram - safety margin).

Benji Okafor: Also worth pinning logging/metrics toggles in profile? Not performance, but it helps rollout. E.g. enable profile selection metrics always.

Sofia Ivanova: That’s not exactly “per SKU tuning” but I’m fine including “observability defaults” in the pack if they’re low overhead.

Isabella Rossi: For kernels, can we freeze:
- `attention.use_flash=true`
- `attention.flash_variant=auto`
- `use_paged_kv=true`
without forcing specific kernel ids?

Hiro Tanaka: `use_flash` as allow yes, but “true” can still be unsafe for some shapes (very small batch/seq where overhead > benefit). Better: `attention.flash.allowed=true` and keep runtime autoselect.

Yuki Sato: +1. Forcing flash everywhere caused regressions in microbench earlier (small batch, short seq). Autoselect is there for a reason.

Grace Kim: What about freezing `prefill_chunking` behavior? Some customers see spiky prefill latencies.

Lena Fischer: Chunking is very workload-sensitive. I’d rather have adaptive chunking with caps. Like: max chunk size pinned per SKU to avoid L40S HBM pressure.

Marcus Reed: That maps to “freeze caps, not policy.”

Noah Patel: Can we write down a decision rule:
- Freeze caps/limits/allowlists that prevent pathological states.
- Avoid freezing timing/fairness/heuristics that require prompt mix knowledge.
- Anything that changes p95 has to be in B with explicit “intended envelope” in docs.

Sofia Ivanova: That’s basically it.

Ethan Park: For docs + schema: we should embed “intent tags” per knob. Example:
- `intent=guardrail`
- `intent=throughput`
- `intent=latency`
And block “throughput” knobs from being default-on unless profile maturity is stable.

Benji Okafor: +1. Then rollout tooling can say “don’t allow throughput knobs in Hosted default profile unless SRE signoff.”

Hiro Tanaka: Another kernel note: don’t freeze `gemm_algo_id` or `cublaslt_heuristic_override` per SKU. Driver versions change behavior. That’s a maintenance trap.

Isabella Rossi: :+1: we can expose it for debugging but not as a profile default.

Omar El-Sayed: KV-cache: freezing eviction policy? L40S might need eviction on by default to avoid OOM, but eviction can hurt latency.

Lena Fischer: Eviction policy is B, but the cap is A. If you cap aggressively enough maybe eviction isn’t needed.

Marcus Reed: Also, eviction interacts with scheduling fairness. If we evict decode-heavy sequences, you can get weird tail behavior.

Sofia Ivanova: So: default profiles pin KV budget cap (A), leave eviction adaptive or off by default; allow turning it on as B with a “long-context / bursty concurrency” envelope.

Grace Kim: Sounds right.

Yuki Sato: For H100, I’d like to freeze one thing: enable the newer attention kernel path by default (allowlist). That’s low risk if we keep fallback.

Hiro Tanaka: If it’s just “allowed” and runtime can fall back, ok.

Noah Patel: We should explicitly require a fallback path for any kernel allowlist change. Profile must declare `fallback_required=true` (or equivalent) and runtime logs when it falls back.

Ethan Park: I can add a schema rule: if profile changes `kernel.allowlist`, it must also set `kernel.fallback=enabled` and declare `min_runtime_version`.

Benji Okafor: Great. Also: for Dedicated/Private, we should keep an env var escape hatch: `RUNTIME_PROFILE_DISABLE=1`.

Sofia Ivanova: Yep. We’ll capture that in the ADR/override order.

Marcus Reed: Summary proposal (for the page + ticket):
A (safe to freeze): caps/limits/guardrails (queue limits, backpressure, KV cache cap + safety margin, max in-flight tokens)
B (freeze w/ envelope + canary): max_batch_tokens, max_concurrent_sequences, eviction policy, chunk-size caps, kernel allowlists
C (don’t freeze): batcher tick/window, fairness weights, preemption heuristics, seq-len bucket thresholds, exact kernel IDs / GEMM algo overrides

Sofia Ivanova: Works for me. I’ll paste this into the schema doc and tag @Hiro @Lena for signoff.

Hiro Tanaka: :thumbsup: from kernels.

Lena Fischer: :thumbsup: from scheduling. Let’s make sure the docs call out “caps not targets” so people don’t read it as guaranteed behavior.

Noah Patel: +1 and add “expected behavior, not guarantees” language (docs thread already tracking this).

Ethan Park: I’ll draft the “intent tags” + validation constraints proposal and link it in ENG-4822.

Benji Okafor: I’ll make sure loader supports allowlists + fallback logging cleanly (and that overrides can’t accidentally disable fallback).

Sofia Ivanova: Closing the loop: next step is turn this into a short checklist in the profile review template. Thanks all.

source.messages:
Claire Dubois: we need to settle canonical defs for the suite before we lock the manifest schema. main confusion: TTFT = “first token on wire” vs “first token produced” (server-side), and TPOT w/ or w/o client overhead. can we decide measurement boundaries today?

Rina Kobayashi: +1. harness can do either, but if we leave it ambiguous we’ll end up comparing apples/oranges across runs.

Sofia Ivanova: from runtime POV, I care about scheduler+decode behavior. “first token produced” (after queueing + prefill + first decode) is the clean signal.

Sean Gallagher: SRE POV: for release gating, we need something stable. client-side measurements can get noisy if the client can’t read fast enough, esp streaming.

Jonas Weber: also applied ML wants comparability across model variants. if TTFT includes network/client parsing, it’ll hide real runtime deltas.

Allison Grant: what’s the current SLO definition? our prod “ttft” dashboard is based on server instrumentation at the gateway (request_received -> first_token_flushed). not purely model.

Omar El-Sayed: right, and the “first_token_flushed” part matters because we’ve seen cases where token is generated but stuck behind buffering/flush behavior. tail pain.

Claire Dubois: so we have 3 candidates:
1) ttft_model: request_enqueued -> first_token_generated
2) ttft_on_wire: request_received_at_gateway -> first_token_flushed
3) ttft_client: client_send_complete -> first_token_byte_received

Rina Kobayashi: harness can capture #3 easily and (with headers) correlate to #2/#1 if runtime exports timestamps.

Sofia Ivanova: but which is THE metric for baseline tables? we can’t have 3 TTFTs everywhere.

Mateo Alvarez: propose: baseline “primary TTFT” should match what we can gate and what maps to prod SLO views. that’s #2. but we *must* also record #1 for attribution.

Sean Gallagher: +1. gating should use server-side. client-side is “debug only” because it depends on bench harness + region routing.

Jonas Weber: if we pick #2 as primary, need to be explicit about “request_received” boundary. eg do we include auth, request parsing, rate limit check? those do change occasionally.

Allison Grant: prod SLO includes gateway overhead by design. if baseline ignores it, we’ll keep shipping changes that look fine in perf suite but break SLO.

Sofia Ivanova: ok but for kernel/scheduler work, I don’t want noise from gateway changes. can we tag these layers separately?

Omar El-Sayed: we already have planned scheduler metrics: queue_wait_ms, batch_assembly_ms, prefill_ms, decode_ms. if we always record those, TTFT_on_wire becomes explainable.

Rina Kobayashi: on the TPOT question: folks keep calculating it as “(end_time - start_time)/num_tokens” with different start times.

Claire Dubois: yeah. and sometimes “client perceived” includes the time it takes the client to read the streaming chunks.

Sean Gallagher: we had a previous incident where client TPOT got worse because the harness did synchronous JSON parsing in the read loop. we don’t want that.

Rina Kobayashi: for the harness I can do:
- record raw byte arrival timestamps from the socket read, no JSON parse in the loop
- decode token boundaries based on SSE chunk boundaries (or length-delimited frames)
- compute tpot_client_wire = mean(inter-arrival between token chunks)

Sofia Ivanova: SSE chunk boundaries are not guaranteed “one chunk per token” though. we sometimes coalesce.

Omar El-Sayed: exactly. server can flush multiple tokens in one write depending on pacing config. so “client inter-arrival” is not stable unless we enforce pacing.

Jonas Weber: which we do plan to do for the representative chat workload (token pacing), right?

Rina Kobayashi: yes, but we still should define TPOT on server side by token emission events, not chunk emission events.

Allison Grant: do we have token emission timestamps in prod? (or only for perf runs)

Omar El-Sayed: we can emit token_event timestamps in perf mode (sampled), but for baseline suite it’s fine. otherwise we can approximate from decode loop stats.

Mateo Alvarez: decision framework:
- Primary metrics used for baseline comparisons + gating: server-side, not client.
- Also record “debug/e2e” metrics but not gating.

Claire Dubois: can we write the definitions down concretely? proposing:
TTFT (canonical) = t(first_token_flushed_at_gateway) - t(request_received_at_gateway)
TPOT (canonical) = (t(last_token_flushed_at_gateway) - t(first_token_flushed_at_gateway)) / (output_tokens - 1)
Throughput = output_tokens / (t(last_token_flushed_at_gateway) - t(request_received_at_gateway))

Sofia Ivanova: for non-streaming endpoints, “first_token_flushed” == “first_byte_of_response”. do we still call it TTFT?

Rina Kobayashi: in harness output I can keep the name TTFT but annotate mode=streaming|non_streaming. (or store ttft_ms for both; semantics described in methodology doc)

Sean Gallagher: ok as long as doc is explicit: non-streaming TTFT behaves like TTFB.

Jonas Weber: and for embeddings there’s no tokens. so just latency_ms and qps/req_s.

Allison Grant: small nit: request_received_at_gateway should be AFTER connection reuse, no DNS/TLS. i.e., we require keepalive in the harness.

Rina Kobayashi: yes. harness uses warm connections, and we’ll document: exclude DNS + TCP + TLS by reusing sessions.

Claire Dubois: back to #1 vs #2. we still want model-only for attribution:
TTFT_model = t(first_token_generated) - t(request_enqueued)
TPOT_model = (t(last_token_generated) - t(first_token_generated)) / (output_tokens - 1)

Omar El-Sayed: request_enqueued should be “enter scheduler queue” not “arrived at gateway”. otherwise queue_wait_ms double counts.

Sofia Ivanova: yes: enqueue_ts = when scheduler accepts it into queue. (after admission control)

Mateo Alvarez: ok. we can live with two sets:
- ttft_ms / tpot_ms = gateway wire-level canonical
- ttft_model_ms / tpot_model_ms = runtime attribution
plus optional e2e_client_ttft_ms, e2e_client_tpot_ms as informational.

Sean Gallagher: and we should state explicitly: TPOT excludes client read/parse and excludes pauses due to client backpressure; if client stalls, we treat that as harness issue.

Rina Kobayashi: I’ll add a guardrail: if we detect client read loop lagging (buffer grows / event loop blocked), we flag the run as invalid.

Allison Grant: can we align naming with metrics dictionary? prefer:
- ttft_ms (wire)
- tpot_ms (wire)
- ttft_server_ms (model)
- tpot_server_ms (model)

Sofia Ivanova: “server” is ambiguous (gateway vs runtime). maybe:
- ttft_wire_ms
- tpot_wire_ms
- ttft_model_ms
- tpot_model_ms

Claire Dubois: +1, clearer.

Jonas Weber: also need to define token count. output_tokens should be “generated tokens” excluding prompt. and for streaming, include the final token? (output_tokens-1 divisor)

Omar El-Sayed: standard: TPOT uses (N-1) intervals between N tokens. if N<2, TPOT is undefined; set null/NaN and omit from aggregates.

Rina Kobayashi: in results export I’ll emit tpot_* only when output_tokens >= 2, else empty.

Mateo Alvarez: sounds good. this is basically what we want in ADR-0172.

Claire Dubois: ok decision:
1) Canonical baseline + gating metrics: wire-level at gateway: ttft_wire_ms, tpot_wire_ms, tokens_per_sec, plus p50/p95/p99.
2) Required attribution metrics: runtime/model-level: ttft_model_ms, tpot_model_ms + queue_wait_ms + batch_assembly_ms + prefill_ms + decode_ms.
3) Optional: client e2e (not gating).
4) Connection setup excluded (keepalive required).

Sean Gallagher: :thumbsup: please make sure methodology doc says “first token on wire = first token flushed by gateway” not “first token received by client”. that distinction keeps SRE sane.

Allison Grant: +1. I’ll sanity check with the SLO dashboard mapping doc. prod uses gateway_received -> first_token_flushed.

Rina Kobayashi: action items:
- I’ll update manifest schema fields + results exporter names to *_wire_ms and *_model_ms.
- add a note in harness: non-streaming TTFT == first_byte.

Claire Dubois: I’ll update methodology page + add examples.

Omar El-Sayed: I’ll confirm we have the needed runtime timestamps behind perf flag and that we don’t add overhead in prod path.

Mateo Alvarez: locking this for Q1 baseline. any objections, speak now.

Sofia Ivanova: no objections.

Jonas Weber: good.

Sean Gallagher: good.

Allison Grant: good.

title: #eng-runtime: 1763261084
preview:
Claire Dubois: we need to settle canonical defs for the suite before we lock the manifest schema. main confusion: TTFT = “first token on wire” vs “first token produced” (server-side), and TPOT w/ or w/o client overhead. can we decide measurement boundaries today?

Rina Kobayashi: +1. harness can do either, but if we leave it ambiguous we’ll end up comparing apples/oranges across runs.

Sofia Ivanova: from runtime POV, I care about scheduler+decode behavior. “first token produced” (after queueing + prefill + first decode) is the clean signal.

Sean Gallagher: SRE POV: for release gating, we need something stable. client-side measurements can get noisy if the client can’t read fast enough, esp streaming.

Jonas Weber: also applied ML wants comparability across model variants. if TTFT includes network/client parsing, it’ll hide real runtime deltas.

Allison Grant: what’s the current SLO definition? our prod “ttft” dashboard is based on server instrumentation at the gateway (request_received -> first_token_flushed). not purely model.

Omar El-Sayed: right, and the “first_token_flushed” part matters because we’ve seen cases where token is generated but stuck behind buffering/flush behavior. tail pain.

Claire Dubois: so we have 3 candidates:
1) ttft_model: request_enqueued -> first_token_generated
2) ttft_on_wire: request_received_at_gateway -> first_token_flushed
3) ttft_client: client_send_complete -> first_token_byte_received

Rina Kobayashi: harness can capture #3 easily and (with headers) correlate to #2/#1 if runtime exports timestamps.

Sofia Ivanova: but which is THE metric for baseline tables? we can’t have 3 TTFTs everywhere.

Mateo Alvarez: propose: baseline “primary TTFT” should match what we can gate and what maps to prod SLO views. that’s #2. but we *must* also record #1 for attribution.

Sean Gallagher: +1. gating should use server-side. client-side is “debug only” because it depends on bench harness + region routing.

Jonas Weber: if we pick #2 as primary, need to be explicit about “request_received” boundary. eg do we include auth, request parsing, rate limit check? those do change occasionally.

Allison Grant: prod SLO includes gateway overhead by design. if baseline ignores it, we’ll keep shipping changes that look fine in perf suite but break SLO.

Sofia Ivanova: ok but for kernel/scheduler work, I don’t want noise from gateway changes. can we tag these layers separately?

Omar El-Sayed: we already have planned scheduler metrics: queue_wait_ms, batch_assembly_ms, prefill_ms, decode_ms. if we always record those, TTFT_on_wire becomes explainable.

Rina Kobayashi: on the TPOT question: folks keep calculating it as “(end_time - start_time)/num_tokens” with different start times.

Claire Dubois: yeah. and sometimes “client perceived” includes the time it takes the client to read the streaming chunks.

Sean Gallagher: we had a previous incident where client TPOT got worse because the harness did synchronous JSON parsing in the read loop. we don’t want that.

Rina Kobayashi: for the harness I can do:
- record raw byte arrival timestamps from the socket read, no JSON parse in the loop
- decode token boundaries based on SSE chunk boundaries (or length-delimited frames)
- compute tpot_client_wire = mean(inter-arrival between token chunks)

Sofia Ivanova: SSE chunk boundaries are not guaranteed “one chunk per token” though. we sometimes coalesce.

Omar El-Sayed: exactly. server can flush multiple tokens in one write depending on pacing config. so “client inter-arrival” is not stable unless we enforce pacing.

Jonas Weber: which we do plan to do for the representative chat workload (token pacing), right?

Rina Kobayashi: yes, but we still should define TPOT on server side by token emission events, not chunk emission events.

Allison Grant: do we have token emission timestamps in prod? (or only for perf runs)

Omar El-Sayed: we can emit token_event timestamps in perf mode (sampled), but for baseline suite it’s fine. otherwise we can approximate from decode loop stats.

Mateo Alvarez: decision framework:
- Primary metrics used for baseline comparisons + gating: server-side, not client.
- Also record “debug/e2e” metrics but not gating.

Claire Dubois: can we write the definitions down concretely? proposing:
TTFT (canonical) = t(first_token_flushed_at_gateway) - t(request_received_at_gateway)
TPOT (canonical) = (t(last_token_flushed_at_gateway) - t(first_token_flushed_at_gateway)) / (output_tokens - 1)
Throughput = output_tokens / (t(last_token_flushed_at_gateway) - t(request_received_at_gateway))

Sofia Ivanova: for non-streaming endpoints, “first_token_flushed” == “first_byte_of_response”. do we still call it TTFT?

Rina Kobayashi: in harness output I can keep the name TTFT but annotate mode=streaming|non_streaming. (or store ttft_ms for both; semantics described in methodology doc)

Sean Gallagher: ok as long as doc is explicit: non-streaming TTFT behaves like TTFB.

Jonas Weber: and for embeddings there’s no tokens. so just latency_ms and qps/req_s.

Allison Grant: small nit: request_received_at_gateway should be AFTER connection reuse, no DNS/TLS. i.e., we require keepalive in the harness.

Rina Kobayashi: yes. harness uses warm connections, and we’ll document: exclude DNS + TCP + TLS by reusing sessions.

Claire Dubois: back to #1 vs #2. we still want model-only for attribution:
TTFT_model = t(first_token_generated) - t(request_enqueued)
TPOT_model = (t(last_token_generated) - t(first_token_generated)) / (output_tokens - 1)

Omar El-Sayed: request_enqueued should be “enter scheduler queue” not “arrived at gateway”. otherwise queue_wait_ms double counts.

Sofia Ivanova: yes: enqueue_ts = when scheduler accepts it into queue. (after admission control)

Mateo Alvarez: ok. we can live with two sets:
- ttft_ms / tpot_ms = gateway wire-level canonical
- ttft_model_ms / tpot_model_ms = runtime attribution
plus optional e2e_client_ttft_ms, e2e_client_tpot_ms as informational.

Sean Gallagher: and we should state explicitly: TPOT excludes client read/parse and excludes pauses due to client backpressure; if client stalls, we treat that as harness issue.

Rina Kobayashi: I’ll add a guardrail: if we detect client read loop lagging (buffer grows / event loop blocked), we flag the run as invalid.

Allison Grant: can we align naming with metrics dictionary? prefer:
- ttft_ms (wire)
- tpot_ms (wire)
- ttft_server_ms (model)
- tpot_server_ms (model)

Sofia Ivanova: “server” is ambiguous (gateway vs runtime). maybe:
- ttft_wire_ms
- tpot_wire_ms
- ttft_model_ms
- tpot_model_ms

Claire Dubois: +1, clearer.

Jonas Weber: also need to define token count. output_tokens should be “generated tokens” excluding prompt. and for streaming, include the final token? (output_tokens-1 divisor)

Omar El-Sayed: standard: TPOT uses (N-1) intervals between N tokens. if N<2, TPOT is undefined; set null/NaN and omit from aggregates.

Rina Kobayashi: in results export I’ll emit tpot_* only when output_tokens >= 2, else empty.

Mateo Alvarez: sounds good. this is basically what we want in ADR-0172.

Claire Dubois: ok decision:
1) Canonical baseline + gating metrics: wire-level at gateway: ttft_wire_ms, tpot_wire_ms, tokens_per_sec, plus p50/p95/p99.
2) Required attribution metrics: runtime/model-level: ttft_model_ms, tpot_model_ms + queue_wait_ms + batch_assembly_ms + prefill_ms + decode_ms.
3) Optional: client e2e (not gating).
4) Connection setup excluded (keepalive required).

Sean Gallagher: :thumbsup: please make sure methodology doc says “first token on wire = first token flushed by gateway” not “first token received by client”. that distinction keeps SRE sane.

Allison Grant: +1. I’ll sanity check with the SLO dashboard mapping doc. prod uses gateway_received -> first_token_flushed.

Rina Kobayashi: action items:
- I’ll update manifest schema fields + results exporter names to *_wire_ms and *_model_ms.
- add a note in harness: non-streaming TTFT == first_byte.

Claire Dubois: I’ll update methodology page + add examples.

Omar El-Sayed: I’ll confirm we have the needed runtime timestamps behind perf flag and that we don’t add overhead in prod path.

Mateo Alvarez: locking this for Q1 baseline. any objections, speak now.

Sofia Ivanova: no objections.

Jonas Weber: good.

Sean Gallagher: good.

Allison Grant: good.

source.body:
Talia Benson: Kicking off the “hardware tuning profiles pack” workstream. Goal: ship a versioned pack of safe-by-default runtime configs tuned per GPU SKU (H100 / L40S initially) + rollout/rollback guardrails. Can we confirm owners + where the draft schema doc lives?

Talia Benson: Proposed initial scope for v1:
- Profile schema + validation + loader + override order
- H100 + L40S tuned presets (scheduler/batching, kernel-selection toggles, KV cache / memory behavior)
- Benchmark suite updates + workload matrix + perf canary gates
- Docs: expected behavior + failure modes + rollout runbook
Non-goals (v1): new kernels from scratch, bespoke per-customer tuning.

Hiro Tanaka: :thumbsup: for scope. Kernels team can own H100 kernel-selection heuristics + any microbench-driven toggles we’re confident are safe to freeze. Suggest we keep “adaptive” knobs out of the profile unless we have hard guardrails.

Sofia Ivanova: Scheduling side ok. We can propose 2 modes per SKU (balanced vs throughput-first) but we need to be explicit about tail latency impact. Also please add “streaming stability” to acceptance checks.

Ethan Park: Schema doc is in Confluence draft (WIP): https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/ENGSRV/Profile+Schema+and+Compatibility+Rules
I’ll take schema + compatibility rules + validation checklist. Benji can own loader + precedence if that works.

Benji Okafor: Yep, I can own runtime loader + override order + safe fallback behavior. We should nail down precedence early (fleet default vs per-pool vs env var vs per-route) so we don’t ship something we’ll regret.

Noah Patel: +1. Also suggest we log the effective profile selection at startup + expose a debug endpoint for oncall. Otherwise we’ll spend weeks arguing “which profile was actually applied” in incidents.

Rina Kobayashi: Perf suite: Claire + I can own. We’ll need a workload matrix that includes:
- seq len buckets (prompt + completion)
- concurrency sweep
- streaming vs non-streaming
- at least 2 model families (one dense, one MoE if available)
We can produce baseline curves per GPU before we start tweaking knobs.

Claire Dubois: Agree. Also we should pin prompt sets + runner versions for reproducibility. I’ll draft the workload matrix today and link it here.

Irene Choi: Infra angle: initial target SKUs = H100 + L40S. B200 should be gated/experimental until we have stable access + we’ve validated driver/CUDA constraints. We can plan staged rollout by pool/region once perf canary gates are in.

Vanessa Ortiz: +1 on gating B200. Fleet detection is messy too (different providers stringify SKUs differently). Please build normalization into detection logic.

Mateo Alvarez: Aligning with leadership expectations: ship H100/L40S profiles first, treat B200 as “experimental behind a flag” with explicit rollback plan. Don’t block the pack release on B200.

Omar El-Sayed: I can help on KV cache / memory behavior presets. L40S needs strict budgeting defaults + OOM prevention guardrails; H100 can be more aggressive but we should still define max cache footprint + fragmentation mitigation.

Elliot Price: Docs: I can pair with whoever owns each profile to capture “expected behavior” vs “guarantees” (we should be careful in wording). Once schema is stable, I’ll start a doc outline for reference pages per SKU.

Talia Benson: Great. Capturing owners:
- Schema/compat: Ethan
- Loader/override order: Benji (with Noah advising on debug/observability)
- Kernel tuning: Hiro (Grace/Yuki/Isabella contributing)
- Scheduling presets: Sofia (Lena/Marcus contributing)
- Perf suite + workload matrix: Rina + Claire
- KV cache/memory presets: Omar (with Samir)
- Rollout sponsor: Irene + Vanessa
- Docs program: Elliot

Talia Benson: Decision check: v1 targets H100 + L40S. B200 is gated/experimental only. Everyone ok? (react with :thumbsup:)

Hiro Tanaka: :thumbsup:

Sofia Ivanova: :thumbsup:

Ethan Park: :thumbsup:

Benji Okafor: :thumbsup:

Rina Kobayashi: :thumbsup:

Claire Dubois: :thumbsup:

Irene Choi: :thumbsup:

Mateo Alvarez: :thumbsup:

Talia Benson: Next steps:
1) Ethan posts schema WIP sections + open questions by EOW
2) Claire posts workload matrix draft today; Rina adds runner details
3) Benji drafts precedence order proposal + fallback behavior
4) Hiro/Sofia propose initial “safe knobs to freeze” list per SKU
We’ll do a 30m weekly sync; calendar invite coming.

Vanessa Ortiz: Please also add an action item: “list of SKU string variants” once we have first logs from each pool. Happy to provide what we’ve seen in fleet telemetry.

Noah Patel: One more: we should define acceptance metrics now (p50/p95 latency, tokens/sec, OOM rate, retry rate) and what counts as a regression. Otherwise perf canary gates will be bikesheddy.

Talia Benson: Good call. I’ll draft success metrics + regression thresholds strawman and share in thread before weekly. (also adding Vanessa’s SKU variants item).

text:
Talia Benson: Kicking off the “hardware tuning profiles pack” workstream. Goal: ship a versioned pack of safe-by-default runtime configs tuned per GPU SKU (H100 / L40S initially) + rollout/rollback guardrails. Can we confirm owners + where the draft schema doc lives?

Talia Benson: Proposed initial scope for v1:
- Profile schema + validation + loader + override order
- H100 + L40S tuned presets (scheduler/batching, kernel-selection toggles, KV cache / memory behavior)
- Benchmark suite updates + workload matrix + perf canary gates
- Docs: expected behavior + failure modes + rollout runbook
Non-goals (v1): new kernels from scratch, bespoke per-customer tuning.

Hiro Tanaka: :thumbsup: for scope. Kernels team can own H100 kernel-selection heuristics + any microbench-driven toggles we’re confident are safe to freeze. Suggest we keep “adaptive” knobs out of the profile unless we have hard guardrails.

Sofia Ivanova: Scheduling side ok. We can propose 2 modes per SKU (balanced vs throughput-first) but we need to be explicit about tail latency impact. Also please add “streaming stability” to acceptance checks.

Ethan Park: Schema doc is in Confluence draft (WIP): https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/ENGSRV/Profile+Schema+and+Compatibility+Rules
I’ll take schema + compatibility rules + validation checklist. Benji can own loader + precedence if that works.

Benji Okafor: Yep, I can own runtime loader + override order + safe fallback behavior. We should nail down precedence early (fleet default vs per-pool vs env var vs per-route) so we don’t ship something we’ll regret.

Noah Patel: +1. Also suggest we log the effective profile selection at startup + expose a debug endpoint for oncall. Otherwise we’ll spend weeks arguing “which profile was actually applied” in incidents.

Rina Kobayashi: Perf suite: Claire + I can own. We’ll need a workload matrix that includes:
- seq len buckets (prompt + completion)
- concurrency sweep
- streaming vs non-streaming
- at least 2 model families (one dense, one MoE if available)
We can produce baseline curves per GPU before we start tweaking knobs.

Claire Dubois: Agree. Also we should pin prompt sets + runner versions for reproducibility. I’ll draft the workload matrix today and link it here.

Irene Choi: Infra angle: initial target SKUs = H100 + L40S. B200 should be gated/experimental until we have stable access + we’ve validated driver/CUDA constraints. We can plan staged rollout by pool/region once perf canary gates are in.

Vanessa Ortiz: +1 on gating B200. Fleet detection is messy too (different providers stringify SKUs differently). Please build normalization into detection logic.

Mateo Alvarez: Aligning with leadership expectations: ship H100/L40S profiles first, treat B200 as “experimental behind a flag” with explicit rollback plan. Don’t block the pack release on B200.

Omar El-Sayed: I can help on KV cache / memory behavior presets. L40S needs strict budgeting defaults + OOM prevention guardrails; H100 can be more aggressive but we should still define max cache footprint + fragmentation mitigation.

Elliot Price: Docs: I can pair with whoever owns each profile to capture “expected behavior” vs “guarantees” (we should be careful in wording). Once schema is stable, I’ll start a doc outline for reference pages per SKU.

Talia Benson: Great. Capturing owners:
- Schema/compat: Ethan
- Loader/override order: Benji (with Noah advising on debug/observability)
- Kernel tuning: Hiro (Grace/Yuki/Isabella contributing)
- Scheduling presets: Sofia (Lena/Marcus contributing)
- Perf suite + workload matrix: Rina + Claire
- KV cache/memory presets: Omar (with Samir)
- Rollout sponsor: Irene + Vanessa
- Docs program: Elliot

Talia Benson: Decision check: v1 targets H100 + L40S. B200 is gated/experimental only. Everyone ok? (react with :thumbsup:)

Hiro Tanaka: :thumbsup:

Sofia Ivanova: :thumbsup:

Ethan Park: :thumbsup:

Benji Okafor: :thumbsup:

Rina Kobayashi: :thumbsup:

Claire Dubois: :thumbsup:

Irene Choi: :thumbsup:

Mateo Alvarez: :thumbsup:

Talia Benson: Next steps:
1) Ethan posts schema WIP sections + open questions by EOW
2) Claire posts workload matrix draft today; Rina adds runner details
3) Benji drafts precedence order proposal + fallback behavior
4) Hiro/Sofia propose initial “safe knobs to freeze” list per SKU
We’ll do a 30m weekly sync; calendar invite coming.

Vanessa Ortiz: Please also add an action item: “list of SKU string variants” once we have first logs from each pool. Happy to provide what we’ve seen in fleet telemetry.

Noah Patel: One more: we should define acceptance metrics now (p50/p95 latency, tokens/sec, OOM rate, retry rate) and what counts as a regression. Otherwise perf canary gates will be bikesheddy.

Talia Benson: Good call. I’ll draft success metrics + regression thresholds strawman and share in thread before weekly. (also adding Vanessa’s SKU variants item).

source.messages:
Mateo Alvarez: kicking off the Q1’26 perf baseline + representative workload suite work. want to get owners + logistics locked today so we can start runs next week.

Mateo Alvarez: umbrella Linear is here: https://linear.app/redwood/issue/ENG-4821/q1-2026-perf-baseline-kickoff-and-scope

Mateo Alvarez: initial scope (v0): top models (llama/qwen/mistral families), regions us-east + eu-west, SKUs H100/A100/L40S. workloads: chat streaming, long-prefill summarization, tool calling (bursty multi-turn), embeddings.

Talia Benson: :wave: I can run the weekly cadence + notes. what’s the target day/time for the standing sync?

Mateo Alvarez: thinking Tue 10am PT, 45 min. owners please confirm.

Rina Kobayashi: confirmed. I’ll own harness + run orchestration. ENG-4822 is the schema work (manifest/results). will post a checklist later today.

Claire Dubois: confirming on methodology. I’ll write the measurement boundaries + noise model assumptions doc, and we’ll align w/ perf regression policy.

Sofia Ivanova: scheduling side: I’ll own baseline scheduler config + the instrumentation requests (queue wait / batch assembly / load shed). already started a doc w/ proposed counters.

Hiro Tanaka: kernels: I’ll cover kernel auto-selection rules + known cliffs. Yuki is out today but I’ll loop them in on kernel pinning questions.

Sean Gallagher: SRE: I’ll take benchmark fleet stability / run hygiene. strong preference we define “standard warmup + isolation steps” before we burn too many GPU hours.

Sergio Costa: dashboards: I can wire the baseline dashboard panels once we know the exact metric tags + where results live. need a stable run naming convention.

Connor O'Brien: release gating: I’ll coordinate perf-canary integration once the suite is runnable end-to-end. we’ll need a “dry run” phase before any hard gate.

Kira Thompson: I can take runbook / docs (reruns, triage, how to repro locally vs in the fleet). just point me at the decisions once they’re made.

Jonas Weber: applied ML/evals here. I’ll help validate workload representativeness + add the lightweight quality spotcheck hook (must-not-break set).

Mateo Alvarez: great. two immediate logistics questions:
1) where do baseline run artifacts live (manifests, raw metrics, traces)?
2) what’s the canonical “source of truth” doc set (confluence vs drive vs repo docs)?

Rina Kobayashi: for artifacts, my proposal:
- manifests + summarized metrics in `benchmark-lab` repo outputs (JSON/Parquet) pushed to `s3://redwood-benchmark-artifacts/prod/perf-suite/…`
- each run writes `run_manifest.json`, `results.json`, and optional `traces/` directory (nsys, chrome trace, scheduler logs)
- we store a short index row in a “run registry” table (so dashboards can query by tags)

Sean Gallagher: +1 S3. please avoid “everyone uploads to Drive” for primary artifacts; Drive is fine for analysis scratch but not the source.

Sergio Costa: dashboards are happiest if there’s a single table-like thing (even if it’s just parquet w/ partitions) keyed by `{run_id, model, region, sku, workload, runtime_sha, manifest_version}`.

Claire Dubois: doc-wise: I’d like confluence to hold the frozen methodology + baseline report, but repo should have the runnable spec (workloads) + schema in version control.

Mateo Alvarez: makes sense. can we set a single folder path convention now?

Rina Kobayashi: yep. propose:
`perf-suite/v1/{year}-{month}/run_id={uuid}/…`
and include a `baseline_anchor` tag for the baseline freeze runs.

Marcus Reed: jumping in—please include the runtime build ID and container digest in the manifest. we’ve been burned by “same SHA, different image” before.

Rina Kobayashi: already in my draft. manifest will capture:
- runtime git SHA
- container digest
- driver/CUDA versions
- region/zone
- GPU SKU + count
- scheduler config blob hash
- workload seed + params

Allison Grant: observability note: let’s align metric names w/ existing prod conventions (ttft_ms, tpot_ms, tokens_per_sec, queue_wait_ms) so dashboards don’t fork.

Aisha Bello: +1. also please explicitly define TTFT boundary for streaming (server first token generated vs first token on wire). SLO dashboards currently use “first token on wire”.

Claire Dubois: I’ll capture that in the methodology doc + propose we report both if needed (gen vs wire). but we need a single gating metric.

Mateo Alvarez: for this kickoff, let’s pick a default:
- TTFT = first token on wire (server-side timestamp), excluding client read delay
- TPOT = avg per-output-token time over streamed tokens (server-side), excluding network
if anyone strongly disagrees, flag now.

Sofia Ivanova: ok w/ that. we should add scheduler timestamps so we can break down TTFT into queue_wait + batch_assembly + prefill.

Hiro Tanaka: ok. for kernel dumps, we’ll want to annotate the prefill length regime + selected kernel + fallback reason. can be sampled (not every request).

Logan Wright: data pipeline question: who’s owning the “run registry” table? happy to help but need a clear interface.

Rina Kobayashi: I can own the initial registry writer in the harness. might need your help making it queryable in our standard analytics stack.

Logan Wright: cool. loop me in once schema is stable. if it’s parquet in S3 w/ partitioning, we can hook it into the existing query layer pretty quickly.

Talia Benson: re cadence: Tue 10am PT works. I’ll send a calendar invite + agenda doc (owners, decisions, run status).

Mateo Alvarez: action items:
- Rina: post artifact storage spec + run naming convention in ENG-4821
- Claire: start methodology doc outline + measurement boundaries (TTFT/TPOT) and link in ENG-4821
- Sean: propose benchmark fleet “standard operating procedure” (warmup, clock settings if any, isolation)
- Sergio/Allison/Aisha: agree on metric tags + dashboard minimum viable panels
- Connor: draft gating phases (PR vs nightly vs release branch) but no hard gate until noise model validated

Linear Bot: ENG-4821 updated by Mateo Alvarez

Kira Thompson: once those docs exist, I’ll start the runbook skeleton: “how to rerun baseline”, “how to triage regression”, “how to attach traces”.

Sean Gallagher: quick note: we should reserve benchmark fleet windows for the baseline sweep. if Neha already approved budget, we still need an internal booking process to avoid contention.

Mateo Alvarez: yep. I’ll ping Neha + infra today and we’ll get a reservation doc. Sean can you drop a strawman for required GPU-hours (H100/A100/L40S) based on your earlier estimates?

Sean Gallagher: will do. I’ll post in-thread later today.

Jonas Weber: also: please don’t forget “cold vs warm cache” decision. telemetry says a meaningful fraction of prod has prefix cache hits; we probably need both in reporting.

Mateo Alvarez: agreed, but let’s not block kickoff. we’ll run cold by default for first pass, and add warm scenario once caching knobs are stable. Jonas/Claire can you propose how to define “warm” reproducibly?

Claire Dubois: yep. definition should be “fixed cache priming sequence + fixed seed” (no real prompt content). will draft.

Rina Kobayashi: I’ll add a harness flag: `--cache_mode=cold|warm` with warm executing a deterministic priming phase before measurement.

Sergio Costa: for dashboards, I’ll treat cache_mode as a top-level filter so folks don’t mix the distributions.

Mateo Alvarez: perfect. thanks all—let’s keep updates on ENG-4821 and use this thread for quick decisions. :thumbsup: (reactions: :thumbsup: from Rina Kobayashi, Sofia Ivanova, Sean Gallagher, Claire Dubois)

source.body:
Sofia Ivanova: digging into yesterday’s mixed-workload behavior (long prompt prefill + short chat) and it looks like our backpressure thresholds are too forgiving for long-prefill. tail latency shoots up *before* admission control kicks in.

Julian Moore: do we have numbers? was this us-east canary or eu-west?

Sofia Ivanova: us-east canary. from the runtime side: when we get a couple of 12–20k prompt requests, they monopolize prefill slots and we start building scheduler lag for everyone.

Sofia Ivanova: quick snapshot from metrics (5m window during burst):
- `runtime.scheduler_lag_ms_p95` went 45ms -> 650ms
- `runtime.queue_depth` flat-ish at ~40–60 (so the queue depth trigger didn’t fire)
- `runtime.prefill_inflight` pegged at max (8)
- `ttft_ms_p95` for “normal” chat (1–2k prompts) went 900ms -> 3.8s

Vikram Singh: that lines up with what routing saw. queue depth wasn’t huge, but tokens-in-flight was. we were gating on queue depth and shedding best-effort too late.

Robert King: +1. capacity signal right now is basically `queue_depth` and `gpu_busy`. but gpu_busy stays high even in “healthy” batching, so it’s noisy. scheduler lag looks like a better leading indicator.

Lena Fischer: also note: long prefill requests don’t show up as “queue depth” because they’re *admitted* and then just occupy the prefill stage longer. so admission control based on queue depth misses the pain.

Mateo Alvarez: so we need a new trigger or adjust thresholds. what’s the minimal change? (don’t want to overfit)

Sofia Ivanova: proposal: lower backpressure threshold when the request mix is “long prompt heavy”. basically a guardrail on prefill occupancy.

Sofia Ivanova: concrete idea:
- today: backpressure starts when `queue_depth > 120` for 3s (example), and shedding starts at `queue_depth > 200`
- new: also start backpressure when `prefill_inflight >= (max_prefill_inflight - 1)` *and* `scheduler_lag_ms_p95 > 250ms` for 5s
- and for shedding best-effort: if `prefill_inflight == max` and `scheduler_lag_ms_p95 > 500ms` for 10s

Julian Moore: we have per-route info on “this is long prompt”?

Sofia Ivanova: not reliably at runtime without parsing, but we have prompt token estimate in the request metadata (from gateway) on the hot routes now. it’s in the envelope. (we already use it for some batching heuristics)

Peter Holtz: if we wire prompt token estimate into the runtime scheduler, can we avoid per-request high-cardinality tags? SRE is going to scream if we start emitting “prompt_len=12345”

Julian Moore: yeah we should bucket it. like `prompt_len_bucket={0-1k,1-4k,4-8k,8k+}` only.

Lena Fischer: +1 buckets. also, I’d prefer we *don’t* fully rely on gateway estimate for the trigger. can we just use prefill occupancy + lag? that’s intrinsic.

Vikram Singh: routing/policy wants deterministic reasons. if we shed, we need to explain “overload:prefill_saturation” vs “overload:queue_depth”. that’s fine.

Sofia Ivanova: yep. no need for prompt length in the trigger. prompt length comes in when we decide who to backpressure first (best-effort + very long prompts) if we want.

Mateo Alvarez: what’s the risk of oscillation? lag spikes can be noisy.

Sofia Ivanova: we’d add hysteresis (time-based) and release threshold lower than engage threshold. e.g.
- engage when `lag_p95 > 500ms` for 10s and prefill full
- release when `lag_p95 < 250ms` for 30s OR prefill_inflight <= max-2

Julian Moore: can you share the actual timeline from the burst? would help tune.

Sofia Ivanova: pasted from my notes (times relative):
- T+0: 6 long-prefill requests arrive (enterprise + best-effort mixed)
- T+8s: prefill_inflight hits 8/8
- T+20s: lag p95 crosses 300ms
- T+35s: ttft p95 starts climbing rapidly
- T+60s: queue_depth finally crosses 120 (too late)
- T+75s: routing starts throttling best-effort, but enterprise already impacted

Robert King: that’s pretty clear. gating on queue depth is just late for this failure mode.

Peter Holtz: also, this maps to the incident thread where we saw “flat queue depth but tail latency rising” and couldn’t explain it quickly.

Lena Fischer: implementation-wise: we already compute scheduler lag. adding a boolean “prefill_saturated” signal exported to routing would be cheap.

Vikram Singh: if you can export `prefill_saturated` + lag, routing can treat it as capacity pressure and preserve enterprise budget (shed best-effort earlier). that’s exactly what hot-route protection wants.

Mateo Alvarez: action items?

Sofia Ivanova: I can take a small PR:
1) add `runtime.prefill_saturated` gauge (0/1) and `runtime.prefill_inflight` already exists
2) add `runtime.backpressure_mode` enum-ish metric (careful) or just counters when we enter/exit
3) implement new admission guard: when saturated+lag, slow accept for best-effort queue first

Julian Moore: for (2) please don’t do free-form enum labels. maybe `runtime.backpressure_enter_total{reason=...}` with a small bounded set: `queue_depth`, `prefill_saturation`, `manual`.

Peter Holtz: +1. also add a log line in the admission decision path with reason and thresholds (sampled).

Lena Fischer: do we need to adjust thresholds specifically for “long prompts”? or are we just adding the missing signal?

Sofia Ivanova: mostly missing signal. but I also want to tweak the existing “prefill max” from 8 to 6 under mixed workloads. long-prefill concurrency of 8 is just too high; it starves decode stage.

Mateo Alvarez: lowering max prefill concurrency is risky for throughput on pure prefill workloads (embeddings-ish). can we make it conditional?

Sofia Ivanova: conditional on observed decode queue starvation. we can do a simple heuristic: if `decode_queue_depth > X` and `prefill_inflight` high, clamp prefill concurrency down temporarily.

Vikram Singh: that’s getting close to scheduler policy changes. but even just exporting the saturation signal + earlier backpressure might be enough for this project.

Sofia Ivanova: agreed. I’ll keep the first PR to signals + trigger. leave the dynamic clamp for later unless we keep seeing it.

Julian Moore: any chance we can reproduce in load test? I’d like a deterministic scenario.

Sofia Ivanova: yes. scenario:
- 70% short chat (1–2k prompt, 256 out)
- 30% long prompt (12–16k prompt, 256 out)
- burst factor 3x for 90s
success criteria: enterprise chat ttft p95 stays < 1.5s and lag p95 < 300ms while best-effort gets 429s.

Robert King: if you put that in the runtime load harness, routing can reuse it in the game day.

Mateo Alvarez: ok. Sofia owns PR for runtime signals + new trigger. Vikram/Robert to wire into routing policy once signal is present. Peter please sanity-check metrics/cardinality before merge.

Peter Holtz: will do. also pls add to the runbook: “if queue depth looks fine, check scheduler lag + prefill saturation”.

Sofia Ivanova: ack. I’ll start a PR today. will ping when up for review.

Julian Moore: :eyes: when you have a diff, tag me for the metrics bits.

Lena Fischer: :thumbsup: and I’ll review the trigger logic/hysteresis.

Sofia Ivanova: thanks all.

title: #eng-runtime: 1763080129
preview:
Sofia Ivanova: digging into yesterday’s mixed-workload behavior (long prompt prefill + short chat) and it looks like our backpressure thresholds are too forgiving for long-prefill. tail latency shoots up *before* admission control kicks in.

Julian Moore: do we have numbers? was this us-east canary or eu-west?

Sofia Ivanova: us-east canary. from the runtime side: when we get a couple of 12–20k prompt requests, they monopolize prefill slots and we start building scheduler lag for everyone.

Sofia Ivanova: quick snapshot from metrics (5m window during burst):
- `runtime.scheduler_lag_ms_p95` went 45ms -> 650ms
- `runtime.queue_depth` flat-ish at ~40–60 (so the queue depth trigger didn’t fire)
- `runtime.prefill_inflight` pegged at max (8)
- `ttft_ms_p95` for “normal” chat (1–2k prompts) went 900ms -> 3.8s

Vikram Singh: that lines up with what routing saw. queue depth wasn’t huge, but tokens-in-flight was. we were gating on queue depth and shedding best-effort too late.

Robert King: +1. capacity signal right now is basically `queue_depth` and `gpu_busy`. but gpu_busy stays high even in “healthy” batching, so it’s noisy. scheduler lag looks like a better leading indicator.

Lena Fischer: also note: long prefill requests don’t show up as “queue depth” because they’re *admitted* and then just occupy the prefill stage longer. so admission control based on queue depth misses the pain.

Mateo Alvarez: so we need a new trigger or adjust thresholds. what’s the minimal change? (don’t want to overfit)

Sofia Ivanova: proposal: lower backpressure threshold when the request mix is “long prompt heavy”. basically a guardrail on prefill occupancy.

- today: backpressure starts when `queue_depth > 120` for 3s (example), and shedding starts at `queue_depth > 200`

Mateo Alvarez: what’s the risk of oscillation? lag spikes can be noisy.

Sofia Ivanova: we’d add hysteresis (time-based) and release threshold lower than engage threshold. e.g.
- engage when `lag_p95 > 500ms` for 10s and prefill full
- release when `lag_p95 < 250ms` for 30s OR prefill_inflight <= max-2

Julian Moore: can you share the actual timeline from the burst? would help tune.

Sofia Ivanova: pasted from my notes (times relative):
- T+0: 6 long-prefill requests arrive (enterprise + best-effort mixed)
- T+8s: prefill_inflight hits 8/8
- T+20s: lag p95 crosses 300ms
- T+35s: ttft p95 starts climbing rapidly
- T+60s: queue_depth finally crosses 120 (too late)
- T+75s: routing starts throttling best-effort, but enterprise already impacted

Robert King: that’s pretty clear. gating on queue depth is just late for this failure mode.

Peter Holtz: also, this maps to the incident thread where we saw “flat queue depth but tail latency rising” and couldn’t explain it quickly.

Vikram Singh: if you can export `prefill_saturated` + lag, routing can treat it as capacity pressure and preserve enterprise budget (shed best-effort earlier). that’s exactly what hot-route protection wants.

Mateo Alvarez: action items?

Sofia Ivanova: I can take a small PR:

2) add `runtime.backpressure_mode` enum-ish metric (careful) or just counters when we enter/exit
3) implement new admission guard: when saturated+lag, slow accept for best-effort queue first

source.content:
Sofia Ivanova: trying to lock down runtime regression classification examples for taxonomy v1. The two we keep arguing about: (1) scheduler stalls / token starvation (batcher stops making progress) and (2) KV cache thrash (evict/recompute spiral) that looks like latency + occasional OOMs. Can we agree on subtype mapping w/ “how to tell” notes?

Noah Patel: +1. We’re seeing on-call bounce between “latency regression” and “capacity” when it’s actually runtime behavior.

Dylan Brooks: goal is: something an IC can pick in 2 mins. If it’s runtime, choose runtime subtype; contributing factors go in postmortem.

Sofia Ivanova: ok. Here are the two patterns with symptoms:
- Scheduler stall: p95/p99 latency blows up but GPU util can be weirdly low/flat; request queue length increases; tokens/sec drops; logs show `scheduler_loop stalled` / `no runnable batches` / long gaps between dispatch.
- KV thrash: p95 grows with long context; GPU util high; sudden spikes in `kv_cache_evictions_total` + `prefill_recompute_tokens`; sometimes OOM / CUDA alloc retries; throughput can dip but GPU looks pegged.

Noah Patel: the taxonomy doc currently has Runtime > “Execution regression” (too broad), Runtime > “Memory pressure/OOM”, Runtime > “KV cache” (kinda). We need explicit “Scheduler/Batcher stall” vs “KV cache thrash”.

Rafael Mendes: (jumping in from SRE side) +1 explicit. Also needs to align with incident-bot auto-suggest. If we can key off a couple metrics/log strings, that’s huge.

Devon Price: incident-bot can suggest from symptoms if we give it: metric names + thresholds + a couple log snippets. We can’t do deep inference.

Sofia Ivanova: proposal:
A) Runtime Regression > Scheduler stall / dispatch starvation
B) Runtime Regression > KV cache thrash / eviction spiral
C) Runtime Regression > OOM / allocator instability (only when it’s truly OOM-first)

Noah Patel: I like that. Rule of thumb:
- If the primary symptom is “no progress” (token generation flatlines / gaps) => Scheduler stall.
- If the primary symptom is “progress but extremely inefficient due to evictions/recompute” => KV thrash.
- If it hard-crashes / restarts / allocator fails before anything else => OOM/allocator.

Rafael Mendes: what about the incidents where kv thrash leads to OOM? people will pick OOM.

Sofia Ivanova: we should bias to “KV cache thrash” when OOM is downstream. “OOM/allocator” is for immediate OOMs (bad batch size limits, fragmenting allocator, leak) where the fix is memory config/limits rather than cache policy.

Noah Patel: + add a note: if `kv_cache_evictions_total` + `recompute_tokens` are elevated before the first OOM, pick KV thrash as primary; list OOM as contributing factor.

Dylan Brooks: works for taxonomy principles: single primary, rest in contributing systems.

Devon Price: give me exact subtype strings so I can add to dropdown allowlist. Also, is this under top-level “Runtime regression” or “Latency regression”? We’d rather keep top-level stable.

Rafael Mendes: from the SRE draft, top-level is “Serving runtime regression” (under Performance/Correctness bucket). Keep it there.

Sofia Ivanova: yep: top-level “Serving runtime regression”. Subtypes as above.

Noah Patel: “Scheduler stall / dispatch starvation” is clear enough. “KV cache thrash / eviction spiral” is also pretty descriptive.

Rafael Mendes: any other quick discriminator for scheduler stall? folks will confuse with fleet capacity because queue grows.

Sofia Ivanova: add: if queue grows AND GPU util is low AND batch size isn’t capped by policy => likely scheduler stall. If GPU util is high AND we’re hitting max batch/prefill compute => could be capacity or kv thrash.

Noah Patel: also check `runtime.scheduler.tick_ms` p99. When it goes from ~5-20ms to 500ms+ we’re basically wedged.

Devon Price: ok, for incident-bot suggestion:
- Scheduler stall: `runtime.scheduler.tick_ms` high OR log contains `scheduler_loop stalled` OR tokens/sec near 0 while requests in flight.
- KV thrash: `runtime.kv.evictions_total` spike OR `runtime.prefill.recompute_tokens_total` spike OR `kv_cache_hit_rate` drops hard.

Dylan Brooks: keep in mind label cardinality constraints. We’ll store just incident_type/subtype, not these metrics.

Sofia Ivanova: also, examples to put in doc:
Example 1 (Scheduler stall): “p99 latency 20s; tokens/sec drops to ~0; GPU util 15%; queue depth rising; fix was rollback runtime scheduler change.” => classify as Serving runtime regression > Scheduler stall.
Example 2 (KV thrash): “long-context workloads; p95 +4x; GPU 95%; evictions + recompute spike; fix was adjust kv cache partitioning + disable aggressive batch compaction.” => Serving runtime regression > KV cache thrash.

Noah Patel: add a third:
Example 3 (Allocator instability): “pods repeatedly restart w/ CUDA OOM on startup; no evictions spike beforehand; fix was reduce default max_batch_tokens for that GPU class.” => Serving runtime regression > OOM / allocator instability.

Rafael Mendes: great. Can we explicitly say “don’t classify as ‘Capacity shortage’ unless there’s a fleet signal” (GPU saturation across cluster / provisioning lag / no headroom)?

Sofia Ivanova: yes, we’ll put in the “exclusion” section:
- Don’t pick Capacity shortage if a single model pool has low GPU util + high queue.
- If routing is sending traffic away due to health, that’s Routing/Fallback.

Devon Price: once you confirm final strings, I’ll update incident-bot allowlist + the auto-suggest heuristic doc.

Sofia Ivanova: final strings:
1) Serving runtime regression / Scheduler stall (dispatch starvation)
2) Serving runtime regression / KV cache thrash (eviction spiral)
3) Serving runtime regression / OOM / allocator instability

Noah Patel: +1

Rafael Mendes: +1, I’ll mirror to the SRE taxonomy doc + add these examples to the decision tree appendix.

incident-bot: :memo: Reminder: taxonomy v1 definitions draft is in Confluence (Reliability Initiatives → Incident taxonomy v1 definitions). Please comment there for permanent record.

Sofia Ivanova: cool. I’ll paste these into the “common runtime incident types” page too and link metrics panels. thanks all.

source.messages:
Sofia Ivanova: aligning on the runtime metrics export for hot-route protection. routing folks are asking for queue depth + scheduler lag signals but we *cannot* blow up cardinality again 🙃

Julian Moore: yep. current WIP has per-(tenant, route, model, pool) tags which is… not gonna fly.

Vikram Singh: +1. needs to be low overhead + bounded tags. data plane can’t do high-card label sets on every enqueue/dequeue.

Mateo Alvarez: can we agree on the exact “contract” metrics that routing consumes? if we lock that, runtime can aggregate accordingly.

Logan Wright: for observability standards: anything used for automated admission should be stable + low-card. think region + route_group + tier + pool at most.

Allison Grant: also please no raw `customer_id` / `api_key_hash` tags in metrics. if we need tenant-level debugging, do it via logs/traces sampled, not metrics.

Sofia Ivanova: ok, proposal:
- export 2 primary gauges per runtime shard:
  1) `runtime_queue_depth` (requests)
  2) `runtime_scheduler_lag_ms` (ms since eligible -> scheduled)
- plus optional histogram `runtime_wait_time_ms` (coarse buckets)
- tags limited to: `region`, `cluster`, `pool`, `route_group`, `priority_class`

Lena Fischer: define `route_group` as the gateway/routing group name, not raw path. e.g. `chat_completions`, `embeddings`, `rerank`. keeps it bounded.

Robert King: routing side can map route -> route_group already. priority_class we’ll set to enterprise/best_effort/internal.

Julian Moore: what about model? scheduler lag differs a lot by model size.

Nadia Rahman: if we tag by model_id we’re dead. could do `model_family` or `compute_class` (small/med/large) but that’s still risk.

Logan Wright: recommendation: don’t tag by model at the runtime metric layer for the “admission” signals. instead, export per-pool metrics. pools are already model-constrained most of the time.

Vikram Singh: yep, pool is the right abstraction. pool cardinality is bounded + controlled by us.

Julian Moore: k. pool implies model mix anyway. but for mixed pools, lag signal might be noisy.

Sofia Ivanova: if we have truly mixed pools, we can also add a `workload_class` tag derived from max_seq_len bucket (short/long) *but only if it’s strictly a fixed enum*.

Allison Grant: careful: anything derived from user input tends to drift. keep it fixed buckets (e.g. `short`, `medium`, `long`) and document mapping.

Mateo Alvarez: do we need queue depth in “tokens” too? routing wants to avoid admitting long prompts when backlog is mostly long.

Lena Fischer: runtime can approximate “pending_tokens” but it’s more compute + has lots of edge cases.

Sofia Ivanova: let’s start with request queue depth + scheduler lag. add pending_tokens later if we prove it adds value.

Caleb Johnson: ingestion constraints: histograms are ok but keep bucket count small (<= 10). also prefer gauges/counters over high-frequency hist updates if possible.

Julian Moore: implementation detail: we currently have per-queue structures: per priority class, per route_group. if we expose per (priority_class, route_group, pool) we’re still bounded.

Logan Wright: what’s the upper bound? route_group ~ 6-10, priority_class 3, pool maybe ~ 20 per region. that’s 600 time series per region per metric. ok.

Nadia Rahman: plus cluster + shard id? please don’t add shard id as label.

Sofia Ivanova: agreed: no shard id tag. runtime will aggregate across shards before export if possible.

Julian Moore: we can do node-local aggregation and emit per node. but then you still get node label via infra (`instance`).

Allison Grant: infra labels happen, but we can avoid *explicit* shard labels. dashboards can aggregate `sum without(instance)`.

Vikram Singh: also: routing doesn’t want per-node, it wants region/pool-level signals. can we emit to a local aggregator (statsd/otel collector) that rolls up before routing reads it?

Robert King: routing service will consume from fleet signals API, not scrape prometheus directly. as long as fleet layer can aggregate to pool/region, we’re good.

Caleb Johnson: fleet signals pipeline can aggregate if the metric names + tags are consistent. but you need deterministic tag set.

Sofia Ivanova: ok let’s lock the tags:
- REQUIRED labels: `region`, `cluster`, `pool`, `route_group`, `priority_class`
- FORBIDDEN labels: `tenant`, `api_key`, `model_id`, `request_id`, `path`, `user_agent`
- OPTIONAL (only if we see need): `workload_class` (short|medium|long)

Julian Moore: naming: do we prefer `scheduler_lag_ms` vs `scheduler_delay_ms`?

Allison Grant: we have a standard for “lag” as time since event. use `*_lag_ms`.

Logan Wright: +1. also add a counter for shed/backpressure events from runtime?

Lena Fischer: shedding happens in gateway/routing mainly. runtime does backpressure. we can export `runtime_backpressure_active` (0/1) per (pool, route_group, priority_class).

Mateo Alvarez: sounds useful for incident debugging.

Nadia Rahman: backpressure flag is fine. no per-reason explosion though.

Sofia Ivanova: reason codes will live at gateway (429). runtime just exports the activation bit + maybe a single enum in logs.

Julian Moore: posting a concrete snippet for review (OTel metrics):
```
name: runtime_queue_depth
unit: {requests}
type: gauge
labels: region, cluster, pool, route_group, priority_class

name: runtime_scheduler_lag_ms
unit: ms
type: gauge
labels: region, cluster, pool, route_group, priority_class

name: runtime_backpressure_active
unit: {bool}
type: gauge
labels: region, cluster, pool, route_group, priority_class
```

Vikram Singh: 👍 this is sane.

Robert King: routing will treat `scheduler_lag_ms` as primary admission input. queue depth is secondary.

Allison Grant: please add doc note: gauges should be computed as EWMA over 5s to avoid jitter causing routing flaps.

Sofia Ivanova: yes. we’ll smooth in runtime before export (5s window), and routing also has a 15s hold-down.

Logan Wright: and let’s add a quick cardinality test in CI (assert label keys). we got burned last time.

Julian Moore: can do. will add unit test to fail build if someone adds `tenant` label to these instruments.

Mateo Alvarez: ok, next steps:
- Julian: update PR to remove model_id/tenant labels + add smoothing
- Sofia/Lena: define `route_group` mapping in runtime (accept from routing header?)
- Logan/Allison: update observability standard doc with these metric names + label set

Sofia Ivanova: runtime can take `x-redwood-route-group` header from routing. fallback to `unknown` if missing.

Nadia Rahman: make sure `unknown` doesn’t get spammy (if header missing across traffic). alert if header missing rate > threshold.

Sofia Ivanova: good call. adding counter `runtime_route_group_missing_total` (no labels except region/cluster/pool) so we notice.

Allison Grant: cool. thanks all. keeping this thread as the canonical agreement for label set + names.

Julian Moore: will push updates today and ping in #eng-platform when ready.
