Sofia Ivanova: aligning on the runtime metrics export for hot-route protection. routing folks are asking for queue depth + scheduler lag signals but we *cannot* blow up cardinality again 🙃

Julian Moore: yep. current WIP has per-(tenant, route, model, pool) tags which is… not gonna fly.

Vikram Singh: +1. needs to be low overhead + bounded tags. data plane can’t do high-card label sets on every enqueue/dequeue.

Mateo Alvarez: can we agree on the exact “contract” metrics that routing consumes? if we lock that, runtime can aggregate accordingly.

Logan Wright: for observability standards: anything used for automated admission should be stable + low-card. think region + route_group + tier + pool at most.

Allison Grant: also please no raw `customer_id` / `api_key_hash` tags in metrics. if we need tenant-level debugging, do it via logs/traces sampled, not metrics.

Sofia Ivanova: ok, proposal:
- export 2 primary gauges per runtime shard:
  1) `runtime_queue_depth` (requests)
  2) `runtime_scheduler_lag_ms` (ms since eligible -> scheduled)

- tags limited to: `region`, `cluster`, `pool`, `route_group`, `priority_class`

Lena Fischer: define `route_group` as the gateway/routing group name, not raw path. e.g. `chat_completions`, `embeddings`, `rerank`. keeps it bounded.

Robert King: routing side can map route -> route_group already. priority_class we’ll set to enterprise/best_effort/internal.

Julian Moore: what about model? scheduler lag differs a lot by model size.

source.text:
Christine Wong: heads up from yesterday’s Sev2-ish blip: we had a failure mode that only hit streaming responses (SSE) — non-streaming / full responses were basically fine. want to make sure the status component split (Hosted API vs Hosted API - Streaming) can actually detect this cleanly.

Logan Wright: yep, saw it too. symptom: stream starts ok, then mid-stream we get a burst of disconnects/reset, tokens stop, clients retry. for non-streaming, request finishes before it hits the bad path.

Caleb Johnson: +1. looks like the runtime write loop was getting backpressured; we ended up timing out writes to the gateway and tearing down the stream. nothing wrong with model compute per se.

Mina Haddad: was this localized to a region or cross-region?

Christine Wong: mostly us-east, but a bit in eu-west. the confusing part was overall 5xx didn’t move much, so status page stayed “API Operational” while customers saw “streaming hangs / disconnects”.

Allison Grant: that matches what Support pinged us on. for component-level detection we need a signal that’s streaming-specific and tagged by region + capability. do we have a reliable metric today?

Logan Wright: we have a couple, but they’re not clean:
- `gateway.http_5xx{route=/v1/chat/completions}` isn’t enough (stream disconnects often aren’t 5xx)
- `gateway.request_duration` percentiles get weird b/c streams are long-lived
what we *need* is a counter for stream terminations by reason.

Aisha Bello: for SLO burn + dashboards, the candidate signals:
1) stream start failures (can be 429/5xx) — easy
2) mid-stream termination rate — currently missing / too coarse
3) “time to first token” — helpful, but doesn’t catch mid-stream drop

Ravi Menon: re mapping rules: if we get `capability=streaming` + `region=us-east` tags, we can map to public component `Hosted API - Streaming (us-east)` without toggling the whole Hosted API. but we need the signals to drive that.

Caleb Johnson: metric proposal from runtime side:
- `runtime.stream_disconnect_total{region, model, reason}`
reasons like `client_disconnect`, `gateway_reset`, `write_timeout`, `internal_error`.
we can roll up all reasons except `client_disconnect` for status purposes.

Devon Price: from incident-bot automation POV, we can pull those counters for “impact evidence” and suggest the correct component. but it needs to be stable (anti-flap) and not page on transient blips.

Allison Grant: agree. for status signals standard, we can do something like:
- mark degraded if `mid_stream_termination_rate > X%` over 10m AND sustained for 15m
- clear when it’s < (X - hysteresis) for 30m
(and region-scoped)

Mina Haddad: can we also capture “no chunks emitted for N seconds” kind of stall? customers describe “hangs” not always disconnect.

Caleb Johnson: stalls are harder but we can approximate:
- `runtime.stream_chunk_write_latency_p95` (or gateway flush latency) spikes when backpressure happens
- or a watchdog counter: `runtime.stream_write_timeout_total`
hangs usually end as timeouts anyway.

Christine Wong: what’s the minimum set we should push for v1 of component detection?

Logan Wright: minimum IMO:
1) `stream_write_timeout_total` (region-tagged)
2) `stream_terminated_total` with reason breakdown (exclude client_disconnect)
3) `time_to_first_token_p95` for streaming path specifically (separate from non-streaming)

Aisha Bello: + add dashboard panels per region for those three, and link them from the status-page runbook so on-call can verify quickly.

Irene Choi: is there already a PR in flight for the region-aware streaming metrics?

Logan Wright: yeah, I’m piggybacking on the gateway changes we discussed — adding region-aware streaming disconnect/error counters + tags. should align with the status taxonomy fields (`component_id`, `region`, `capability`).

Ravi Menon: perfect. once those land, I’ll update the component map tests to ensure streaming-only degradations don’t flip “Hosted API” overall.

Christine Wong: awesome. action items:
- Logan/Caleb: lock the metric names + tags (capability=streaming) and share in #eng-sre
- Allison/Aisha: draft thresholds for degraded/partial outage using those metrics
- me: update the taxonomy doc w/ “streaming-only” failure mode example + what to look at
thanks all :thumbsup:

- clear when it’s < (X - hysteresis) for 30m
(and region-scoped)

Mina Haddad: can we also capture “no chunks emitted for N seconds” kind of stall? customers describe “hangs” not always disconnect.

Caleb Johnson: stalls are harder but we can approximate:
- `runtime.stream_chunk_write_latency_p95` (or gateway flush latency) spikes when backpressure happens
- or a watchdog counter: `runtime.stream_write_timeout_total`
hangs usually end as timeouts anyway.

Christine Wong: what’s the minimum set we should push for v1 of component detection?

Logan Wright: minimum IMO:
1) `stream_write_timeout_total` (region-tagged)
2) `stream_terminated_total` with reason breakdown (exclude client_disconnect)
3) `time_to_first_token_p95` for streaming path specifically (separate from non-streaming)

Aisha Bello: + add dashboard panels per region for those three, and link them from the status-page runbook so on-call can verify quickly.

Irene Choi: is there already a PR in flight for the region-aware streaming metrics?

Logan Wright: yeah, I’m piggybacking on the gateway changes we discussed — adding region-aware streaming disconnect/error counters + tags. should align with the status taxonomy fields (`component_id`, `region`, `capability`).

Ravi Menon: perfect. once those land, I’ll update the component map tests to ensure streaming-only degradations don’t flip “Hosted API” overall.

Christine Wong: awesome. action items:
- Logan/Caleb: lock the metric names + tags (capability=streaming) and share in #eng-sre
- Allison/Aisha: draft thresholds for degraded/partial outage using those metrics
- me: update the taxonomy doc w/ “streaming-only” failure mode example + what to look at
thanks all :thumbsup:

source.text:
Ethan Park: FYI: v3 /events is going to switch from newline-delimited JSON to an "envelope" binary framing (size-prefixed + optional compression). Old clients expect NDJSON. We need a compatibility shim plan for streaming + batch reads.

Lina Morales: Oof. Two main paths: gateway on-the-fly translator vs dedicated streaming sidecar that handles framing and backpressure. Gateway path is simpler deploy-wise but risks adding tail-latency.

Ravi Singh: For batch reads we can also offer a cache-transform job that stores pre-unpacked NDJSON for hot tenants. That helps read-heavy workloads.

Ethan Park: Important constraints: preserve chunked streaming semantics for long-poll clients, maintain memory bounds (no unbounded buffering), and keep CPU cost predictable.

Priya Kapoor: Suggestion: opt-in header X-Redwood-Compat: ndjson to enable shim. Roll canary with 3 tenants first. We'll emit compat_shim=true, shim_mode=(gateway|sidecar|backfill), unpack_time_ms, buffered_bytes.

Marcus Chen: Streaming note: envelope framing may send partial event bodies across frames. Reassembly needs to be incremental with a small ring buffer. If we do gateway, implement as a streaming coroutine, not read-all.

CI Bot: Canary build deployed to runtime-gw-canary, sidecar image pushed: registry/redwood/events-sidecar:canary-2026-03-21.

Lina Morales: Pseudo-code for gateway coroutine (non-blocking):
```
async def translate_stream(upstream):
  buf = bytearray()
  async for chunk in upstream:
    buf.extend(chunk)
    while buf has complete envelope:
      frame = pop_frame(buf)
      event = decode_frame(frame)
      if request.headers.get("X-Redwood-Compat") == "ndjson":
        await client.send(jsonl(event))
      else:
        await client.send(frame)
```
Keep reassembly bounded by a per-connection max (e.g. 2MB) and drop/metric if exceeds.

Ravi Singh: If gateway CPU looks bad, fallback is route-to-sidecar pool (stateful) or route to v2 pool and force client upgrade. We need an emergency flag to flip routing.

Ethan Park: Action items:
- Lina: implement gateway coroutine + feature flag (owner)
- Priya: add telemetry + dashboards (owner)
- Marcus: prototype sidecar streaming reframe with memory limits (owner)
- Ravi: backfill job prototype for hot-tenant cache + eviction policy (owner)
- CI Bot: coordinate canary deploy and smoke tests
Timeline: canary today, collect 24h metrics, decide next step.

Priya Kapoor: I'll create alerts: shim_error_rate > 1%, unpack_time_ms p95 > 150ms, per-connection buffer > 1MB -> pager.

Marcus Chen: One more: we should make the shim pluggable via an interface so we can split load: lightweight translation in-gateway for small streams, hand off big/long-lived streams to sidecar.

Ethan Park: Agreed. Keep this thread updated with canary numbers. If anyone sees a spike, @here and we'll roll back.

Lina Morales: quick note: if we add TLS termination differences in sidecar, make sure client cert info is preserved.

Ravi Singh: I'll add a short e2e test that simulates partial frames and verifies incremental reassembly.

Ethan Park: Thanks all. Please add PR links here as you open them.

source.messages:
Omar El-Sayed: Seeing pretty ugly variance in p95 on the region sweep reruns. Same commit/config, 3 runs back-to-back.

Omar El-Sayed: Snapshot (A100 80GB, us-east):
- p50: 410-430ms (stable)
- p95: 980ms, 1.9s, 1.1s (not stable)
Throughput is within ~5%.

Rina Kobayashi: is that end-to-end including queue, or just model time?

Omar El-Sayed: e2e (client send -> last token). harness is closed-loop concurrency=32, but we *do* include server-side queue.

Noah Patel: p50 stable + p95 jumping smells like queue tail, not kernel perf.

Jonas Weber: +1. Also: are we mixing prompt shapes? the v3 prod mix has some long prefill + short decode, right?

Omar El-Sayed: yep. prompt_set=v3-production-mix. prefill distribution is wide.

Claire Dubois: can we check whether the runtime is switching kernels between runs? autotune can choose different fused-attn paths depending on observed shapes.

Noah Patel: It shouldn’t if shapes are the same, but if the prompt mix shuffles order, the initial calibration can land on different kernel variants.

Mateo Alvarez: What’s the GPU utilization like on the bad p95 run? If we’re near saturation, small arrival jitter can spike queue.

Omar El-Sayed: pulled server stats from logs:

```text
run=us-east-2025-06-05T18:10Z
gpu_util_avg=71%
queue_depth_p95=14
batch_size_avg=6.2

run=us-east-2025-06-05T18:24Z
gpu_util_avg=73%
queue_depth_p95=41
batch_size_avg=6.4

run=us-east-2025-06-05T18:38Z
gpu_util_avg=72%
queue_depth_p95=18
batch_size_avg=6.3
```

Noah Patel: queue_depth_p95=41 explains the 1.9s p95 if max_wait is permissive.

Rina Kobayashi: what are we using for scheduler settings in these sweeps? (max_wait_ms, max_batch_tokens, etc)

Omar El-Sayed: from the manifest:

```yaml
scheduler:
  policy: continuous-batching
  max_wait_ms: 8
  max_batch_tokens: 8192
  max_batch_size: 16
```

Jonas Weber: max_wait 8ms is fine; but if we’re getting bursts, queue can still build even with low wait.

Claire Dubois: could be a “convoy” effect: long-prefill requests arrive clustered early in the run, they occupy compute longer, queue builds, tail gets hammered.

Noah Patel: Exactly. p95 is sensitive to ordering with heterogeneous service times.

Mateo Alvarez: Are we randomizing the prompt order each run? If yes, that’s a big source of variance.

Rina Kobayashi: we do shuffle with seed derived from timestamp right now (oops). for fairness, maybe we need deterministic seed.

Omar El-Sayed: ah that’s probably it. I’ll confirm. :facepalm:

Vanessa Ortiz: (from infra side) also flagging: on the 18:24Z run, fleet had a noisy neighbor alert in that cluster. Not a hard preemption, but some PCIe retries logged. Not sure if related.

Noah Patel: Even without noisy neighbor, prompt shuffle alone can do this. But good to know.

Claire Dubois: Can we separate “service time” (runtime compute) vs “queue time” in the exported metrics? For the report we probably want both, or at least to be explicit that latency includes queue under a fixed concurrency.

Jonas Weber: +1. We’ve been asked to publish latency/$, but if p95 is dominated by queue ordering, that’s still “real,” just needs explanation.

Omar El-Sayed: I can add `queue_time_ms` and `model_time_ms` to the per-request log + export. We already have spans internally.

Mateo Alvarez: Also check kernel selection. If autotune is happening at startup, we should log kernel IDs chosen for key ops (attn, GEMM).

Noah Patel: There’s an env var to pin kernel profile:
`RW_KERNEL_PROFILE=stable_v2` (disables some heuristics).
We could run a quick A/B: stable_v2 vs default.

Rina Kobayashi: If we do that, we need to note it in methodology. But for debugging right now, it’ll isolate whether kernel drift is contributing.

Omar El-Sayed: I’ll rerun locally with fixed prompt seed + pinned kernel profile to see if p95 tightens.

Jonas Weber: What about KV cache behavior? Are we seeing evictions / cache fragmentation that could affect tail?

Noah Patel: In this harness each request is independent, so KV cache reuse shouldn’t matter unless we’re enabling prefix cache (are we?)

Rina Kobayashi: prefix cache is ON in these runs (policy: "auto"). It might help mean cost but can introduce variability if hit rate swings.

Claire Dubois: For the public benchmark, do we want prefix cache on? It’s a production feature, but it can be workload-dependent.

Jonas Weber: for now: keep it consistent and disclose. but for debugging variance, maybe turn it off to understand baseline.

Omar El-Sayed: ok plan:
1) rerun with `--prefix-cache=off` and fixed prompt order seed.
2) rerun with cache on + fixed seed.
3) compare p95 and queue_depth distributions.

Mateo Alvarez: Also confirm harness mode: you said closed-loop concurrency=32, which *should* reduce arrival jitter vs open-loop. But if each worker is sync and we have variable response sizes, the effective arrival pattern can still cluster.

Noah Patel: We might consider adding an open-loop mode for stability checks (Poisson arrivals) and report both. But that’s bigger scope.

Rina Kobayashi: for report, we committed to closed-loop as “fixed concurrency,” right? (I think Claire wrote that draft.)

Claire Dubois: yes, closed-loop fixed concurrency. But we should add the queue-time breakdown so people don’t interpret it as pure model compute.

perf-bot: Perf alert: p95 regression > 25% detected for suite=latency_per_dollar region=us-east model=ri-llama-70b run_id=us-east-2025-06-05T18:24Z. Threshold=20%. Auto-rerun enabled.

Omar El-Sayed: lol perf-bot piling on.

Vanessa Ortiz: If the auto-rerun lands on a different host, that’ll also help rule out node noise.

Noah Patel: One more thought: are clocks stable? Some nodes run with different power caps after maintenance.

Vanessa Ortiz: power caps should be uniform, but I’ll double-check that pool.

Omar El-Sayed: Here’s a trace sample from the bad run (one request in the p95 tail):

```text
req_id=... 
queue_ms=812
prefill_ms=512
decode_ms=146
stream_overhead_ms=18
```

Omar El-Sayed: Tail is overwhelmingly queue.

Jonas Weber: That’s good news for runtime perf (ish). But it means we need to control ordering / publish stability gates.

Claire Dubois: Action items:
- Omar: add queue/model time fields to export + run the fixed-seed experiments
- Rina: update sweep runner to use deterministic prompt shuffle seed (configurable)
- Noah: add kernel selection logging + check stability of autotune
- Vanessa: sanity-check noisy-neighbor / power cap notes on that pool

Mateo Alvarez: +1. Let’s aim to have a clean explanation by tomorrow so PMM doesn’t get surprised by “why did p95 change?” questions.

Omar El-Sayed: ack. I’ll post rerun graphs once I have them.

Noah Patel: Even without noisy neighbor, prompt shuffle alone can do this. But good to know.

Claire Dubois: Can we separate “service time” (runtime compute) vs “queue time” in the exported metrics? For the report we probably want both, or at least to be explicit that latency includes queue under a fixed concurrency.

Jonas Weber: +1. We’ve been asked to publish latency/$, but if p95 is dominated by queue ordering, that’s still “real,” just needs explanation.

Omar El-Sayed: I can add `queue_time_ms` and `model_time_ms` to the per-request log + export. We already have spans internally.

Mateo Alvarez: Also check kernel selection. If autotune is happening at startup, we should log kernel IDs chosen for key ops (attn, GEMM).

Noah Patel: There’s an env var to pin kernel profile:
`RW_KERNEL_PROFILE=stable_v2` (disables some heuristics).
We could run a quick A/B: stable_v2 vs default.

Rina Kobayashi: If we do that, we need to note it in methodology. But for debugging right now, it’ll isolate whether kernel drift is contributing.

Omar El-Sayed: I’ll rerun locally with fixed prompt seed + pinned kernel profile to see if p95 tightens.

Jonas Weber: What about KV cache behavior? Are we seeing evictions / cache fragmentation that could affect tail?

Noah Patel: In this harness each request is independent, so KV cache reuse shouldn’t matter unless we’re enabling prefix cache (are we?)

Rina Kobayashi: prefix cache is ON in these runs (policy: "auto"). It might help mean cost but can introduce variability if hit rate swings.

Claire Dubois: For the public benchmark, do we want prefix cache on? It’s a production feature, but it can be workload-dependent.

Jonas Weber: for now: keep it consistent and disclose. but for debugging variance, maybe turn it off to understand baseline.

Omar El-Sayed: ok plan:
1) rerun with `--prefix-cache=off` and fixed prompt order seed.
2) rerun with cache on + fixed seed.
3) compare p95 and queue_depth distributions.

Mateo Alvarez: Also confirm harness mode: you said closed-loop concurrency=32, which *should* reduce arrival jitter vs open-loop. But if each worker is sync and we have variable response sizes, the effective arrival pattern can still cluster.

Noah Patel: We might consider adding an open-loop mode for stability checks (Poisson arrivals) and report both. But that’s bigger scope.

Rina Kobayashi: for report, we committed to closed-loop as “fixed concurrency,” right? (I think Claire wrote that draft.)

Claire Dubois: yes, closed-loop fixed concurrency. But we should add the queue-time breakdown so people don’t interpret it as pure model compute.

perf-bot: Perf alert: p95 regression > 25% detected for suite=latency_per_dollar region=us-east model=ri-llama-70b run_id=us-east-2025-06-05T18:24Z. Threshold=20%. Auto-rerun enabled.

Omar El-Sayed: lol perf-bot piling on.

Vanessa Ortiz: If the auto-rerun lands on a different host, that’ll also help rule out node noise.

Noah Patel: One more thought: are clocks stable? Some nodes run with different power caps after maintenance.

Vanessa Ortiz: power caps should be uniform, but I’ll double-check that pool.

Omar El-Sayed: Here’s a trace sample from the bad run (one request in the p95 tail):

```text
req_id=... 
queue_ms=812
prefill_ms=512
decode_ms=146
stream_overhead_ms=18
```

Omar El-Sayed: Tail is overwhelmingly queue.

Jonas Weber: That’s good news for runtime perf (ish). But it means we need to control ordering / publish stability gates.

Claire Dubois: Action items:
- Omar: add queue/model time fields to export + run the fixed-seed experiments
- Rina: update sweep runner to use deterministic prompt shuffle seed (configurable)
- Noah: add kernel selection logging + check stability of autotune
- Vanessa: sanity-check noisy-neighbor / power cap notes on that pool

Mateo Alvarez: +1. Let’s aim to have a clean explanation by tomorrow so PMM doesn’t get surprised by “why did p95 change?” questions.

Omar El-Sayed: ack. I’ll post rerun graphs once I have them.

source.body:
Diego Santos: quick q for folks thinking about autoscaling signals — with continuous batching on runtime, is external `queue_depth` actually stable enough as a scaler input for Dedicated? seeing cases where queue blows up then collapses when batcher flips modes.

Vanessa Ortiz: depends on what you mean by queue_depth. if it’s “pending requests in front of router” it’s *sometimes* ok, but if it’s “requests waiting inside runtime scheduler” it gets weird with long-prefill / decode split.

Keiko Yamamoto: +1. queue depth can spike from a couple long-context prefill requests and looks like “need 10x replicas” even though decode side is underutilized.

Samantha Roy: we’re currently exporting `pending_requests` from fleet side (load balancer queue) + `inflight` from runtime. but the current autoscaler loop mostly keys on pending + p95 queue wait.

Diego Santos: yeah, the model matters too. for 70B w/ long prompts, prefill is the bottleneck and batching behaves differently. queue depth is basically “how many big prefills just landed” not steady demand.

Logan Wright: this is the same issue we saw in replay sims: batching increases throughput but also increases service time variance. queue depth becomes a lagging indicator + very noisy.

Mina Haddad: routing side also sees this as “capacity available” flip-flopping. when batcher switches to bigger batches, latency rises, then autoscaler adds pods, then batcher drops batch size because more replicas, then we overshoot… oscillation city.

Ravi Menon: can we normalize the signal to tokens instead of requests? queue_depth(requests) is a bad proxy when prompt lengths are heavy-tailed.

Keiko Yamamoto: runtime can emit `queued_tokens_prefill` and `queued_tokens_decode` (or at least `estimated_prefill_ms_backlog` / `estimated_decode_ms_backlog`). that’s more aligned with actual work.

Vanessa Ortiz: we had a draft metric called “time-to-drain” from scheduler:
`drain_s = backlog_tokens / max(tokens_per_s, eps)`
then scale based on drain_s > threshold. more stable than raw queue.

Allison Grant: from observability POV, tokens-based metrics are doable but we need to be careful w/ labels (deployment_id, model, region ok; avoid request_id / prompt_len hist labels). if we add new metrics, pls follow the naming doc.

Diego Santos: what’s the best single metric then? p95 queue wait? but that also gets weird when batch size grows (queue wait increases by design).

Rafael Mendes: from SRE side, queue wait is useful for paging but not necessarily for scaling control. if you scale based on a paged symptom you’ll chase your tail. we’d rather use “saturation” signals that lead the symptom.

Logan Wright: we should distinguish:
- **symptom**: queue depth / queue wait / tail latency
- **cause proxy**: GPU util, active sequences, KV cache pressure, scheduler backlog (tokens)
queue depth is ok as a guardrail (“we’re falling behind”) but shouldn’t be primary target signal.

Samantha Roy: FYI runtime GPU util is not super reliable for some kernels (util stays high even when effective tokens/s drops due to memory stalls). but active_sequences + decode_step_time might be better.

Keiko Yamamoto: yep. also, for some models w/ speculative decoding or aggressive caching, the relationship between queue depth and capacity is nonlinear.

Mina Haddad: for Dedicated bounds + predictive mode, we’re planning arbitration anyway: prediction suggests desired, reactive can override when “overload” signals fire. question is what overload signal should be.

Vanessa Ortiz: current proposal for overload is OR of:
- `queue_wait_p95_ms > X` for Y minutes
- `time_to_drain_s > A`
- `requests_older_than_Zs > B`
Queue depth alone is too spiky.

Diego Santos: can we get `requests_older_than` from runtime? (oldest enqueue time) that might be robust to batching quirks.

Keiko Yamamoto: we can. scheduler has enqueue_ts per request already. exporting “oldest_age_s” and “age_p95_s” is cheap.

Allison Grant: if we add `oldest_age_s`, that’s a great dashboard panel too (much more interpretable for CS than “GPU util”).

Rafael Mendes: also helps with runbooks: “queue age increasing while replicas pinned at max bound” is a clear story.

Ravi Menon: so conclusion: queue depth isn’t stable across models, but “queue age” + “time-to-drain” are. we can keep queue depth for debug / context.

Vanessa Ortiz: agree. action item: @Keiko Yamamoto + @Samantha Roy can you draft what runtime metrics are available by next week? we’ll update autoscaler to prefer token/age-based overload signal (still respects min/max bounds).

Keiko Yamamoto: yep. I’ll put together a short list + recommended semantics (prefill vs decode split, how we compute tokens_per_s).

Diego Santos: thanks. also, pls call out anything model-specific (long-context vs normal chat) so we don’t accidentally tune for llama-3.1-8b and break 70b.

Logan Wright: I can drop a snippet from the sim results showing queue_depth oscillation vs time-to-drain stability. will post in infra thread too.

Mina Haddad: :thumbsup: once we have the new overload signal, routing can use “bounded + overload” state to trigger graceful degradation (fallback model/region) without misreading batch-induced queue spikes.

Rafael Mendes: +1. and for paging: we can still alert on sustained queue age + max-bound hit rate, not raw depth.

Allison Grant: ok, when you finalize metric names, tag me and Sergio so we can keep Grafana panels consistent.

Vanessa Ortiz: cool. I’ll open a small follow-up ticket for “queue age/time-to-drain as primary reactive signal” and link this thread.

source.body:
Omar El-Sayed: quick q for folks thinking about overload: do we want the primary saturation signal to be queue depth (prefill/decode) or token/sec saturation? trying to nail what we export from runtime for the breaker policy engine

Sofia Ivanova: both, but careful w/ semantics. queue depth is “pressure” and token/sec is “throughput ceiling”. they correlate but not always.

Lena Fischer: +1. queue depth catches backlog early. token/sec saturation catches “we’re running hot even if queue looks fine” (eg long decode).

Julian Moore: if we pick only one, queue depth is easier + less noisy. but it can false-trigger if batcher is doing work (queue spikes during batch formation).

Christine Wong: from gateway/policy side: we need something stable + explainable. if it’s noisy we’ll open/close breakers and create thrash.

Omar El-Sayed: right. token/sec saturation idea was something like `observed_decode_toks_per_sec / expected_max_decode_toks_per_sec` per pool, clamp 0-1

Sofia Ivanova: expected_max is the hard part. depends on model, kv cache state, seq lens, quant profile, etc.

Lena Fischer: and “expected max” changes with scheduler knobs (continuous batching params) and GPU type. we’ll be constantly chasing it.

Julian Moore: also token/sec is a rate. you’ll need a window. if the window is too short it’s jitter city. too long and breaker reacts late.

Omar El-Sayed: what about using “scheduler backlog time” instead? like estimate time-to-admit based on queue length * avg service time

Sofia Ivanova: that’s basically queue depth with a little modeling. I like it but we still need the avg service time estimator.

Elena Popov: from routing: any signal we use needs bounded cardinality. we can’t have per-model-per-tenant-per-gpu metrics.

Logan Wright: ^ please. if you export per-worker queue depth with labels `gpu_uuid` or `pod` you will melt prometheus + grafana.

Omar El-Sayed: current draft exports:
- `runtime_prefill_queue_depth{pool,model}` gauge
- `runtime_decode_queue_depth{pool,model}` gauge
- `runtime_tokens_per_sec{pool,model,phase}` rate
- `runtime_kv_cache_pressure{pool,model}` gauge
is `model` already too much?

Logan Wright: depends on how many models per pool and how many pools per region. if we also label by `tenant` (pls no) it explodes.

Christine Wong: we only need tier-aware decisions at gateway. per-tenant can be handled by the admission layer w/out per-tenant runtime metrics.

Rafael Mendes: jumping in (sre hat): for alerting we prefer pool-level aggregates. model dimension is ok if it’s small + stable. but don’t do request_path/tenant_id.

Sofia Ivanova: runtime side, model label is doable if we aggregate at “model family + quant profile” not every exact version. ex: `llama-3.1-70b-instruct` and not `llama-3.1-70b-instruct@2025-03-28-rc3`.

Julian Moore: we can also put exact model version in logs/traces, not metrics.

Omar El-Sayed: sampling rate: scheduler can compute queue depth every 100ms internally. do we export at 1s? 5s?

Logan Wright: scrape interval for most runtime pods is 15s today. we *can* do 5s in the hot regions but it increases cost. also high-freq gauges still churn.

Lena Fischer: for breaker decisions we want faster than 15s though, otherwise you’re already in tail latency hell.

Christine Wong: breaker is evaluated in gateway/routing at request time. can we feed it via a faster channel than prometheus scrape? (e.g. cached value in control plane)

Sofia Ivanova: we can publish a cheap “health snapshot” to the router every 1s over our existing runtime heartbeat. similar to autoscaler signals.

Elena Popov: +1. router already consumes regional capacity signals. adding `pool_pressure` there is clean. prometheus can still scrape for dashboards.

Rafael Mendes: that aligns with ADR-015 layering. request path should not depend on prom scrape freshness.

Julian Moore: if we do heartbeat snapshots, we should still compute using an EWMA (say 5s half-life) so we don’t flap.

Omar El-Sayed: ok. so metrics for observability at 15s, and a separate 1s snapshot for routing/breaker.

Lena Fischer: what’s in the snapshot? my vote:
- `admission_backlog_ms` (prefill + decode)
- `kv_cache_pressure` (0-1)
- `oom_risk` boolean-ish (based on fragmentation proxy)
- `tokens_per_sec_utilization` (0-1) but computed as ratio to moving baseline, not theoretical max

Sofia Ivanova: baseline ratio is better: compare current toks/sec to p50 of last N minutes under healthy state, per pool+model_family. indicates “we’re pegged”.

Julian Moore: but careful: during a real incident the baseline will drift downward and still show “fine”. maybe freeze baseline when breakers open.

Christine Wong: yeah we need “incident mode” freeze, otherwise it self-normalizes.

Rafael Mendes: +1 freeze baseline once shed_rate > X or breaker_open_rate > Y for >2m.

Omar El-Sayed: on cardinality: if snapshot is per pool, we can include `top_model_family` as a field? or keep it pure pool-level?

Elena Popov: keep snapshot pool-level. routing can already know which model it’s trying to send. if we need model-specific pressure later, add a small allowlist.

Logan Wright: if you *do* export model dimension in metrics, cap it. like only top K per pool + aggregate the rest into `model="other"`.

Sofia Ivanova: +1. same for quant profile.

Julian Moore: one more gotcha: queue depth units. “number of requests” isn’t comparable across models (one request could be 2k tokens vs 200). better to measure queued tokens or queued work units.

Lena Fischer: queued “estimated decode tokens remaining” is best but needs request metadata. can approximate with `queued_requests * avg_decode_tokens` per pool.

Omar El-Sayed: we already track per-request `max_tokens` and prompt len at admission. we can sum queued `planned_decode_tokens` cheaply in scheduler.

Sofia Ivanova: great. export `decode_queue_tokens` + `prefill_queue_tokens` rather than request counts.

Christine Wong: that’s also more explainable for customer comms (“service is overloaded, retry after X”) because it ties to backlog time.

Rafael Mendes: action items?

Omar El-Sayed: proposing:
1) Runtime heartbeat snapshot @1s: `backlog_ms` (computed from queued tokens + ewma service time), `kv_pressure`, `oom_risk`, `utilization_ratio` (baseline+freeze)
2) Prom metrics @15s: pool-level + optional model_family dimension with topK cap, no tenant/pod/gpu labels
3) document sampling + cardinality guardrails for policy engine consumers

Sofia Ivanova: sounds good. Julian can you sanity check backlog_ms estimator?

Julian Moore: yep. I’ll prototype with last week’s load test traces.

Logan Wright: pls add a lint check in metrics pipeline to reject new labels like `tenant_id` / `request_id` / `pod` on these metrics.

Christine Wong: I’ll sync with Benji/Jordan on policy schema so it expects pool-level signals, not per-model unless explicitly enabled.

Elena Popov: and I’ll update routing integration notes: heartbeat snapshot as the primary input, prom as secondary.

Rafael Mendes: :thumbsup: keep me posted; SRE will want to review before canary so we don’t page ourselves with cardinality explosions.

Omar El-Sayed: :thumbsup: will post a draft in the runtime signals notes doc later today

Omar El-Sayed: what about using “scheduler backlog time” instead? like estimate time-to-admit based on queue length * avg service time

Sofia Ivanova: that’s basically queue depth with a little modeling. I like it but we still need the avg service time estimator.

Elena Popov: from routing: any signal we use needs bounded cardinality. we can’t have per-model-per-tenant-per-gpu metrics.

Logan Wright: ^ please. if you export per-worker queue depth with labels `gpu_uuid` or `pod` you will melt prometheus + grafana.

Omar El-Sayed: current draft exports:
- `runtime_prefill_queue_depth{pool,model}` gauge
- `runtime_decode_queue_depth{pool,model}` gauge
- `runtime_tokens_per_sec{pool,model,phase}` rate
- `runtime_kv_cache_pressure{pool,model}` gauge
is `model` already too much?

Logan Wright: depends on how many models per pool and how many pools per region. if we also label by `tenant` (pls no) it explodes.

Christine Wong: we only need tier-aware decisions at gateway. per-tenant can be handled by the admission layer w/out per-tenant runtime metrics.

Rafael Mendes: jumping in (sre hat): for alerting we prefer pool-level aggregates. model dimension is ok if it’s small + stable. but don’t do request_path/tenant_id.

Sofia Ivanova: runtime side, model label is doable if we aggregate at “model family + quant profile” not every exact version. ex: `llama-3.1-70b-instruct` and not `llama-3.1-70b-instruct@2025-03-28-rc3`.

Julian Moore: we can also put exact model version in logs/traces, not metrics.

Omar El-Sayed: sampling rate: scheduler can compute queue depth every 100ms internally. do we export at 1s? 5s?

Logan Wright: scrape interval for most runtime pods is 15s today. we *can* do 5s in the hot regions but it increases cost. also high-freq gauges still churn.

Lena Fischer: for breaker decisions we want faster than 15s though, otherwise you’re already in tail latency hell.

Christine Wong: breaker is evaluated in gateway/routing at request time. can we feed it via a faster channel than prometheus scrape? (e.g. cached value in control plane)

Sofia Ivanova: we can publish a cheap “health snapshot” to the router every 1s over our existing runtime heartbeat. similar to autoscaler signals.

Elena Popov: +1. router already consumes regional capacity signals. adding `pool_pressure` there is clean. prometheus can still scrape for dashboards.

Rafael Mendes: that aligns with ADR-015 layering. request path should not depend on prom scrape freshness.

Julian Moore: if we do heartbeat snapshots, we should still compute using an EWMA (say 5s half-life) so we don’t flap.

Omar El-Sayed: ok. so metrics for observability at 15s, and a separate 1s snapshot for routing/breaker.

Lena Fischer: what’s in the snapshot? my vote:
- `admission_backlog_ms` (prefill + decode)
- `kv_cache_pressure` (0-1)
- `oom_risk` boolean-ish (based on fragmentation proxy)
- `tokens_per_sec_utilization` (0-1) but computed as ratio to moving baseline, not theoretical max

Sofia Ivanova: baseline ratio is better: compare current toks/sec to p50 of last N minutes under healthy state, per pool+model_family. indicates “we’re pegged”.

Julian Moore: but careful: during a real incident the baseline will drift downward and still show “fine”. maybe freeze baseline when breakers open.

Christine Wong: yeah we need “incident mode” freeze, otherwise it self-normalizes.

Rafael Mendes: +1 freeze baseline once shed_rate > X or breaker_open_rate > Y for >2m.

Omar El-Sayed: on cardinality: if snapshot is per pool, we can include `top_model_family` as a field? or keep it pure pool-level?

Elena Popov: keep snapshot pool-level. routing can already know which model it’s trying to send. if we need model-specific pressure later, add a small allowlist.

Logan Wright: if you *do* export model dimension in metrics, cap it. like only top K per pool + aggregate the rest into `model="other"`.

Sofia Ivanova: +1. same for quant profile.

Julian Moore: one more gotcha: queue depth units. “number of requests” isn’t comparable across models (one request could be 2k tokens vs 200). better to measure queued tokens or queued work units.

Lena Fischer: queued “estimated decode tokens remaining” is best but needs request metadata. can approximate with `queued_requests * avg_decode_tokens` per pool.

Omar El-Sayed: we already track per-request `max_tokens` and prompt len at admission. we can sum queued `planned_decode_tokens` cheaply in scheduler.

Sofia Ivanova: great. export `decode_queue_tokens` + `prefill_queue_tokens` rather than request counts.

Christine Wong: that’s also more explainable for customer comms (“service is overloaded, retry after X”) because it ties to backlog time.

Rafael Mendes: action items?

Omar El-Sayed: proposing:
1) Runtime heartbeat snapshot @1s: `backlog_ms` (computed from queued tokens + ewma service time), `kv_pressure`, `oom_risk`, `utilization_ratio` (baseline+freeze)
2) Prom metrics @15s: pool-level + optional model_family dimension with topK cap, no tenant/pod/gpu labels
3) document sampling + cardinality guardrails for policy engine consumers

Sofia Ivanova: sounds good. Julian can you sanity check backlog_ms estimator?

Julian Moore: yep. I’ll prototype with last week’s load test traces.

Logan Wright: pls add a lint check in metrics pipeline to reject new labels like `tenant_id` / `request_id` / `pod` on these metrics.

Christine Wong: I’ll sync with Benji/Jordan on policy schema so it expects pool-level signals, not per-model unless explicitly enabled.

Elena Popov: and I’ll update routing integration notes: heartbeat snapshot as the primary input, prom as secondary.

Rafael Mendes: :thumbsup: keep me posted; SRE will want to review before canary so we don’t page ourselves with cardinality explosions.

Omar El-Sayed: :thumbsup: will post a draft in the runtime signals notes doc later today
text:
Omar El-Sayed: quick q for folks thinking about overload: do we want the primary saturation signal to be queue depth (prefill/decode) or token/sec saturation? trying to nail what we export from runtime for the breaker policy engine

Sofia Ivanova: both, but careful w/ semantics. queue depth is “pressure” and token/sec is “throughput ceiling”. they correlate but not always.

Lena Fischer: +1. queue depth catches backlog early. token/sec saturation catches “we’re running hot even if queue looks fine” (eg long decode).

Julian Moore: if we pick only one, queue depth is easier + less noisy. but it can false-trigger if batcher is doing work (queue spikes during batch formation).

Christine Wong: from gateway/policy side: we need something stable + explainable. if it’s noisy we’ll open/close breakers and create thrash.

Omar El-Sayed: right. token/sec saturation idea was something like `observed_decode_toks_per_sec / expected_max_decode_toks_per_sec` per pool, clamp 0-1

Sofia Ivanova: expected_max is the hard part. depends on model, kv cache state, seq lens, quant profile, etc.

Lena Fischer: and “expected max” changes with scheduler knobs (continuous batching params) and GPU type. we’ll be constantly chasing it.

Julian Moore: also token/sec is a rate. you’ll need a window. if the window is too short it’s jitter city. too long and breaker reacts late.

source.messages:
Ben Carter: Uploaded profiling dump from this morning's A-load run. p95 up ~30% vs last week, p99 climbs when we hit microbatch=8. KV cache miss rate spikes in tail. https://artifacts/redwood/profiles/174250

Alice Nguyen: Can you paste the kernel-selection log? are we falling back to generic matmul at tail? :thinking:

Cheng Li: Quick summary: yes, we see kernel fallback after sequence len > 1024 + odd prompt shapes. batch=8 microbatching exposes head-of-line stalls. raw numbers:
```
- qps: 120 (stepped)
- gpu util: 78% avg, 95th burst 98%
- p95 latency: 180ms -> 235ms
- p99 latency: 420ms -> 710ms
- kv miss rate: 3% -> 12% at tail
```
Fallback log excerpt: `kernel_selector: no-opt-kernel -> generic_attention` repeated during bursts.

InfraBot: alert: gpu_util > 90% for group 'dedicated-prod' (3m window) at 1742502100 :rotating_light:

Ryan Patel: Two quick thoughts: 1) target_utilization should be lower for p99 sensitive routes. If our SLO is 200ms p95, cap util at ~70-75% for that route. 2) Admission control: throttle below headroom threshold, admit until predicted latency curve crosses SLO. We ran a simulation last month — I'll paste later.

Maria Gomez: +1 on admission control. Also consider graceful load-shedding with response hinting: return `cached_preview` when KV miss + queue depth high. Users prefer degraded quick reply to long tail. :thumbsup:

Cheng Li: Could also tune microbatch sizing per-request-class. For short-seq chat we keep microbatch=2; for long-seq batched jobs we allow microbatch=16. Proposed heuristic:
```
if seq_len < 256: mb=2
elif seq_len < 1024: mb=4
else: mb=8
```
But must guard with per-GPU headroom check: if util_predicted > 0.78 -> reduce mb by half.

Ben Carter: Good. Do we have profiling that ties kernel selection to seq_len distribution? If kernel selector picks fallback at >1024 we should add a special quantized kernel for that shape. Cheng, can you run a kernel sweep with quantized compiled kernel variant QK-v2?

Cheng Li: I'm on it. Running jobs with `--kernel-suite=qkv-v2 --microbatch-sweep 1,2,4,8,16`. ETA 2h. I'll post perf table.

DeployBot: Canary deploy scheduled: runtimes/kernel-qkv-v2 -> eng-runtime canary at 1742503000

Alice Nguyen: For the admission control prototype, let's add a simple PID-style controller in front of the queue that uses p95 rolling baseline. Example config snippet:
```
admission:
  target_p95_ms: 200
  headroom_util: 0.72
  action: shed/queue
```
Canary this on 10% traffic.

Ryan Patel: For load-shedding thresholds, suggest: shed when queue_depth > 20 AND predicted_gpu_util > 0.85 OR observed_p99 > 2x target. Keep telemetry so we can compute economic impact.

Maria Gomez: Also instrument KV cache TTL and shard hot keys. Right now hot key storms thrashed the LRU and amplified misses. Maybe add hot-key pinning for top-1000 keys.

Ben Carter: Next steps: Cheng -> kernel sweep + quantized perf table. Alice -> admission controller prototype + canary config. Ryan -> simulate headroom vs revenue tradeoffs. Maria -> KV pinning experiment. We'll reconvene when Cheng posts results. Thanks :raised_hands:

source.content:
Sofia Ivanova: seeing something nasty in scheduler under bursty mixed workloads. looks like a starvation edge case: long-context requests keep getting pushed back when short ones keep arriving, even though we try to age/boost.

Sofia Ivanova: question for LTS: do we treat this as backport-eligible for 1.18-lts? i think it’s at least Sev2 for Dedicated (tail latency + apparent “stuck” requests). want eyes on risk + test plan.

Samir Qureshi: +1, I have a repro from pilot env. not a deadlock, but effectively infinite wait if short queue never drains.

Samir Qureshi: symptoms: p95 ok-ish but p99/p999 blow up and you get a handful of requests sitting in queue for minutes.

Samir Qureshi: quick trace snippet (request IDs anonymized):
```
queue=gen.default
arrival_rate_short=~220 rps
arrival_rate_long=~6 rps
long_ctx_tokens=24k
scheduler_policy=wfq+aging

long reqs queued:
req_8f2... wait=187s
req_91a... wait=204s
req_c17... wait=226s

worker utilization ~92%
short batch size oscillating 24-32
long batch size stuck at 1 (never reaches dispatch)
```

Noah Patel: that sounds like our “aging” only applies within a bucket but not across the admission stage? (iirc we do a two-stage: admission -> queue -> dispatcher)

Sofia Ivanova: yup. the boost happens after admission. if admission keeps selecting short because of instantaneous cost heuristic, long never makes it into the runnable set.

Marcus Reed: do we have this in the scheduler regression suite yet? i.e. mixed prompt lengths + constant stream of shorts.

Sofia Ivanova: not exactly. closest is overload/backpressure test but it drains eventually. this one doesn’t if you keep shorts coming.

Connor O'Brien: for LTS backport rubric: need severity + customer impact + minimal diff + strong tests. is this hitting any customer incidents yet or just pilot / internal?

Sean Gallagher: SRE view: this is “silent badness”. it won’t page until someone notices stuck jobs / timeouts. for Dedicated, this is pretty painful if they have a mix of interactive + offline long jobs.

Rafael Mendes: agreed. also makes rollback messy because it looks like “capacity issue” at first glance.

Jonas Weber: perf note: fixing starvation might change throughput distribution. we should validate p50/p95 stay within gate, but expect p99 improves. risk is we might give long jobs too much priority and hurt interactive.

Samir Qureshi: i tried a local patch that adds admission aging (very small). with a conservative cap it stops the starvation without tanking short p95.

Samir Qureshi: numbers from my laptop sim (not perf-canary quality):
- before: short p95 180ms, long p99 > 120s
- after: short p95 195ms, long p99 4.2s

Noah Patel: sounds promising. want to keep it minimal: no new knobs if possible; reuse existing `scheduler.aging_max_boost` maybe.

Sofia Ivanova: proposed approach:
1) add “age” term into admission score for any request waiting > X ms
2) cap boost so it can’t fully override cost (prevents long jobs taking over)
3) add regression test that reproduces constant short arrivals + periodic long and asserts max wait < threshold.

Marcus Reed: test plan suggestion:
- deterministic sim test in unit/integration (no GPU needed)
- plus a release-branch smoke test variant under `scheduler_smoke_bursty_mix` so we catch it in LTS gates

Leila Farouk: i can help wire the sim test into CI so it runs on release branches too (we’ve had a couple scheduler tests only on main)

Connor O'Brien: question is: does this qualify for LTS backport under Sev2? i think yes if we can show customer impact + low-risk change.

Sean Gallagher: from SLO perspective: it’s effectively a tail-latency regression / starvation bug. for LTS we promised stability + avoid “surprises”. having requests hang for minutes is definitely not acceptable.

Rafael Mendes: +1 qualifies. would mark as Sev2 (maybe Sev1 if it causes timeouts/crashes, but this is more latency/availability at request level). still backportable.

Noah Patel: risk assessment:
- correctness risk: low if change is just scoring; but we must avoid priority inversion under sustained long traffic
- perf risk: moderate; needs perf-canary validation under mixed workload + interactive-only
- operational risk: low; no new config, no rollout complexity

Sofia Ivanova: i’ll put together a PR to main with:
- admission aging fix
- new regression test reproducer
- metrics: `scheduler_admission_starvation_events` counter when wait>threshold

Marcus Reed: for LTS gate: we can add a perf-canary scenario with fixed mix (e.g. 95% short @ 1k tokens, 5% long @ 24k) and assert:
- short p95 regression <= +3%
- long p99 improves (no hard threshold but must be sane)

Jonas Weber: careful with hard thresholds on long p99; noisy. maybe assert “no request waits > 30s” in the canary run logs.

Connor O'Brien: ok, if PR lands on main quickly, we can mark `backport-candidate` and target next 1.18.x-lts patch. need approvals from Sofia + Noah, and perf signoff (Marcus/Jonas) per policy.

Samir Qureshi: i’ll attach my repro traces and a minimal config for the test. also can validate in staging w/ synthetic load.

Sofia Ivanova: action items:
- Sofia: open PR + write risk section + propose backport notes
- Marcus/Leila: wire new test into release branch suite
- Samir: staging validation + share dashboards

Connor O'Brien: please include in PR body: “LTS backport justification” + expected behavior change. also call out any tuning parameter if we end up touching one.

Sean Gallagher: once there’s a PR, tag me. we’ll want a quick SRE review for rollout/monitoring: metrics + alert if starvation events > 0 on Dedicated.

Sofia Ivanova: will do. aiming to have PR up today.

Noah Patel: one more thought: make sure the admission aging uses monotonic time (we had clock skew weirdness in another path).

Sofia Ivanova: good call. will use existing monotonic timer used by dispatcher aging.

Connor O'Brien: cool. leaning “yes backport” contingent on: minimal diff, test coverage, perf gate pass. thanks all.

Samir Qureshi: :thumbsup: I’ll drop the repro config here once I sanitize:
```
load_profile:
  short_rps: 220
  short_tokens: 800
  long_rps: 6
  long_tokens: 24000
runtime:
  scheduler:
    policy: wfq
    admission_cost_heuristic: on
    aging: on
duration_s: 900
success_criteria:
  max_queue_wait_s: 30
```

Sofia Ivanova: perfect. closing loop once PR is up.

source.thread:
Omar El-Sayed: heads up: we're going to deprecate the old cache metric names in runtime. PR is up (18431) to align naming w/ the standards + ADR. Need to make sure dashboards/alerts don't silently break.

Omar El-Sayed: old -> new mapping in the PR description, but tl;dr we’re moving to `redwood.cache.*` prefixes and separating `prefix_cache` vs `kv_cache` consistently.

Nadia Rahman: +1. naming needs to match the observability standards doc or we’ll end up with 3 variants again :)

Allison Grant: can you paste the exact metric name changes here? I need to update the observability-pack dashboards + alert rules. Also any changes in label keys?

Omar El-Sayed: sure. the big ones:
- `redwood.runtime.kv_cache_hit_rate` -> (removed as a direct gauge) replaced by counters + derived recording rule
  - new: `redwood.cache.kv.requests_total{result="hit|miss|partial"}`
  - new: `redwood.cache.kv.tokens_total{result="hit|miss"}`
- `redwood.runtime.prefix_cache_reuse_tokens` -> new histogram `redwood.cache.prefix.reused_tokens_bucket` (+ _sum/_count)
- `redwood.runtime.cache_evictions_total` -> split:
  - `redwood.cache.kv.evictions_total{reason=...}`
  - `redwood.cache.prefix.evictions_total{reason=...}`
- wasted prefill: `redwood.runtime.wasted_prefill_tokens` -> `redwood.cache.wasted_prefill.tokens_total`
labels mostly the same, but `model` becomes `model_id` and `model_version` becomes `model_version_id` (joinability work).

Samir Qureshi: also: old `route` label becomes `route_id` (string allowlisted). tenant_id is *not* attached by default (we keep that in gateway analytics only).

Caleb Johnson: re: ingestion guards: if we start emitting both old+new during deprecation window, series count will spike. Please confirm plan (dual emit vs aliasing).

Omar El-Sayed: proposal: dual-emit for 1 release only, *at 10% sampling* for the old names, then remove old entirely next release.

Allison Grant: dual emit even at 10% is still a lot for histograms. can we instead do “compat” recording rules on the backend? Like create old metric names as recording rules from new counters/histos.

Logan Wright: +1 from telemetry. recording rules are way cheaper than doubling raw ingestion.

Nadia Rahman: +1. also avoids dashboards accidentally comparing apples/oranges due to sampling differences.

Omar El-Sayed: ok makes sense. runtime can ship ONLY new metrics. then we add recording rules to keep the old names alive for a grace period.

Sergio Costa: that’s what we did for the request latency rename last year. Works as long as old semantics match. For hit-rate, old was a gauge computed in-process, new is counters. close enough?

Nadia Rahman: semantics are slightly different: old “hit_rate” treated partial as hit (i think), new has explicit `partial`. we should be careful if we backfill old name.

Omar El-Sayed: good call. in ADR we landed on partial semantics: count partial separately, dashboards can choose hit_only or (hit+partial). Old dashboards probably assumed partial counted as hit.

Allison Grant: so compat recording rule for `redwood.runtime.kv_cache_hit_rate` should use (hit+partial)/total to match old.

Sergio Costa: ack. I’ll implement that in observability-pack along w/ dashboard updates.

Caleb Johnson: if we do recording rules, ingestion side won’t see old name at all, which is good. but we need to ensure alerts are migrated before we remove the recording rules.

Connor O'Brien: release planning question: what’s the deprecation timeline? I need it for runtime release notes + rollout checklist.

Omar El-Sayed: suggested:
- RUNTIME 1.28: ship new names + publish mapping + update dashboards/alerts to new names
- OBS: add recording rules providing old names for 2 weeks
- RUNTIME 1.29: no change
- After 2 weeks: remove recording rules + delete old dashboards panels.

Allison Grant: 2 weeks might be tight for customers with private stacks who fork dashboards. Can we keep recording rules for 30 days but mark as deprecated?

Logan Wright: +1, esp for Private deployments (they run their own prom rules). We should give them time to update.

Omar El-Sayed: ok 30 days. for Hosted we control it all, but no harm keeping the compat layer longer.

Marcus Reed: perf canary note: if runtime only emits new metrics, no perf regressions. dual emit would have been riskier. :thumbsup:

Sergio Costa: action items:
1) I’ll open PR in observability-pack to update dashboard panels + alerts to new metric names.
2) Add recording rules for old metric names w/ correct partial-hit semantics.
3) Mark old panels as deprecated (maybe add “(deprecated)” suffix) for a short window.

Allison Grant: also update runbook references. cache telemetry incident runbook currently screenshots `redwood.runtime.*` names.

Nadia Rahman: label keys changes (`model_id`, `model_version_id`, `route_id`) will break a lot of promql in panels. Please make sure the new dashboards use the standard label set + avoid raw `model_version_id` in high-level panels (cardinality).

Sergio Costa: yep. for overview panels, I’ll use `model_family` (if present) or top-N `model_id` only, and keep `model_version_id` behind a filter w/ `__name__` restrictions.

Caleb Johnson: careful: label allowlist drops any unknown values. dashboards should not rely on free-form `route_id` unless it’s from the allowlisted set. If someone tries to filter by an unapproved route, they’ll see gaps.

Omar El-Sayed: allowlist list is in 18426; but for runtime metrics, we only attach `route_id` for “named routes” (not ad-hoc). should be ok.

Allison Grant: can we add a small dashboard note: “Route breakdown only available for allowlisted named routes” so SRE doesn’t think it’s broken.

Sergio Costa: will do.

Redwood Deploy Bot: PR merged: redwood#18431 "reconcile prefix cache vs kv cache naming" -> main

Connor O'Brien: cool. I’ll make sure runtime release notes mention:
- new cache telemetry names
- old names available via recording rules for 30 days
- link to migration doc

Omar El-Sayed: thx. Nadia/Allison can you sanity check the mapping section in release notes when it’s up? want to avoid confusion.

Nadia Rahman: yep. also: please add “do not alert on old metrics after <date>” in the notes.

Allison Grant: and in alerting: we should run both alert rules (old + new) for a few days but route pages only from the new ones, otherwise we might double page.

Sergio Costa: agreed. I’ll keep old alerts disabled (or ticket-only) during overlap.

Caleb Johnson: one more: ingestion drop detection uses metric name pattern matching. If we removed `redwood.runtime.*` we should update the drop detector to include `redwood.cache.*` or we’ll miss pipeline issues.

Logan Wright: good catch. I’ll update the detector config in telemetry after Sergio’s PR lands, and will add a “metrics missing” panel that checks for both names during the transition.

Omar El-Sayed: ok sounds like we’re aligned:
- runtime: new names only (already merged)
- observability-pack: migrate panels + alerts, add compat recording rules for 30 days
- telemetry: update drop detection patterns
- release: note migration + timeline

Allison Grant: :thumbsup: please @ me on the observability-pack PR so I can review promql changes.

Sergio Costa: will do, opening it now.

source.body:
Noah Patel: We need to lock the backport policy risk rubric for the LTS line this week. Can we align here on: (1) what qualifies (Sev1/Sev2/security), (2) who has to approve, (3) what testing is mandatory before we merge into runtime-*-lts?

Mateo Alvarez: +1. Let’s keep it boring. Default should be “no backport” unless it meets clear criteria.

Connor O'Brien: Draft rubric proposal (trying to match what Release Eng can operationalize):
- Eligible classes:
  A) Security: CVE or Redwood Security advisory, or credible vuln w/ exploitability (even pre-CVE). Always eligible.
  B) Sev1: crash/data corruption/hard outage in production (Hosted/Dedicated/Private) OR “cannot serve” for a material customer segment.
  C) Sev2: customer-impacting correctness issue OR latency/throughput regression exceeding thresholds and confirmed not workload-specific.
- Non-eligible by default:
  - New features, behavior changes without strong customer need
  - Refactors / “cleanup”
  - Perf optimizations unless they fix a Sev2 regression (or are needed to mitigate a Sev1)

Connor O'Brien: Risk scoring for each candidate (to decide gates + approvals):
- Low: small, isolated fix; no kernel/scheduler hot paths; easy to test; no config changes.
- Med: touches hot path but localized; adds/updates tests; perf impact uncertain but measurable.
- High: kernel changes, scheduler/policy changes, autotune/defaults changes, memory management (KV cache), threading, or anything that can shift perf characteristics across workloads.

Hiro Tanaka: I like the “default no” framing.
On kernel side: any change to kernel selection / autotune / fused ops should be auto High risk. Even a tiny diff can shift perf or correctness (esp across H100 vs A10). If we backport, we need at least:
- microbench on the affected kernel path
- perf-canary full suite on A100 + H100 (or at minimum one datacenter SKU + one edge SKU)
- explicit note in release notes “kernel hot path changed”

Sofia Ivanova: +1 for scheduler/policy: anything touching queueing, admission, backpressure, load-shedding, or batching heuristics should be High. Also: if a fix changes fairness/latency tradeoffs, it needs explicit sign-off and a known-issue/mitigation note.

Sean Gallagher: From SRE perspective: for LTS we should treat perf regressions as reliability. Eligibility for Sev2 perf backports should be tied to a threshold.
Proposal: if p95 latency regresses >3% OR throughput drops >5% on canonical workloads vs the LTS baseline, that’s Sev2 eligible. But we need a waiver process because benches are noisy.

Allison Grant: On thresholds: I’m ok with that as a starting point, but call out “must reproduce on dedicated runners” + “2/3 runs” to reduce false positives. Also we should require the perf report artifact attached to the backport PR.

Sahana Iyer: Testing/gates: can we be explicit so engineers don’t guess?
For any LTS backport PR:
- unit + integration suite on release branch CI
- deterministic build check (same inputs => same artifact hash) (we have this partly done)
- smoke test deploy in staging env with pinned model set
For Med/High risk:
- scheduler regression suite (release branch)
- perf-canary gate summary attached (and reviewed)
For High risk:
- 2 GPUs minimum (A100 + H100) OR documented exception with rationale

Mateo Alvarez: Agree.
On approvals, I want clear owners so no one is surprised during patch week.
My take:
- Security: Security sponsor (Aisha/Hanae) + Release Eng + component owner (kernel/scheduler/runtime)
- Sev1: Runtime TL (Noah or delegate) + Release Eng + component owner + SRE (Sean/Rafael) for rollout risk
- Sev2: Component owner + Release Eng; SRE required if perf related; Runtime TL optional unless High risk

Noah Patel: That’s close. I’d like one more constraint: no backport unless the fix is already in main and has baked for at least 48h (unless security emergency). Keeps us from “inventing” on the LTS branch.

Connor O'Brien: +1. We can encode as a checklist item: “Fix merged to main + commit SHA referenced” and “>=48h bake” (exception only for security).

Hiro Tanaka: Also: “minimal diff” requirement. If the PR is bigger than it needs to be, we bounce it back to mainline cleanup.
Maybe a hard rule: if it changes more than X files or includes unrelated formatting, it’s not backportable.

Sofia Ivanova: Not sure we need a hard number (people will game it), but we can say: “cherry-pick cleanly preferred; conflict resolution must be surgical; any rewrite is High risk”.

Sean Gallagher: Mandatory rollback guidance for High risk backports. Even if it’s “rollback to previous patch” we should document steps + expected data plane behavior.

Allison Grant: +1 and for perf-related changes, require “before/after” numbers in the PR description (p50/p95 + throughput) even if it’s just canary output.

Sahana Iyer: How do we capture this in GitHub workflow? Labels + templates?

Connor O'Brien: Plan:
- Labels: `backport-candidate`, `lts-approved`, `lts-risk:low|med|high`, `lts-type:security|sev1|sev2`, `lts-perf-impact`.
- Backport PR template sections:
  - Eligibility (security/sev1/sev2) + incident/ticket link
  - Risk score + rationale
  - Test evidence (CI links)
  - Perf evidence (perf-canary link + summary)
  - Approvals checklist

Mateo Alvarez: Make sure “lts-approved” is only applied by Release Eng after approvals are actually collected.

Noah Patel: One more: Sev2 correctness (not perf) — what’s the bar? I’d say must be user-visible wrong output (not “we prefer different wording”). Think: tool calling schema breaks, structured output invalid JSON, tokenization mismatch, etc.

Hiro Tanaka: +1. Also: kernel numerical correctness bugs qualify even if no crash.

Sofia Ivanova: Scheduler correctness: starvation / deadlock / pathological queueing qualifies.

Connor O'Brien: Summarizing decision (tell me if anything is off):
1) Eligible for LTS backport:
- Security: CVE/advisory/credible vuln (exception to bake requirement allowed)
- Sev1: crash, data corruption, cannot serve, major incident
- Sev2: customer-impacting correctness; perf regression beyond threshold (p95 >3% or throughput >5% on canon suite, reproduced)
2) Not eligible by default: new features, refactors, optimizations not tied to Sev1/Sev2 regression
3) Preconditions: merged to main + references commit SHA; >=48h bake unless security emergency
4) Risk rubric:
- High = kernels/autotune/defaults, scheduler/admission/backpressure/batching heuristics, KV/memory/threading, config default changes
- Med/Low as discussed based on hot path + blast radius
5) Mandatory testing:
- Always: unit+integration on release branch CI, deterministic build check, staging smoke
- Med/High: scheduler regression suite + perf-canary gate artifact reviewed
- High: perf-canary on 2 GPU SKUs (A100+H100 preferred) or exception w/ documented waiver + SRE sign-off
6) Approvals:
- Security: Security sponsor + Release Eng + component owner
- Sev1: Runtime TL + Release Eng + component owner + SRE
- Sev2 correctness: component owner + Release Eng (Runtime TL if High risk)
- Sev2 perf: component owner + Release Eng + SRE

Mateo Alvarez: Looks good. Add: “waivers are rare” + waiver must be written in PR w/ explicit risk acceptance.

Sean Gallagher: :thumbsup: And waivers should be time-boxed (e.g., must rerun perf on next available runner).

Sahana Iyer: I’ll update the CI checklist doc and PR template to reflect this. Connor, can you own labels + who can apply what?

Connor O'Brien: Yep. I’ll take action items:
- [ ] create labels + permissions
- [ ] backport PR template
- [ ] short doc page that Support/CS can point to when customers ask “why didn’t you backport X?”

Noah Patel: Great. Once the doc exists, I’ll pin it in #eng-runtime and we’ll refer back when exceptions come up.

Hiro Tanaka: Please include a line: “Kernel changes in LTS are exceptional” so we don’t normalize it.

Connor O'Brien: Will do.
