Noah Patel: One more: Sev2 correctness (not perf) — what’s the bar? I’d say must be user-visible wrong output (not “we prefer different wording”). Think: tool calling schema breaks, structured output invalid JSON, tokenization mismatch, etc.

Hiro Tanaka: +1. Also: kernel numerical correctness bugs qualify even if no crash.

Sofia Ivanova: Scheduler correctness: starvation / deadlock / pathological queueing qualifies.

Connor O'Brien: Summarizing decision (tell me if anything is off):
1) Eligible for LTS backport:
- Security: CVE/advisory/credible vuln (exception to bake requirement allowed)
- Sev1: crash, data corruption, cannot serve, major incident
- Sev2: customer-impacting correctness; perf regression beyond threshold (p95 >3% or throughput >5% on canon suite, reproduced)
2) Not eligible by default: new features, refactors, optimizations not tied to Sev1/Sev2 regression
3) Preconditions: merged to main + references commit SHA; >=48h bake unless security emergency
4) Risk rubric:
- High = kernels/autotune/defaults, scheduler/admission/backpressure/batching heuristics, KV/memory/threading, config default changes
- Med/Low as discussed based on hot path + blast radius
5) Mandatory testing:
- Always: unit+integration on release branch CI, deterministic build check, staging smoke
- Med/High: scheduler regression suite + perf-canary gate artifact reviewed
- High: perf-canary on 2 GPU SKUs (A100+H100 preferred) or exception w/ documented waiver + SRE sign-off
6) Approvals:
- Security: Security sponsor + Release Eng + component owner
- Sev1: Runtime TL + Release Eng + component owner + SRE
- Sev2 correctness: component owner + Release Eng (Runtime TL if High risk)
- Sev2 perf: component owner + Release Eng + SRE

Mateo Alvarez: Looks good. Add: “waivers are rare” + waiver must be written in PR w/ explicit risk acceptance.

Sean Gallagher: :thumbsup: And waivers should be time-boxed (e.g., must rerun perf on next available runner).

Sahana Iyer: I’ll update the CI checklist doc and PR template to reflect this. Connor, can you own labels + who can apply what?

Connor O'Brien: Yep. I’ll take action items:
- [ ] create labels + permissions
- [ ] backport PR template
- [ ] short doc page that Support/CS can point to when customers ask “why didn’t you backport X?”

Noah Patel: Great. Once the doc exists, I’ll pin it in #eng-runtime and we’ll refer back when exceptions come up.

Hiro Tanaka: Please include a line: “Kernel changes in LTS are exceptional” so we don’t normalize it.

Connor O'Brien: Will do.

source.thread:
Noah Patel: Quick confirm on the runtime log/tracing annotations for route-level quant profiles before we lock the names. Want this consistent across hosted/dedicated/private.

Mateo Alvarez: yup. what do we have right now?

Noah Patel: Proposal in PR: add `quant_profile_id` + `quant_profile_resolution` on every request.
- `quant_profile_id`: string id when a profile was requested/resolved (or empty)
- `quant_profile_resolution`: enum-ish string: `applied` | `fallback` | `disabled`
Also include `quant_profile_ref`? (route config can point to alias)

Caleb Johnson: from tracing side: we can add these as span attributes on the server span. pls don’t add a bunch of extra fields unless we need them. we can reconstruct config from audit logs if we have route + resolved profile id.

Omar El-Sayed: +1 on minimal. biggest need for oncall is: “was quant actually applied for THIS request id?” and if not, why.

Hiro Tanaka: runtime can also expose `quant_profile_reason` when resolution != applied. compatibility checks already compute a reason code.
examples: `incompatible_gpu`, `max_seq_len_exceeded`, `artifact_load_failed`, `kill_switch_enabled`

Sofia Ivanova: careful: reasons can get too granular / high-cardinality if we dump raw error strings. but a small fixed set is good.

Jordan Lee: (policy side) compatibility reason codes already normalized in policy engine. can share the list. would prefer we reuse exactly those strings so dashboards can join.

Noah Patel: ok so fields:
1) `quant_profile_id` (resolved concrete versioned id)
2) `quant_profile_resolution` (applied/fallback/disabled)
3) `quant_profile_reason` (only when fallback/disabled)
And skip `quant_profile_ref`.

Mateo Alvarez: what about “disabled” vs “fallback”? if customer didn’t set anything, is that disabled or not present?

Noah Patel: thinking:
- if feature flag off / no route setting => `quant_profile_resolution=disabled` and omit id
- if route requested a profile but we can’t apply => `fallback` + id (requested/resolved?) + reason
- if applied => `applied` + id
Question is: for fallback do we log requested id or resolved id? route config is `ref` but policy resolves to concrete id.

Jordan Lee: policy resolves alias/ref -> concrete `profile_id` before evaluating compatibility. in fallback case we still know the concrete id we *tried* to apply. i’d log that as `quant_profile_id`.

Caleb Johnson: +1. from a trace/dash perspective: you want to group by the concrete version that caused fallbacks.

Omar El-Sayed: agree. otherwise we can’t see “profile v3 is incompatible on L4” etc.

Grace Kim: re: kill-switch. if global kill-switch flips, do we still emit `quant_profile_id`? might confuse.

Noah Patel: if kill-switch is enabled we shouldn’t even resolve/fetch artifacts ideally. so `quant_profile_resolution=disabled` and `quant_profile_reason=kill_switch_enabled`, and omit id.

Mateo Alvarez: works.

Hiro Tanaka: sampling question: we’re adding span attrs, but we don’t want to explode trace volume. what’s the rule?

Caleb Johnson: proposed sampling policy:
- baseline sampling stays whatever it is (0.1% on hosted, 0.01% on dedicated, varies)
- ALWAYS sample when `quant_profile_resolution=fallback`
- ALWAYS sample when `quant_profile_reason` is `artifact_load_failed` or any runtime exception in apply path
- optionally bump sampling for `applied` to 1% for canary routes only (gate by route tag)
We can implement “force sample” by setting trace_flags=1 in the runtime when those conditions happen.

Sofia Ivanova: can we do “force sample” without double-counting? also does it override upstream sampling?

Caleb Johnson: yes, we can set `x-trace-sampled: 1` on egress to collector / or flip the sampler decision in our local tracer before finishing span. depends on implementation; we’ve done it for 5xx already.

Noah Patel: force-sampling on fallback is super important. fallback rate is our early signal during rollout.

Mateo Alvarez: +1. also we should force-sample when `quant_profile_resolution=applied` BUT latency > threshold? maybe too much.

Caleb Johnson: i’d avoid auto-latency-based sampling for now. canary route bump is enough.

Jordan Lee: FYI policy engine already emits metric counters for `fallback_reason`. logs/traces should match those labels.

Grace Kim: can we confirm the exact key names? lower snake case?

Noah Patel: yep, keeping consistent with existing annotations:
- logs: JSON structured fields
- traces: span attributes with same names
Exact:
`quant_profile_id`
`quant_profile_resolution`
`quant_profile_reason`

Hiro Tanaka: ok. can we add `quant_profile_method` (int8/fp8/etc)? helps kernel debugging.

Noah Patel: that’s tempting but it’s derived from profile metadata. might be OK but risk on cardinality? method is low-cardinality.

Caleb Johnson: method is fine as a dimension. but if we add it, add it everywhere (log + trace) so ppl don’t have to pivot.

Mateo Alvarez: +1 on method, useful for perf regressions.

Sofia Ivanova: ok with method IF the allowed values are a tight set (e.g. `int8`, `fp8`, `int4`, `mixed`). no free-form.

Noah Patel: cool. adding:
`quant_profile_method`

Omar El-Sayed: pls also make sure kv-cache logs include the resolution state (applied vs fallback) so we can correlate memory spikes.

Noah Patel: will be included at request log level (same log event we use for per-request perf summary). kv-cache sub-logs can reference it via context.

Mateo Alvarez: can someone paste an example of what the log line looks like?

Noah Patel: example (structured):
```
{
  "msg": "request_complete",
  "route": "chat-prod",
  "model_id": "llama-3.1-70b-instruct",
  "quant_profile_resolution": "fallback",
  "quant_profile_reason": "incompatible_gpu",
  "quant_profile_id": "qpl_llama-3.1-70b_int8_kv-v3",
  "quant_profile_method": "int8",
  "latency_ms": 842,
  "tokens_out": 512
}
```
If applied, reason omitted.

Caleb Johnson: and in trace span attrs:
`quant_profile_resolution=fallback`
`quant_profile_reason=incompatible_gpu`
`quant_profile_id=qpl_llama-3.1-70b_int8_kv-v3`
`quant_profile_method=int8`

Jordan Lee: please ensure `incompatible_gpu` matches policy label exactly. we use: `gpu_not_supported` today, not `incompatible_gpu`.

Noah Patel: good catch. i’ll align w policy. list i have: `gpu_not_supported`, `seq_len_too_large`, `profile_deprecated`, `artifact_missing`, `kill_switch_enabled`.

Hiro Tanaka: what about kernel unsupported (method supported on GPU but kernel path missing)?

Noah Patel: that should map to `gpu_not_supported`? or a separate `kernel_not_supported`.

Jordan Lee: policy can only know hardware/model constraints. kernel-not-supported is runtime-specific. i’d add `kernel_not_supported` as a runtime reason code, but keep it in the fixed set.

Caleb Johnson: fine as long as it’s fixed + documented.

Mateo Alvarez: ok action items?

Noah Patel: i’ll update PR to:
- add `quant_profile_method`
- normalize reasons to match policy labels + add runtime-only `kernel_not_supported`
- force-sample traces on fallback + on `artifact_missing`/`artifact_load_failed` (naming TBD) + runtime exceptions

Caleb Johnson: i’ll confirm how to force-sample in the tracer in this service + update docs for span attributes.

Jordan Lee: i’ll paste the authoritative reason code list from policy engine in a comment on the PR.

Mateo Alvarez: :thumbsup: pls link PR here when ready.

Noah Patel: will do. should be in `pr-18555-add-runtime-log-annotation-quant_profile_id` (update coming shortly).

source.body:
Omar El-Sayed: seeing something weird in the new cache hit-rate counters on the perf canary. under continuous batching, `kv_cache_hit_total` looks too high (like > requests) and the miss-reason breakdown doesn’t add up. anyone else seeing it?

Samir Qureshi: yeah I saw that in staging last night. looked like hits got attributed to the “wrong” request when multiple requests share a single prefill batch.

Julian Moore: +1. the scheduler emits a single prefill op for N reqs (continuous batch), then we split decode steps per req. if instrumentation is attached to the prefill op, it’s gonna smear across reqs.

Omar El-Sayed: exactly. I dug a bit and the counter increment is currently happening at `PrefillDone` with whatever `active_request_ctx` happens to be “first” in the batch.

Omar El-Sayed: repro: send 2 prompts, A repeated (should be hit), B unique (miss). in a batched prefill, both get a hit.

Omar El-Sayed: logs from a local run (instrumentation debug build):
```text
I0303 18:12:41.102 runtime/cache_metrics.rs:211] prefill_complete batch_id=cb_7 reqs=[r_201,r_202] prefix_reuse_tokens=384
I0303 18:12:41.102 runtime/cache_metrics.rs:226] emit kv_cache_hit_total+1 route=/v1/chat/completions req_id=r_201 model=llama-3.1-70b
I0303 18:12:41.102 runtime/cache_metrics.rs:226] emit kv_cache_hit_total+1 route=/v1/chat/completions req_id=r_201 model=llama-3.1-70b
# ^ second increment should have been miss for r_202 (no reuse)
```

Samir Qureshi: :facepalm: yep. we’re using `batch_ctx.primary_req` there.

Noah Patel: what’s the intended semantic per ADR? hit/miss at request-level, not batch-level. if batch contains mixed reuse, we need partial hit semantics per request.

Omar El-Sayed: ADR says: per request, `hit` iff reused_prefill_tokens > 0 (and reuse attributed to that request). for partial hits, we track reuse histogram separately, but hit-rate counter should still reflect per-request.

Julian Moore: we have the per-request mapping already in scheduler: each req has `prefill_reused_tokens` and `prefill_total_tokens` after the batch finishes. we just don’t plumb it down to metrics.

Sofia Ivanova: instrumentation should happen when we finalize request accounting, not at batch prefill completion. otherwise any batch-level aggregation will break. can we move emission to `RequestAccounting::finalize(req)`?

Omar El-Sayed: that’s my current plan.

Omar El-Sayed: quick sketch (pseudo):
```rust
for req in batch.reqs {
  let reused = req.cache.reused_prefill_tokens;
  let prefill = req.cache.prefill_tokens;
  if reused > 0 { kv_cache_hit_total.inc(labels(req)); }
  else { kv_cache_miss_total.inc(labels(req, reason=req.cache.miss_reason)); }
  kv_cache_reuse_tokens.observe(reused, labels(req));
  kv_cache_prefill_tokens.observe(prefill, labels(req));
}
```

Samir Qureshi: +1. we need to be careful not to double count when a request gets rescheduled across batches (e.g. partial prefill). but for continuous batch, each req should have one finalized prefill accounting.

Noah Patel: also check streaming retry path doesn’t re-finalize the same request (we just fixed double counting there in another PR).

Omar El-Sayed: yeah, I’ll guard with `if req.metrics_emitted.swap(true) { return; }` like we did for stream retries.

Marcus Reed: can you post perf numbers once you move it? request-finalize runs on hot path too, but should be minimal.

Omar El-Sayed: yep. it’s just counters/histograms with sampling on. no extra allocations.

Julian Moore: can you include a negative test for mixed batch? something like: A repeated prompt twice + B unique, force them into same prefill batch.

Samir Qureshi: how do we force them into the same batch deterministically in tests?

Julian Moore: integration test can set `continuous_batching.max_delay_ms=50` and send the two requests within the window. we already have a test harness for “batch of 2”.

Omar El-Sayed: ok I’ll add an integration test variant.

Omar El-Sayed: opening PR now with the fix.

buildkite-bot: Omar El-Sayed opened PR https://github.com/redwood/redwood/pull/18433 "Fix cache hit attribution under continuous batching" (status: running)

Sofia Ivanova: please also update comment/docs in cache_metrics.rs. someone will otherwise “optimize” it back to batch-level emission.

Omar El-Sayed: will do.

Marcus Reed: once green, I’ll run it through perf-canary with continuous batching enabled. expecting hit-rate to drop (correctly) for mixed workloads.

Mateo Alvarez: thanks for catching. this is exactly the kind of bug that makes dashboards look great :)

Samir Qureshi: reviewer note: ensure miss_reason stays stable. if reused==0 but reason is “unknown”, we should still emit miss_total with `reason=unknown`.

Omar El-Sayed: yep. I’m preserving miss_reason from request ctx (`capacity`, `ttl_expired`, `policy_disabled`, `hash_mismatch`, `version_mismatch`, else `unknown`).

Noah Patel: also confirm the per-request labels (route/model/version/deployment_mode) come from the request, not batch. batch may have mixed routes in theory.

Omar El-Sayed: good call. labels pulled from req ctx.

buildkite-bot: PR https://github.com/redwood/redwood/pull/18433 build #1 passed (unit, lint)

Omar El-Sayed: pushed integration test `cache_metrics_mixed_batch_attribution` and it fails on main, passes on PR.

Omar El-Sayed: snippet from test assertion:
```text
expected: hits=1 misses=1
main:     hits=2 misses=0
pr:       hits=1 misses=1
```

Sofia Ivanova: :thumbsup: approve from scheduling side.

Marcus Reed: ran perf-canary (continuous batching on, sampling default). no measurable p95 regression (<0.2%). hit-rate graphs now match expectation (mixed workload no longer all-hit).

Noah Patel: approved. please add a short note to release notes / known limitations: pre-18433 dashboards likely overreported hits for batched prefills.

Omar El-Sayed: added note in PR description + will ping Allison/observability to annotate dashboard.

Mateo Alvarez: ship it. also let’s make sure the dashboard alert rules don’t start firing when this rolls out (hit-rate will “drop” but it’s a correction).

Omar El-Sayed: agreed. I’ll post in #eng-sre once merged so they can mute/adjust alerts during rollout.

Samir Qureshi: nice catch. thanks all.

Omar El-Sayed: yep. I’m preserving miss_reason from request ctx (`capacity`, `ttl_expired`, `policy_disabled`, `hash_mismatch`, `version_mismatch`, else `unknown`).

Noah Patel: also confirm the per-request labels (route/model/version/deployment_mode) come from the request, not batch. batch may have mixed routes in theory.

Omar El-Sayed: good call. labels pulled from req ctx.

buildkite-bot: PR https://github.com/redwood/redwood/pull/18433 build #1 passed (unit, lint)

Omar El-Sayed: pushed integration test `cache_metrics_mixed_batch_attribution` and it fails on main, passes on PR.

Omar El-Sayed: snippet from test assertion:
```text
expected: hits=1 misses=1
main:     hits=2 misses=0
pr:       hits=1 misses=1
```

Sofia Ivanova: :thumbsup: approve from scheduling side.

Marcus Reed: ran perf-canary (continuous batching on, sampling default). no measurable p95 regression (<0.2%). hit-rate graphs now match expectation (mixed workload no longer all-hit).

Noah Patel: approved. please add a short note to release notes / known limitations: pre-18433 dashboards likely overreported hits for batched prefills.

Omar El-Sayed: added note in PR description + will ping Allison/observability to annotate dashboard.

Mateo Alvarez: ship it. also let’s make sure the dashboard alert rules don’t start firing when this rolls out (hit-rate will “drop” but it’s a correction).

Omar El-Sayed: agreed. I’ll post in #eng-sre once merged so they can mute/adjust alerts during rollout.

Samir Qureshi: nice catch. thanks all.

source.messages:
Samir Qureshi: Quick gut check on the proposed `wasted_prefill` metric in the PR. What exactly are we calling “wasted” here?

Samir Qureshi: If it’s “prefill tokens that didn’t contribute to subsequent decode due to misses/evictions” that seems super decoding-strategy dependent (spec decode / chunked prefill / streaming cancels etc). Worried we’ll ship a metric nobody trusts.

github-bot: PR opened: https://github.com/redwood/redwood/pull/18424 (add-wasted-prefill-metric)

Omar El-Sayed: Yeah +1 to tightening the definition. The intent (at least from the runtime side) was: prefill work we did that never produced any user-visible output, i.e. request dies before we decode even 1 token.

Omar El-Sayed: Not “didn’t get reused by a later request” (that’s a different thing, and hard to attribute).

Noah Patel: Agree those are two different concepts.

Noah Patel: “wasted because cache churn” is really “recomputed prefill” (miss due to eviction / ttl / policy disabled) — that should be covered by miss_reason + missed_tokens, not by a new metric name.

Samir Qureshi: So wasted_prefill := request-level abort before decode starts?

Omar El-Sayed: More or less. Proposed definition I have in my notes:
- we emit `prefill_tokens_total` always
- we emit `wasted_prefill_tokens_total` only when `output_tokens == 0` AND termination_reason in {client_cancel, deadline_exceeded, internal_error}

Omar El-Sayed: That makes it comparable across decoding strategies because it’s not about acceptance/rejection, it’s simply “did we ever get to output.”

Sofia Ivanova: Important nuance with scheduler: we can finish prefill, enqueue decode, then request gets dropped (client disconnect) before first decode step. That’s still “no output”. Works w/ Omar’s definition.

Sofia Ivanova: But pls be explicit it’s *after prefill completes*. Otherwise partial-prefill aborts are messy (we may not know the exact token count depending on kernel path).

Julian Moore: +1. In continuous batching we can start prefill, then the batch gets reshaped, and if the request is cancelled mid-prefill we may only have partial counts.

Julian Moore: I’d rather define it in terms of already-existing counters: `prefill_tokens_total` (increment as we go) and then “wasted” computed at end if `decode_steps == 0`.

Marcus Reed: From perf side, I want something that doesn’t require extra per-token branching.

Marcus Reed: If we already have `prefill_tokens_total` and `completion_tokens_total`, then wasted can be:

Marcus Reed: ```
if completion_tokens == 0 && prefill_tokens > 0 && request_final_status != "success":
  wasted_prefill_tokens += prefill_tokens
```

Marcus Reed: That’s 1 extra counter increment in the end-of-request path.

Samir Qureshi: What about “success” responses that are 0 tokens (e.g., stop sequences / tool call immediately)?

Noah Patel: Those are not “wasted” IMO. If user asked for tool call and model returns tool invocation w/ zero generation tokens (or extremely short), prefill still “contributed” to the decision.

Noah Patel: Also some customers use `max_tokens=0` for embeddings-ish patterns (lol). We shouldn’t flag that as wasted.

Omar El-Sayed: Yep. That’s why I like filtering on non-success terminal statuses.

Omar El-Sayed: Another question: speculative decoding.

Omar El-Sayed: Spec decode can “decode” draft tokens that get rejected, but that’s decode-side compute; doesn’t change prefill semantics. We just care whether we produced *any* committed tokens / response payload.

Sofia Ivanova: For chat streaming: do we treat “we streamed 1 token then client disconnects” as not wasted? It’s not 0 output.

Omar El-Sayed: Correct, not wasted in that metric. It might still be “unproductive” from a cost POV, but that’s a different metric.

Julian Moore: +1 keep it narrow or it becomes a grab bag.

Samir Qureshi: Ok. Then the phrase “due to misses/evictions” in the sprint doc is misleading.

Noah Patel: I’d rewrite that. Misses/evictions should be covered by:
- `prefix_cache_miss_reason{reason=evicted|ttl_expired|policy_disabled|hash_mismatch|version_mismatch}`
- and something like `prefix_cache_missed_tokens_total` (or histogram)

Noah Patel: That tells you how much prefill you had to redo because cache didn’t help.

Mateo Alvarez: +1. If we ship “wasted prefill” and it’s actually “cache churn,” PMs + SRE will interpret it wrong.

Mateo Alvarez: Let’s keep wasted_prefill = “request aborted before producing output”. Then separately we talk about “recompute due to eviction” etc.

Mateo Alvarez: Also please document expected baseline. Streaming workloads will have non-trivial client_cancel; don’t page on it.

Marcus Reed: Another gotcha: retries. We had that double-counting bug on stream retries last week.

Marcus Reed: If a request gets retried internally and the customer sees one response, do we count wasted for the failed attempt?

Omar El-Sayed: Good call. For now:
- count wasted per *attempt* with `attempt_outcome!=success` AND `completion_tokens==0`
- BUT ensure the retry path sets attempt_outcome properly so we don’t double increment in the same attempt.

Omar El-Sayed: We already have the “attempt_id” in tracing; we are not putting that in metrics labels, but internally the request object should only flush metrics once.

Samir Qureshi: That’s fine. As long as metric isn’t a proxy for “customer saw errors”.

Omar El-Sayed: Exactly. It’s a compute-efficiency signal.

Sofia Ivanova: Please also decide whether we want this as counter vs histogram.

Sofia Ivanova: Counter is fine but histogram might help (are we wasting 50 tokens vs 5k tokens).

Omar El-Sayed: I was thinking histogram for tokens (bucketed) + a counter for events.

Marcus Reed: Histogram is okay if buckets are fixed + low cardinality. Don’t want per-route surprise.

Omar El-Sayed: Yup. Use the same token buckets we’re using elsewhere (prompt length buckets). No extra labels beyond model/region/deployment mode + allowed route tag.

Julian Moore: So summary definition text we can paste into the PR:

Julian Moore: “Wasted prefill tokens are prefill-stage tokens computed for requests that terminate without producing any completion tokens (completion_tokens==0) and with a non-success termination reason (client_cancel, timeout, internal_error). This metric is intended to capture prefill compute that produces no user-visible output. It is not a cache reuse metric; cache churn is captured via miss_reason and missed_tokens.”

Omar El-Sayed: Perfect. I’ll update the PR description + metric comment and sync with Nadia on naming.

Samir Qureshi: Works for me. I’ll add a unit test case: cancel after prefill => increments wasted; cancel after 1 streamed token => does not.

Mateo Alvarez: :thumbsup: ship it with that wording. Also add a one-liner in the internal docs so SRE doesn’t interpret spikes as “cache busted.”

Omar El-Sayed: Will do. Action items:
- [Omar] Update PR + metric docstring w/ definition + non-goals
- [Samir] Add tests for cancel/no-output vs cancel/after-output
- [Marcus] sanity check overhead w/ histogram enabled in canary

source.content:
Connor O'Brien: Need to lock feature-flag strategy for TP watchdog before we finalize 1.19 canary plan. PR-21860 has the flag plumbing, but we need: name(s), defaults by env, and how we flip for Dedicated-first.

Noah Patel: +1. Also want to be explicit about blast radius: watchdog triggers => safe abort + drain replica (per ADR-0127). That means an error surfaced to client + potential retry/fallback. We should not default this on everywhere day 1.

Rafael Mendes: From SRE side: default OFF in prod until we’ve seen it behave in canary w/ alerting. But ON in staging/perf-canary is fine. Key is making it easy to enable per cluster/account and roll back quickly.

Sofia Ivanova: What’s the intended scope? per-replica? per-model? I’d like to keep it per “runtime deployment config” first, then optional per-model override later.

Hiro Tanaka: Kernel team concern: false positives if timeout too aggressive on long-context prefill or if comms slows. If we flip it on with a single global timeout we’ll page ourselves all day.

Connor O'Brien: Proposed split:
1) `tp_watchdog_enabled` (bool)
2) `tp_watchdog_decode_iter_timeout_ms`
3) `tp_watchdog_collective_timeout_ms`
4) `tp_watchdog_action` = {"drain_replica", "panic"} (panic should never be used outside dev)
Thoughts?

Omar El-Sayed: For observability, can we also ensure we emit structured reason: decode_iter vs collective vs scheduler heartbeat gap. PR-21860 mentions it but we should align w/ ENG-4823 naming.

Aisha Bello: Please make the “enabled” flag separable from alerting thresholds. If we enable watchdog in canary, we should route alerts only when watchdog-trigger rate > X/min or correlated request timeouts. Otherwise it’s too noisy.

Tomáš Novák: + runbook dependency. If watchdog triggers we need a clear “what now” for oncall: verify drain succeeded, watch for repeated triggers, reduce concurrency cap if needed, etc. (I’m updating `runtime-tp-hang-mitigation` but need the exact error codes.)

Noah Patel: Re: names, I’d prefer something consistent w/ other runtime guardrails:
- `runtime.tp.watchdog.enabled`
- `runtime.tp.watchdog.collective_timeout_ms`
- `runtime.tp.watchdog.decode_timeout_ms`
- `runtime.tp.watchdog.mode` (mode=drain)
And we keep a single “mode” to avoid people picking panic accidentally.

Julian Moore: +1 on “mode=drain only” for now. If we ever want “soft fail + load shed” we can add later. Also, for Hosted, load-shed integration (PR-21875) should kick in when watchdog starts firing so we stop piling more requests.

Connor O'Brien: Ok. Defaults by environment proposal:
- dev: enabled=ON, timeouts low-ish (fast feedback)
- staging: enabled=ON, timeouts moderate
- perf-canary: enabled=ON, timeouts production-like
- prod (Dedicated + Hosted): enabled=OFF initially
Then rollout:
1) Dedicated canary clusters (1-2 accounts) => ON
2) Dedicated broader (10-20%) => ON
3) Hosted canary => ON
4) Gradual to 100%

Rafael Mendes: Works. Need the “prod default off” to be explicit in config repo so someone doesn’t accidentally inherit staging defaults. Also we need a single kill switch we can flip in <5 min.

Marissa Cole: From release side, we can gate “enable watchdog in Dedicated canary” behind the 1.19 go/no-go checklist. But we need to decide the first canary accounts by EOD so PM can prep comms.

Grace Kim: PR has flags behind the runtime config service already. Kill switch is basically flipping `runtime.tp.watchdog.enabled=false` at the cluster level. We also have `timeout_ms` values config’d per environment.

Hiro Tanaka: What are we thinking for actual timeout numbers? For 70B-ish workloads w/ long context, prefill can be chunky. For decode, iterations should be << 1s normally, but under load could be ~1-2s?

Noah Patel: Tentative (subject to perf-canary data):
- collective_timeout_ms: 2000ms (if we’re stuck that long it’s basically a stall)
- decode_timeout_ms: 5000ms (gives headroom for long tokens + intermittent contention)
But I’d rather start conservative to avoid false positives, then ratchet down.

Aisha Bello: If we start conservative, that’s fine, but please make sure we still get a “near-stall” metric (e.g. heartbeat gap > 1s but < timeout) so we can see the distribution before it trips.

Omar El-Sayed: Yep, ENG-4823 is adding heartbeat + in-flight collective counters. We can add a “watchdog time remaining” gauge or at least emit spans when we cross 50% of timeout.

Tomáš Novák: Can we standardize the surfaced error? Something like `RUNTIME_TP_WATCHDOG_TIMEOUT` with a subreason `collective`/`decode` and include rank id + collective seq. That’s what oncall needs.

Julian Moore: Also important: what’s the behavior when only one rank hits timeout? We should assume ranks diverged and drain the entire replica, not try to keep partial ranks alive.

Grace Kim: Current implementation drains the whole replica process group, not per-rank. It triggers a controlled abort path + marks replica unhealthy so scheduler stops routing. (No global GPU reset.)

Rafael Mendes: That’s good. We should ensure we don’t thrash: if watchdog trips repeatedly during rollout, autoscaler might churn. We need dedupe/rate limit on drain events + alert noise. (I saw PR-21921 mentioned, but not merged yet.)

Connor O'Brien: Action items:
- Noah: confirm flag names + consolidate “mode=drain-only” (no panic)
- Grace: update PR-21860 config examples w/ explicit prod defaults OFF
- Aisha/Rafael: define canary alert threshold for watchdog-trigger rate + route to eng-runtime (not page) initially
- Tomáš: runbook update with error code + mitigation steps
- Marissa: align canary enablement step w/ 1.19 release ticket (ENG-4829)

Noah Patel: Ack. I’ll comment on PR w/ final naming + propose initial conservative timeouts, and we can tweak after perf-canary runs.

Marissa Cole: Please also document the exact flip sequence (Dedicated canary -> Dedicated wider -> Hosted canary) in the release thread so nobody turns it on in Hosted first.

Rafael Mendes: +1. Dedicated first, always. Hosted can tolerate a bit more error budget, but Dedicated customers will notice drain/retry behavior more. We need to coordinate w/ PM.

Connor O'Brien: Got it. I’ll post a short checklist in ENG-4829 + link here once up.

source.messages:
Hiro Tanaka: heads up on the mixed FP8/INT8 path. we need stronger kernel selection guardrails before we let more profiles ship.

Hiro Tanaka: current issue: profile sets weights=int8 (per-channel) but activations fp8. on H100 this *usually* picks the right fused GEMM, but on L40S we’re sometimes falling into a “fallback” kernel that technically runs but blows up decode latency and occasionally OOMs at long ctx.

Noah Patel: +1. the selector is treating fp8 act as “supports flash-attn v3” but the int8 weight path doesn’t always have the same max seqlen coverage.

Grace Kim: can we just hard-cap context length for that combo? like `max_supported_seq_len` per kernel family and force policy fallback to baseline if request ctx > cap.

Omar El-Sayed: careful: cap needs to consider *prefill+decode* and KV cache growth. we saw: prefill at 12k ok, then decode pushes memory over on batch merges.

Sofia Ivanova: scheduler angle: continuous batching merges requests w/ different remaining ctx. if we only check request.seq_len at admission time, we can still exceed cap when merged (effective max across batch).

Hiro Tanaka: exactly. guardrail should be in runtime kernel picker at execution time (per batch), not just policy.

Jordan Lee: determinism requirement too: if we sometimes pick mixed path and sometimes fallback based on transient batch composition, customers will see nondeterministic performance. do we want “hard rule”: if any request in batch exceeds cap => entire batch uses baseline?

Noah Patel: that’s the simplest. and it’s deterministic *given the batch* but the batch itself depends on timing.

Sofia Ivanova: right, but we can make it deterministic at request-level by *pinning* execution mode at admission.

Sofia Ivanova: e.g. once request admitted, set `execution_quant_mode = MIXED_FP8_INT8` only if request.max_ctx <= cap. then batching only groups compatible modes together.

Omar El-Sayed: that would avoid the KV cache blowups too, because we won’t merge mixed-mode with baseline-mode.

Grace Kim: ok but what’s the cap? per GPU and per kernel implementation.

Hiro Tanaka: for now, propose conservative caps:
- H100: mixed FP8/INT8 supported up to 16k context (prefill), decode ok
- L40S: mixed FP8/INT8 supported up to 8k context; above that force baseline (fp16/bf16) or pure int8 path if available

Jonas Weber: from perf suite: L40S mixed path p95 decode regresses badly >8k anyway. at 12k it’s ~1.7x baseline decode time due to non-fused epilogue path. so cap at 8k seems fine.

Noah Patel: what about A100? we still have some dedicated clusters.

Hiro Tanaka: A100 doesn’t have fp8 tensor cores (at least not in our stack), so mixed fp8/int8 is “not supported” and should never be selected. today it’s blocked by `gpu_capability >= 90` but we should make that explicit.

Jordan Lee: can we encode this in profile metadata so policy engine blocks incompatible enables? (profile declares supported_gpu_skus + max_context)

Hiro Tanaka: yes, but i still want runtime check because profiles can be wrong / future kernels change.

Grace Kim: +1 defense in depth.

Mateo Alvarez: joining late. what’s the concrete change request?

Hiro Tanaka: 1) add guardrail table in runtime kernel selector for quant mode combos
2) expose `max_supported_seq_len` (and maybe `max_prefill_seq_len` vs `max_total_seq_len`) per combo+gpu_sku_class
3) admission-time pinning so batching doesn’t mix modes / exceed caps

Mateo Alvarez: do it. also add observability: count how often we fallback due to seq_len cap (separate from general incompatibility).

perf-canary-bot: perf-canary run #4821 flagged regression: scenario `mixed_fp8_int8_decode_longctx` L40S p95 +62% vs baseline. threshold 20%. link: https://perf-canary.redwood/run/4821

Jonas Weber: yep that’s the same. in that scenario, requests are 10k-12k ctx. if we cap at 8k it should stop selecting mixed.

Noah Patel: question: do we cap based on `prompt_tokens` only or `prompt_tokens + max_new_tokens`?

Omar El-Sayed: KV cache depends on total (prompt + generated). we should cap based on `effective_context = prompt + max_new_tokens`.

Sofia Ivanova: but `max_new_tokens` is user provided and often huge. for streaming, it’s a ceiling. if we use the ceiling, we’ll disable mixed for a ton of traffic.

Hiro Tanaka: maybe two caps:
- “safe cap” on prompt (prefill) for kernel availability
- “memory cap” on total for KV cache

Hiro Tanaka: for v1 guardrail, i’d do: if `prompt_tokens > cap_prefill` => baseline.

Omar El-Sayed: but our observed OOM was decode-phase KV. prompt was fine.

Noah Patel: we can compute an upper bound memory estimate from `prompt_tokens` + min(`max_new_tokens`, `server_decode_cap`). we already cap decode at server side for some tiers.

Mateo Alvarez: keep it simple but correct. if it’s too conservative, that’s acceptable for v1. correctness > savings.

Jordan Lee: policy side: should return a compatibility warning when user enables profile and has routes configured for >8k context.

Grace Kim: console can show “this profile supports up to 8k on L40S; requests above will run baseline”.

Sofia Ivanova: runtime admission pinning still needs to know the cap per deployment’s gpu class. in hosted, route can span multiple gpu types across regions.

Noah Patel: right. for hosted multi-region, policy resolves per region anyway. runtime only sees resolved region+pool. so admission pinning can use local pool gpu class.

Hiro Tanaka: implementation sketch:

Hiro Tanaka: 
```cpp
// pseudo
QuantMode resolve_quant_mode(Request r) {
  if (!feature_flags.quant_profiles_enabled) return BASELINE;
  auto mode = r.route_resolved_quant_mode; // from policy
  if (mode == MIXED_FP8_INT8) {
    auto caps = caps_table[gpu_class][mode];
    if (r.prompt_tokens > caps.max_prompt_tokens) return BASELINE;
    if (estimate_kv(r) > caps.max_kv_bytes) return BASELINE;
  }
  return mode;
}

// scheduler groups by resolved_quant_mode
```

Grace Kim: + add a specific reason enum for fallback
`fallback_reason=SEQ_LEN_CAP` / `fallback_reason=KV_ESTIMATE`.

Mateo Alvarez: pls also include `kernel_family_selected` tag for traces. we’re debugging these too often blind.

Jonas Weber: and perf-canary scenario should assert fallback rate ~100% for long ctx when mixed is enabled on L40S.

Jordan Lee: determinism note: if we use kv estimate based on *batch size* it becomes nondeterministic. kv estimate should be per-request only.

Omar El-Sayed: agreed. per-request kv estimate (worst case) is fine. don’t use current batch shape.

Sofia Ivanova: one more: prompt_tokens sometimes unknown until tokenization. we have it at request admission though.

Noah Patel: yep, after tokenize. for embeddings it’s different but mixed path only for generation.

Hiro Tanaka: action items:
- Hiro: PR to add caps table + fallback reasons + trace tags
- Sofia/Noah: admission pinning + batching separation by quant mode
- Jonas: update perf-canary scenarios/thresholds + add assertion on fallback reason

Mateo Alvarez: sounds good. ping me for review on selector changes.

Grace Kim: i’ll drop the proposed caps in the thread once i pull latest microbench.

Hiro Tanaka: :thumbsup: thanks all
